```mermaid
    xorC --> termC[Terminator CR LF]
    termC --> txC[UART TX Pin to ECU]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Sensor-Fusion Architecture for Autonomous Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gpsS[GPS Receiver 1 to 10 Hz] --> preS[Pre processing Time Sync]
    imuS[IMU 100 to 1000 Hz Accel plus Gyro] --> preS
    ldrS[LiDAR 10 to 20 Hz Point Cloud] --> preS
    camS[Camera 30 to 60 Hz] --> preS
    odoS[Wheel Odometry 50 to 200 Hz] --> preS

    preS --> ekfS[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    preS --> ukfS[Unscented Kalman Filter UKF Option]
    preS --> pfS[Particle Filter Option Non linear]

    ekfS --> poseS[Vehicle Pose x y z roll pitch yaw]
    ukfS --> poseS
    pfS --> poseS

    poseS --> planS[Path Planner A star RRT]
    poseS --> ctrlS[Steering Throttle Brake Controller]
    poseS --> v2xS[V2X Cooperative Awareness Message CAM]
    poseS --> logS[Cloud Telematics Logger]

    classDef sens fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fus fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class gpsS,imuS,ldrS,camS,odoS sens
    class preS,ekfS,ukfS,pfS fus
    class poseS,planS,ctrlS,v2xS,logS out
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Autonomous Vehicle Navigation Stack (GPS as Core)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    percC[Perception Layer] --> locC[Localization Layer]
    nmeaC[GPS NMEA 0183 Input] --> locC
    mapC[HD Map GDF Format] --> locC
    locC --> globC[Global Path Planning]
    globC --> locPL[Local Path Planning]
    percC --> locPL
    locPL --> behC[Behavioral Layer Traffic Rules]
    behC --> motC[Motion Control Longitudinal plus Lateral]
    motC --> actC[Vehicle Actuators Steering Throttle Brake]
    motC --> fbC[Feedback to Localization]
    actC --> vehC[Vehicle Dynamics Response]
    vehC --> percC

    classDef layer1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef layer2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef layer3 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class behC,motC,fbC layer3
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024, Module 1]**
> Define the term *Pseudo-range* as used in GPS positioning. Why is it called "pseudo" and not "true" range?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pseudo-range is the apparent distance between a GPS satellite and a user receiver, computed by multiplying the measured signal propagation time by the speed of light ($c = 299{,}792{,}458\ \text{m/s}$). It is called "pseudo" because the receiver's inexpensive quartz clock is *not* perfectly synchronised with the satellite's atomic clock; the measured range therefore contains an unknown **receiver clock bias** $b_u$ in addition to the true geometric range. A minimum of **4 satellites** is required to solve for the three position coordinates $(x, y, z)$ plus this clock bias — one of the canonical "Why 4?" facts KTU examiners test.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> List any three errors that degrade standalone GPS accuracy and state the typical magnitude of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Ionospheric delay** — caused by free electrons in the ionosphere refracting the L-band signal — magnitude **2–10 m** (worst near the geomagnetic equator at solar maximum).
2. **Tropospheric delay** — due to dry air and water-vapour refraction — magnitude **1–3 m** at sea level.
3. **Multipath error** — signal reflection from buildings, ground, or the vehicle body — magnitude **0.5–5 m** in urban canyons and tree-lined roads.
*(A fourth acceptable answer is Selective Availability, now 0 m since May 2000.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **GPS receiver** used in automotive navigation. Identify each block and state its function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **NMEA 0183** data standard? Decode the following sentence and state the meaning of every field, including how the checksum is computed:

```
$GPRMC,134520.00,A,1028.5612,N,07653.6512,E,015.5,084.4,230124,,,A*6B
```

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) GPS Receiver Architecture (7 marks):**

A standard automotive GPS receiver consists of the following cascaded functional blocks (refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block diagram):

| # | Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Antenna** | Right-hand-circularly-polarised patch antenna; receives ~ −130 dBm L1 signal | 0.5 |
| 2 | **LNA (Low Noise Amplifier)** | Boosts signal by 20–30 dB while keeping noise figure < 2 dB | 0.5 |
| 3 | **Bandpass Filter** | Rejects out-of-band interference; centre = 1575.42 MHz, BW ≈ 2 MHz | 0.5 |
| 4 | **Down-converter / Mixer + Local Oscillator** | Translates RF to IF (typically 4.092 MHz) for ADC | 0.5 |
| 5 | **IF Amplifier + ADC** | Amplifies IF and digitises with 2-bit or more quantisation | 0.5 |
| 6 | **Acquisition block** | Coarse search over 1023 C/A-code phases and ±10 kHz Doppler bins | 0.5 |
| 7 | **Tracking loops (DLL + PLL)** | Fine code-delay (Delay Lock Loop) and carrier-phase (Phase Lock Loop) tracking | 0.5 |
| 8 | **Navigation decoder** | Demodulates 50-bps navigation message (sub-frames 1–5) | 0.5 |
| 9 | **Ephemeris / Almanac extractor** | Reads satellite orbit parameters and clock corrections | 0.5 |
| 10 | **Pseudo-range & Doppler calculator** | Outputs range and range-rate per satellite | 0.5 |
| 11 | **Position-Velocity-Time (PVT) solver** | Iterative least-squares or Kalman filter on the 4 unknowns $(x, y, z, b_u)$ | 0.5 |
| 12 | **ECEF → Geodetic converter** | Computes latitude, longitude, altitude using WGS-84 | 0.5 |
| 13 | **NMEA 0183 formatter + UART** | Serialises the fix at 1 Hz, 4800 bps, 8N1 | 0.5 |
| 14 | **Output to vehicle ECU** | CAN-bus gateway or direct UART to infotainment ECU | 0.5 |

**(b) NMEA 0183 Decoding (7 marks):**

NMEA 0183 is a **single-talker, multi-listener** ASCII protocol defined by the National Marine Electronics Association (now NMEA 0183 v4.11). It uses RS-422 electrical levels, 4800 bps default (38.4 kbps for high-speed variants), 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit. Every sentence begins with `$` and ends with `CR LF`. The checksum is the **XOR** of all characters between `$` and `*`, written in 2-digit hex.

| Field | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `$GPRMC` | — | Talker GP (GPS) + Sentence RMC (Recommended Minimum Specific) |
| `134520.00` | UTC time | 13:45:20.000 |
| `A` | Status | A = Active (valid); V = Void |
| `1028.5612` | Latitude | 10° 28.5612′ N |
| `N` | N/S indicator | North |
| `07653.6512` | Longitude | 076° 53.6512′ E |
| `E` | E/W indicator | East |
| `015.5` | Speed over ground | 15.5 knots |
| `084.4` | Course over ground | 084.4° true |
| `230124` | Date | 23 Jan 2024 |
| `, ,` | Magnetic variation | Not available |
| `A` | Mode | A = Autonomous (no differential) |
| `*6B` | Checksum | 0x6B = 107 |

**Checksum verification (1 mark):**
XOR of all bytes from `G` (in GPRMC) through the last `A` (mode) yields 0x6B, matching the transmitted value. Sentence is therefore valid.

**Vehicle interpretation:** At 13:45:20 UTC on 23 Jan 2024, the vehicle is located at 10.476° N, 76.894° E (Kerala region), moving at 15.5 knots ≈ **28.7 km/h** on a track of 084.4° (almost due east). No differential corrections are active.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **pseudo-range observation equation** of GPS, clearly stating the assumptions, and show how **four satellites** are sufficient to solve for the user's three-dimensional position and clock bias. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Why is standalone GPS inadequate for **fully autonomous** (Level-5) vehicles? Discuss **RTK GPS, DGPS, IMU fusion, and HD-map matching** as enabling technologies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of pseudo-range equation (7 marks):**

Let $\vec{r}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ be the ECEF position of satellite *i* at the signal transmission instant, and $\vec{r}_u = (x_u, y_u, z_u)$ be the unknown user position at the reception instant.

The **true geometric range** is:
$$R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2}$$

The **measured pseudo-range** is the product of measured propagation delay $\Delta t_i$ and speed of light $c$, containing a receiver clock bias $b_u$ and satellite clock bias $b_s$, plus ionospheric $I_i$, tropospheric $T_i$, and unmodelled errors $\varepsilon_i$:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i = R_i + b_u - b_s + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Satellite positions $\vec{r}_i$ are known from the broadcast ephemeris (Keplerian propagation).
2. $b_s$, $I_i$, $T_i$ are corrected to within sub-metre accuracy by broadcast models.
3. The unknowns are $(x_u, y_u, z_u, b_u)$ — 4 scalar unknowns.

Squaring and expanding the first equation for 4 satellites yields 4 non-linear equations in 4 unknowns. Linearising around an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ produces the linear system (3 marks):

$$\Delta \rho_i = -\frac{x_i - x_0}{R_i^{(0)}}\Delta x - \frac{y_i - y_0}{R_i^{(0)}}\Delta y - \frac{z_i - z_0}{R_i^{(0)}}\Delta z + b_u$$

In matrix form, $\Delta \vec{\rho} = G \vec{x}$ where $G$ is the **4×4 geometry matrix**. Its inverse $(G^T G)^{-1} G^T$ gives the corrections iteratively. **Convergence** is reached in 1–3 iterations because pseudo-range is well-behaved. Hence 4 satellites are sufficient (1 mark for stating this).

**(b) Why standalone GPS is inadequate for Level-5 autonomy (7 marks):**

| Reason | Quantitative Justification | Mitigation Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Positioning accuracy** is only 2.5 m CEP — 50× worse than the 0.05 m lane-keeping requirement | SAE J3016 Level-5 demands cm-level localisation | **RTK GPS** (2 cm) or **PPP** (5 cm) |
| **Update rate** is 1–10 Hz, too slow for highway speeds (at 120 km/h, 100 ms latency = 3.3 m of error) | Mechanical response time ~50 ms | **IMU fusion** (100–1000 Hz) bridges the gap |
| **Signal blockage** in tunnels, urban canyons, parking garages | 100% outage possible | **Wheel odometry + LiDAR SLAM** for dead reckoning |
| **Multipath** corrupts pseudo-ranges in cities | Up to 50 m error | **Map-matching** with HD map (HERE, TomTom) rejects outliers |
| **Geometric weakness** (high PDOP) when satellites are clustered | HDOP > 6 invalidates fix | **Multi-constellation** (GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + BeiDou) |
| **Atmospheric and ionospheric storms** | 10–50 m errors during solar maxima | **Dual-frequency L1+L5** iono-free combination |

**RTK GPS (Real-Time Kinematic):** A fixed base station transmits carrier-phase corrections via radio/4G; the rover resolves the **integer ambiguity** $N_i$ using techniques like LAMBDA, achieving **1–2 cm** accuracy. Cost: needs a base within 10–20 km.

**DGPS (Differential GPS):** Base station transmits code-range corrections only, yielding **1–3 m** accuracy — a 5–10× improvement, sufficient for ADAS but not full autonomy.

**IMU Fusion (EKF / UKF):** A tactical-grade IMU (e.g., Honeywell HG1930) drifts ~1 km/h; with GPS aiding at 1 Hz, the EKF bounds drift to ~1 m even during a 30 s GPS outage. (Full derivation: see standard Brown & Hwang textbook.)

**HD Map Matching:** The on-board HD map contains lane-centre polylines accurate to 10 cm. The vehicle's pose estimate is snapped to the closest lane centre using a particle filter, instantly improving localisation accuracy by an order of magnitude in mapped areas.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse DGPS with RTK.** DGPS corrects *code pseudo-ranges* (metre level). RTK corrects *carrier phase* and resolves integer ambiguity (cm level). Mixing them up is an instant 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Do NOT say "GPS uses triangulation."** It is **trilateration** — distances only, no angles. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 3. **Always mention 4 satellites = 4 unknowns** when asked "why 4?". Many students wrongly say "4 because 3 for position and 1 spare."
> 4. **WGS-84 is the datum**, not the projection. Lat/Lon is *not* a projection — it is a geodetic coordinate on an ellipsoid. KTU deducts marks if you call it a "projection."
> 5. **Forgetting to convert knots → km/h** in NMEA speed fields. 1 knot = 1.852 km/h.
> 6. **Selective Availability was switched off in May 2000** — never cite it as a current error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **GPS = Trilateration**, not triangulation. Minimum **4 satellites** to solve $(x, y, z, b_u)$.
- [ ] Pseudo-range = geometric range + receiver clock bias (unknown).
- [ ] **Carrier phase** observation (with integer ambiguity resolution) gives cm-level accuracy.
- [ ] **WGS-84** is the reference ellipsoid: $a = 6{,}378{,}137.0$ m, $f = 1/298.257223563$.
- [ ] **PDOP < 2** is ideal, **< 5** acceptable, **> 10** poor. Use it as a satellite-acceptance mask.
- [ ] Major error sources: ionosphere (2–10 m), troposphere (1–3 m), multipath (0.5–5 m). SA is **off** since 2000.
- [ ] **NMEA 0183** uses ASCII, RS-422, 4800 bps default, 8N1, `$…*hh<CR><LF>` with XOR checksum.
- [ ] **GPGGA** = position fix; **GPRMC** = recommended minimum; **GPGSA** = DOP & active SVs; **GPGSV** = satellites in view; **GPVTG** = track & speed.
- [ ] **DGPS** ≈ 1–3 m accuracy (code correction). **RTK** ≈ 1–2 cm (carrier-phase + integer ambiguity).
- [ ] Autonomous vehicles **must fuse** GPS + IMU + LiDAR + HD-map (sensor fusion via EKF/UKF/particle filter).
- [ ] **Speed of light** $c = 299{,}792{,}458$ m/s is *exact* (definition).
- [ ] **L1 = 1575.42 MHz** (civilian C/A code); **L2 = 1227.60 MHz**; **L5 = 1176.45 MHz** (safety-of-life).
- [ ] **NMEA 2000** is CAN-bus based (250 kbps); **ADASIS v2/v3** is the predictive-horizon protocol for ADAS.
- [ ] **ETSI EN 302 637-2 CAM** messages contain GPS position + 95 % confidence ellipse for V2X.
- [ ] **WGS-84 ECEF** is the engineering coordinate frame; **ENU** is used for local tangent-plane control laws.
- [ ] **GDOP = √(PDOP² + TDOP²)** — geometry quality of the satellite constellation.
- [ ] **EGNOS / WAAS / GAGAN / MSAS** are **SBAS** augmentation systems delivering ~1 m accuracy.
- [ ] **Multi-constellation** (GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + BeiDou + IRNSS) reduces PDOP and improves availability.
- [ ] **GNSS-denied** environments (tunnels, urban canyons, hostile jamming) require dead-reckoning and SLAM fallback.
- [ ] **Hedgehog in the fog** analogy: GPS is the *outline*, IMU is the *inertial sense*, HD map is the *memory*, LiDAR/camera is the *eyes*.
- [ ] **Velocity** in GPS comes from the **Doppler shift** on the carrier — not from successive position fixes (which would be noisy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip for KTU PEMUT861 Module 1:**
> Whenever a question asks *"Discuss GPS-based navigation in autonomous vehicles"*, structure your answer in **4 layers**:
> 1. **Physical layer** — satellites, signals, frequencies, pseudo-range.
> 2. **Data layer** — NMEA 0183 / 2000, ADASIS, ETSI ITS-G5.
> 3. **Algorithmic layer** — PVT solver, EKF/UKF fusion, RAIM, map-matching.
> 4. **Application layer** — ACC, lane centring, e-Horizon, V2X, fleet management.
> Covering all four layers systematically guarantees full marks under the KTU 2024 Scheme OBE rubric aligned to CO1 ("Understand the principles of automotive navigation systems").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Architecture of Autonomous GPS Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Architecture of Autonomous GPS Vehicle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Autonomous GPS Vehicle** is a self-driving ground vehicle that integrates the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** with a layered cyber-physical control architecture to perceive its environment, localize itself globally, plan an optimal trajectory, and execute motion commands without continuous human intervention. According to the **SAE J3016 standard** adopted by the automotive industry, autonomy is classified into **six levels (Level 0 to Level 5)**, where Level 5 represents full automation under all driving conditions. For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, the **Basic Architecture** of an autonomous GPS vehicle is formally defined as the *systematic integration of four interdependent functional stacks*: 1. **Perception Layer** — Sensor suite and signal conditioning. 2. **Localization Layer** — GPS/INS fusion and map matching. 3. **Planning Layer** — Global route + local behavioral trajectory. 4. **Actuation Layer** — Throttle, brake, steering control via drive-by-wire. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 expects students to articulate the *block-level functional architecture* of an autonomous GPS-guided vehicle, identify the role of each subsystem, and explain data flow between perception, decision, and actuation modules. Marks are frequently awarded for correctly drawing/labeling the architecture diagram and naming the sensors. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **pilot flying a modern commercial aircraft on autopilot**. The pilot does not physically grip the yoke at every second; instead, the aircraft's flight management computer continuously receives: - Position from satellites (GPS) and inertial sensors (IMU), - Surrounding traffic from radar/TCAS, - Destination inputs from the pilot. The computer then *decides* heading, altitude, and thrust, and *commands* the control surfaces. An autonomous GPS vehicle is conceptually identical but operates on a 2-D road plane with stricter latency requirements (typically **< 100 ms** control loop period vs. ~1 s for aircraft). In a simpler analogy: think of an autonomous car as a **tourist in an unfamiliar city** who uses three things simultaneously: - A **smartphone map** (GPS + digital map), - **Eyes and ears** (cameras, radar, LiDAR), - A **local guide's instructions** (planning algorithm). The architecture simply describes *how these three assistants talk to each other inside the car's brain (ECU)*. ### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Constants | Metric | Standard Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | GPS positional accuracy (民用/SPS) | **~2.5 m CEP** | Horizontal Circular Error Probable | | GPS update rate | **1 Hz – 10 Hz** | Position fix per second | | Control loop period | **10 ms – 100 ms** | Real-time actuation deadline | | Latency budget (perception→actuation) | **< 100 ms** | Safety-of-life threshold | | IMU sampling rate | **100 Hz – 1000 Hz** | High-rate dead reckoning | | Vehicle CAN bus speed | **500 kbps (CAN 2.0B)** | Intra-vehicle communication | > [!NOTE] > **GPS Civilian Accuracy Standard:** The Standard Positioning Service (SPS) used in civilian autonomous vehicles provides **2.5 m horizontal accuracy at 95% probability** and **4–6 m vertical accuracy**, which is why GPS is almost always *fused* with IMU and odometry — raw GPS alone is too noisy for lane-level control. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four-Layer Architectural Decomposition The basic architecture of an autonomous GPS vehicle is conventionally decomposed into **four cooperating layers**. Each layer has well-defined inputs, outputs, and computational responsibilities. #### Layer 1 — Perception Stack (Sensor Layer) **Goal:** Convert raw physical phenomena into structured digital signals. Sensors deployed: - **GNSS Receiver** (GPS/GLONASS/Galileo/BeiDou) → absolute position in WGS-84 frame. - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** → 3-axis linear acceleration + 3-axis angular rate. - **LiDAR** → 3-D point cloud for obstacle detection. - **Camera (mono/stereo)** → lane markings, traffic signs, objects. - **Radar (24 GHz / 77 GHz)** → relative velocity of obstacles. - **Wheel Odometry** → distance travelled via wheel encoders. #### Layer 2 — Localization Stack (Where am I?) **Goal:** Estimate vehicle pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ with bounded uncertainty. The dominant algorithm family is the **Kalman Filter (KF)** and its nonlinear variants: **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** and **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)**. Modern stacks additionally use **Particle Filters** for global localization. #### Layer 3 — Planning Stack (Where should I go?) **Goal:** Generate a collision-free, kinematically feasible, time-optimal trajectory. Sub-modules: - **Global Planner** — Dijkstra / A* on a road-graph (HD map). - **Behavioral Layer** — finite state machine (lane change, intersection, overtake). - **Local Planner** — Model Predictive Control (MPC) or Dynamic Window Approach (DWA). #### Layer 4 — Actuation Stack (How do I move?) **Goal:** Translate planned trajectory into physical steering, throttle, brake commands. Common interfaces: - **Drive-by-wire** — electronic throttle, electronic stability control (ESC), electric power steering (EPS). - **Controller Area Network (CAN bus)** — SAE J1939 / ISO 11898 protocol. ### 2.2 Why a Layered Architecture? The separation into layers enforces the **separation of concerns** principle in embedded software engineering: - Each layer can be *developed, tested, and replaced* independently. - Failure in one layer (e.g., GPS outage) is *isolated* and compensated by redundancy in another (e.g., IMU dead-reckoning). - Real-time scheduling becomes tractable: perception runs at 10–30 Hz, control loop at 100 Hz, planning at 1–10 Hz. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** This layered model directly maps to **AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform** and **ROS 2 (Robot Operating System)** node graphs used in production autonomous vehicle stacks by industry leaders like Waymo, Tesla, and Cruise. ### 2.3 Mathematical Foundations of Localization The vehicle state vector in the 2-D ground plane is conventionally defined as: $$\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \psi_k \\ v_k \end{bmatrix}$$ where $(x_k, y_k)$ are coordinates in a local East-North frame, $\psi_k$ is the heading angle, and $v_k$ is the longitudinal velocity. #### Vehicle Kinematic Bicycle Model (used in planning & control) $$\dot{x} = v \cos(\psi + \beta)$$ $$\dot{y} = v \sin(\psi + \beta)$$ $$\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L} \cos(\beta) \tan(\delta)$$ $$\dot{v} = a$$ where: - $L$ = wheelbase length (typically **2.5 – 3.0 m**) - $\delta$ = steering angle of front wheel - $\beta$ = slip angle at the center of mass (small for low-speed operation) - $a$ = longitudinal acceleration #### Extended Kalman Filter Prediction Step $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\mid k-1}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1})$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\mid k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_k$$ #### EKF Update Step $$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^{\top} \left( \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_k \right)^{-1}$$ $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \left( \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}) \right)$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = \left( \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k \right) \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}$$ where: - $\mathbf{F}_k$ = Jacobian of process model $\mathbf{f}$ w.r.t. state - $\mathbf{H}_k$ = Jacobian of measurement model $\mathbf{h}$ - $\mathbf{Q}_k$ = process noise covariance - $\mathbf{R}_k$ = measurement noise covariance - $\mathbf{K}_k$ = Kalman gain ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | GPS Horizontal Accuracy | **2.5 m CEP (SPS)** | Civilian L1 signal | | State vector (2-D) | $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \psi, v]^{\top}$ | ENU local frame | | Kinematic bicycle | $\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\delta$ | Valid when $\beta \approx 0$ | | Steering curvature | $\kappa = \frac{\tan\delta}{L}$ | rad/m | | GPS NMEA update rate | **1 Hz** (default) / 10 Hz (survey-grade) | Hz | | CAN bus bandwidth | **500 kbps** (CAN 2.0B) | kbps | | Control loop period | **10–100 ms** | ms | | EKF process covariance | $\mathbf{Q}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | PSD matrix | | EKF measurement covariance | $\mathbf{R}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ | PSD matrix | | HD map precision | **< 10 cm** | Lane-level | | Wheel odometry error | **1–2%** of distance travelled | Drift-prone | | IMU bias stability | **< 10°/hr** (consumer MEMS) | deg/hr | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From GPS Pseudorange to Vehicle Position GPS receivers compute position by solving a system of nonlinear equations based on **pseudorange measurements** to at least four satellites. **Step 1 — Pseudorange definition** The measured pseudorange $\rho_i$ from the $i$-th satellite is the geometric range plus receiver clock bias: $$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ where: - $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ = satellite $i$ coordinates (known from ephemeris) - $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$ = receiver coordinates (unknown) - $c$ = speed of light = **$2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s** - $\delta t_r$ = receiver clock bias (unknown) **Step 2 — Linearisation around an initial guess** Let $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_0)$ be an initial guess. Taylor-expanding around this guess: $$\rho_i \approx r_{i,0} + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z}\Delta z + c\,\Delta(\delta t)$$ where $r_{i,0}$ is the geometric range computed at the guess, and the partial derivative w.r.t. $x$ is: $$\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x} = -\frac{x_i - x_0}{r_{i,0}}$$ **Step 3 — Forming the linear system** Writing the linearised equation for $N \geq 4$ satellites gives: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta\rho_i &= \frac{x_0 - x_i}{r_{i,0}}\Delta x + \frac{y_0 - y_i}{r_{i,0}}\Delta y + \frac{z_0 - z_i}{r_{i,0}}\Delta z + c\,\Delta(\delta t) \\ \mathbf{\Delta\rho} &= \mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{\Delta x} \end{aligned}$$ where $\mathbf{G}$ is the $N \times 4$ geometry matrix of line-of-sight unit vectors and a column of ones for the clock bias term. **Step 4 — Least-squares solution** The classical least-squares estimator for the unknown correction vector is: $$\mathbf{\Delta x} = (\mathbf{G}^{\top}\mathbf{G})^{-1}\mathbf{G}^{\top}\mathbf{\Delta\rho}$$ **Step 5 — Iterative refinement** The receiver updates its guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_0)$ by $\mathbf{\Delta x}$ and re-linearises. The iteration converges when $\vert \Delta x \vert < 10^{-4}$ m, typically within **5–10 iterations**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Kinematic Bicycle Model from First Principles **Step 1 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** For a vehicle with front-wheel steering angle $\delta$, the ICR lies on the line extending from the rear axle at distance $R$ (turning radius) from the centre of mass. By rigid-body kinematics: $$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}$$ **Step 2 — Angular velocity of the body** Since the rear axle is non-steering, the yaw rate is: $$\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\delta$$ This is the **kinematic bicycle model** used in nearly every autonomous vehicle control textbook. **Step 3 — Position update** Projecting velocity onto global axes: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v \cos\psi \\ \dot{y} &= v \sin\psi \\ \dot{\psi} &= \frac{v}{L}\tan\delta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Discrete-time integration (for simulation)** Using forward Euler with sample time $\Delta t$: $$\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \cos(\psi_k)\,\Delta t \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \sin(\psi_k)\,\Delta t \\ \psi_{k+1} &= \psi_k + \frac{v_k}{L}\tan(\delta_k)\,\Delta t \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this model matters for KTU:** The bicycle model is the *de facto* baseline used by examiners when framing a 14-mark question on vehicle dynamics for autonomous navigation. You are expected to derive $\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\delta$ from first principles. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Trade-offs Across Architecture Choices | Architecture Choice | Strength | Weakness | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | GPS-only localization | Simple, globally referenced | 2.5 m noisy, fails in tunnels | Highway demo, open-sky | | GPS + IMU (loosely coupled) | Bridges GPS outages briefly | Drift accumulates over minutes | Level 2 ADAS | | GPS + IMU + LiDAR (tightly coupled) | Lane-level accuracy | High compute, expensive sensors | Level 4 robotaxis | | Camera-only (Tesla vision) | Low cost, rich semantics | Photometric fragile | Highway autopilot | | Rule-based planning | Predictable, verifiable | Cannot handle rare events | Highway-only L3 | | Learning-based planning (end-to-end) | Human-like intuition | Black-box, hard to certify | Research prototypes | | Centralized compute (single SoC) | Low latency | Single point of failure | Low-cost L2 | | Distributed compute (domain controllers) | Fault-tolerant | Higher wiring cost | L3+ production | ### 3.4 Numerical Example — One EKF Update Cycle Suppose a vehicle has predicted state at time $k-1$: $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 10.0 \\ 5.0 \\ 0.785 \\ 12.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \text{ [m]} \\ y \text{ [m]} \\ \psi \text{ [rad]} \\ v \text{ [m/s]} \end{bmatrix}$$ Process Jacobian $\mathbf{F}_k$ (assuming $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, $\delta = 0.05$ rad, $L = 2.7$ m): $$\mathbf{F}_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t\sin\psi & \cos\psi\,\Delta t \\ 0 & 1 & v\Delta t\cos\psi & \sin\psi\,\Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \frac{\Delta t}{L}\tan\delta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Substituting values: $$\mathbf{F}_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.848 & 0.0707 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.848 & 0.0707 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0.00186 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ The GPS measurement at time $k$ arrives with covariance $\mathbf{R}_k = \text{diag}(2.5^2, 2.5^2)$ (horizontal only). The Kalman gain $\mathbf{K}_k$ is then computed from $\mathbf{F}_k, \mathbf{P}_{k-1}, \mathbf{H}_k, \mathbf{R}_k$, and the innovation $\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1})$ updates the state. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of Autonomous GPS Vehicle Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[SATELLITE CONSTELLATION
GPS GLONASS GALILEO BEIDOU] --> B[GNSS RECEIVER MODULE] B --> C[LOCALIZATION LAYER
EKF UKF PARTICLE FILTER] D[IMU 6 AXIS MEMS] --> C E[WHEEL ODOMETRY ENCODERS] --> C F[HD MAP DATABASE
LANE LEVEL 10 CM] --> C C --> G[PLANNING LAYER
GLOBAL A STAR LOCAL MPC] H[PERCEPTION LAYER
LIDAR CAMERA RADAR] --> G G --> I[CONTROL LAYER
PID OR LQR OR MPC] I --> J[ACTUATION LAYER
STEERING THROTTLE BRAKE] J --> K[VEHICLE DYNAMICS
BICYCLE MODEL] K --> H K --> C K --> L[CAN BUS SAE J1939
500 KBIT PER SECOND] L --> M[TELEMETRY AND V2X
DSRC OR C V2X] M --> N[CLOUD FLEET MANAGER] O[SAFETY SUPERVISOR
ISO 26262 ASIL D] --> I O --> J ``` ### 4.2 Sensor Fusion Data Flow (Localization Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[GPS RAW PSEUDORANGE] --> stepB[PRE PROCESSING] stepB --> stepC[EKF PREDICTION] stepD[IMU ANGULAR RATE] --> stepC stepE[WHEEL SPEED] --> stepC stepC --> stepF[STATE PREDICTION X HAT] stepF --> stepG[EKF UPDATE] stepH[GPS POSITION FIX] --> stepG stepG --> stepI[POSTERIOR ESTIMATE X POST] stepI --> stepJ[POSE FOR PLANNER] stepI --> stepK[ERROR COVARIANCE P] stepK --> stepC ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture Stack (Layered View) ```mermaid flowchart TB L4[LEVEL 4 APPLICATION
HMI FLEET MGMT OTA] L3[LEVEL 3 DECISION
BEHAVIOR PLANNING MOTION PLANNING] L2[LEVEL 2 LOCALIZATION
GPS IMU FUSION MAP MATCHING] L1[LEVEL 1 PERCEPTION
SENSOR PROCESSING OBJECT DETECTION] L0[LEVEL 0 HARDWARE
SENSORS ECUs ACTUATORS CAN BUS] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L1 --> L0 ``` ### 4.4 Control Loop Timing Diagram (Textual Schematic) The autonomous vehicle control loop is a **hierarchical real-time system** with decreasing period at lower levels: - **Global planner:** 1–10 Hz (re-plans every few hundred metres). - **Behavioral layer:** 10 Hz (state machine transitions). - **Local trajectory planner (MPC):** 10–50 Hz. - **Low-level controller (PID/LQR):** 100–1000 Hz. - **Sensor acquisition:** 100 Hz (IMU), 10–30 Hz (LiDAR/camera), 1–10 Hz (GPS). This **temporal hierarchy** is essential to guarantee that slow deliberative decisions do not starve safety-critical fast loops of compute time. The architecture must therefore be **multi-rate**, with explicit scheduling on a real-time operating system such as **AUTOSAR OS** or **QNX Neutrino**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Autonomous Vehicle" as per the SAE J3016 standard. List the four main layers in the basic architecture of an autonomous GPS-guided vehicle.* **Model Answer:** An autonomous vehicle is one that can perform sustained lateral and longitudinal control of driving tasks (Level 3 or higher) under defined Operational Design Domain (ODD) without human input, as classified by the SAE J3016 standard (Levels 0–5). The four main architectural layers are: 1. **Perception Layer** — sensor acquisition (GPS, IMU, LiDAR, camera, radar). 2. **Localization Layer** — sensor fusion, map matching, pose estimation. 3. **Planning Layer** — global routing, behavioural decision, local trajectory. 4. **Actuation/Control Layer** — drive-by-wire command execution. **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1.5 marks, Layer listing = 1.5 marks. --- #### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** *Why is GPS not used alone for autonomous vehicle localization? What sensor is fused with GPS, and why?* **Model Answer:** Raw GPS provides only **2.5 m horizontal accuracy (SPS)** at low update rate (1–10 Hz), with signal outage in tunnels, urban canyons, and under foliage. Lane-level control requires **< 0.2 m accuracy**. GPS is fused with an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** because IMU provides high-rate (100–1000 Hz) acceleration and angular rate measurements that **bridge GPS outages** and **smooth out GPS jitter** via Kalman filtering. The combination gives a robust, high-rate pose estimate. **Mark Distribution:** GPS limitation = 1 mark, IMU role = 1 mark, fusion rationale = 1 mark. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** — *Autonomous Vehicle Architecture & Dynamics* **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the basic architecture of an autonomous GPS-guided vehicle. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the kinematic bicycle model of a four-wheeled vehicle starting from the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) concept. State all assumptions. **Model Solution (a):** The architecture comprises **four cooperating layers**: | Layer | Function | Key Components | |---|---|---| | Perception | Acquire raw environment data | GPS, IMU, LiDAR, Camera, Radar | | Localization | Estimate pose $(x, y, \psi, v)$ | EKF, UKF, Particle Filter, HD Map | | Planning | Generate collision-free trajectory | A*, DWA, MPC, FSM behaviour | | Actuation | Execute commands | Drive-by-wire, CAN bus, ESC, EPS | **Data flow:** Perception → Localization → Planning → Control → Actuation → Vehicle dynamics → (feeds back to Perception and Localization). **Auxiliary systems:** Safety supervisor (ISO 26262 ASIL-D), V2X module, HMI, telemetry. **Mark split:** Diagram = 3 marks, Layer description = 4 marks. **Model Solution (b):** **Assumptions:** 1. Vehicle moves on a flat 2-D plane (no roll/pitch). 2. Front-wheel steering only (rear axle fixed). 3. Low-speed operation → slip angle $\beta \approx 0$. 4. Rigid body, no suspension dynamics. **Derivation:** Let $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta$ = front-wheel steering angle, $R$ = turning radius from rear axle to ICR. From the ICR geometry: $$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}$$ The yaw rate equals velocity divided by turning radius: $$\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{R}$$ Substituting $R = L/\tan\delta$: $$\boxed{\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\delta}$$ The position update in global ENU frame: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\psi, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\psi$$ **Mark split:** Assumptions = 1 mark, ICR geometry = 2 marks, Yaw-rate derivation = 3 marks, Position update = 1 mark. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** — *GPS-Based Localization* **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of GPS-based position estimation. Derive the pseudorange equation and state how at least four satellites are used to solve for $(x, y, z)$ and receiver clock bias. **(b) [7 marks]** What is an Extended Kalman Filter? Write the prediction and update equations for an EKF applied to GPS/IMU fusion in an autonomous vehicle. **Model Solution (a):** **Principle:** Each GPS satellite broadcasts a ranging signal stamped with its transmission time. The receiver multiplies the time-of-flight by $c$ to get a **pseudorange** — the geometric distance plus an unknown receiver clock bias. **Pseudorange equation:** $$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c\,\delta t_r$$ where subscript $i$ refers to the $i$-th satellite. The unknowns are $(x_r, y_r, z_r, \delta t_r)$ — four unknowns. **Solving the system:** With $N \geq 4$ satellites, the system is linearised around an initial guess, producing a linear system $\mathbf{\Delta\rho} = \mathbf{G\,\Delta x}$, which is solved via least squares: $$\mathbf{\Delta x} = (\mathbf{G}^{\top}\mathbf{G})^{-1}\mathbf{G}^{\top}\mathbf{\Delta\rho}$$ The solution is iterated until convergence to within **$10^{-4}$ m**. **Mark split:** Principle = 2 marks, Pseudorange derivation = 3 marks, Linearisation + LS solution = 2 marks. **Model Solution (b):** The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** is the nonlinear extension of the linear Kalman Filter that linearises the process and measurement models around the current state estimate via Jacobians. **Prediction step:** $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1})$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k\mathbf{P}_{k-1}\mathbf{F}_k^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_k$$ **Update step:** $$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{\top}\left(\mathbf{H}_k\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_k\right)^{-1}$$ $$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k(\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}))$$ $$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k\mathbf{H}_k)\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}$$ For GPS/IMU fusion, the **process model** propagates state via the kinematic bicycle equations using IMU acceleration/gyro inputs. The **measurement model** maps predicted pose to GPS pseudorange to each visible satellite. Jacobians $\mathbf{F}_k, \mathbf{H}_k$ are computed analytically each cycle. **Mark split:** EKF concept = 2 marks, Prediction eqns = 2 marks, Update eqns = 2 marks, GPS/IMU fusion interpretation = 1 mark. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing levels of autonomy** — students often claim Level 5 is "currently deployed on roads." This is false; Level 5 is *not* commercially deployed as of 2024. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this error. > 2. **Stating GPS accuracy as 1 m** — the SPS civilian standard is **2.5 m CEP**, not 1 m. Survey-grade RTK-GPS achieves cm-level, but that is differential GPS, not standalone. > 3. **Missing the "four-layer" framework** — KTU answers without an explicit layered decomposition lose 2–3 marks. Always structure answers into Perception → Localization → Planning → Actuation. > 4. **Bicycle model: forgetting the slip-angle assumption** — examiners check whether students explicitly state $\beta \approx 0$ for the simplified bicycle model. > 5. **In EKF questions, writing $\mathbf{H}_k$ without defining it** — always state "Jacobian of measurement model w.r.t. state" explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Definition:** Autonomous GPS vehicle = GPS-integrated self-driving car using layered cyber-physical architecture. - ✅ **SAE Levels:** 0 (none) → 5 (full). Level 5 is *not yet deployed*. - ✅ **Four Layers:** Perception → Localization → Planning → Actuation (in that order, with feedback loops). - ✅ **Sensors:** GPS, IMU, LiDAR, Camera, Radar, Wheel Odometry. - ✅ **GPS Civilian Accuracy:** **2.5 m CEP (SPS)** at **1 Hz**. - ✅ **Why GPS + IMU?** GPS is noisy and intermittent; IMU is high-rate and bridges outages via dead-reckoning. - ✅ **Kinematic Bicycle Model:** $\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\delta$, valid at low speed when slip angle is negligible. - ✅ **Pseudorange Equation:** $\rho_i = r_i + c\,\delta t_r$ — needs **at least 4 satellites** to solve for $(x, y, z, \delta t_r)$. - ✅ **EKF** = nonlinear Kalman Filter; uses Jacobians $\mathbf{F}_k, \mathbf{H}_k$ of process and measurement models. - ✅ **Process noise $\mathbf{Q}_k$** models IMU bias / unmodelled dynamics; **Measurement noise $\mathbf{R}_k$** models GPS uncertainty. - ✅ **Control loop period:** **10–100 ms** for safety-critical actuation; perception runs at 10–30 Hz. - ✅ **CAN bus:** **500 kbps**, SAE J1939 / ISO 11898 protocol. - ✅ **Safety standard:** **ISO 26262**, with ASIL-D for the highest-criticality autonomous functions. - ✅ **Common exam line:** "An autonomous vehicle architecture is *hierarchical, multi-rate, and feedback-rich*." - ✅ **Modern industry stack examples:** ROS 2, AUTOSAR Adaptive, Apollo (Baidu), Autoware.

topics.title: Distance and Direction Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distance and Direction Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In automotive navigation, **Distance and Direction Calculation** refers to the mathematical procedures used to determine the **shortest path (great-circle)** or **constant-bearing path (rhumb-line)** between two points on the Earth's surface, expressed in terms of arc length and azimuth (compass bearing). It forms the computational backbone of every in-vehicle navigation system (IVNS), GPS receiver, ADAS routing module, and fleet-management platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT861):** The module treats the Earth as a sphere of mean radius **R = 6371 km** for most distance calculations, with optional correction to the WGS-84 ellipsoid (a = 6378.137 km, b = 6356.752 km) for high-precision automotive positioning.

The two principal quantities are:

- **Distance (d)** — the spatial separation, measured along a chosen geodesic (great-circle) or loxodrome (rhumb-line), typically expressed in kilometres.
- **Direction (θ)** — the angle between the local meridian (true North) and the line joining the two points, measured clockwise from 0° to 360°.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Mapping**
> - **Azimuth / Bearing / Heading** — synonyms used in different contexts: azimuth (astronomy), bearing (marine/aviation), heading (vehicle). All are angles measured clockwise from true North.
> - **Forward Azimuth** — direction from Point A → Point B.
> - **Back Azimuth** — direction from Point B → Point A.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are standing on a giant transparent globe. Every point on the globe has two coordinates — its **latitude** (how far North or South of the equator) and its **longitude** (how far East or West of the prime meridian). When a car's GPS in Kochi says "turn left in 350 metres towards Bengaluru", the navigation computer has already performed two calculations:

1. **How far is Bengaluru?** — A "distance" problem.
2. **In what compass direction?** — A "direction" problem.

Because the Earth is a curved 3D object, you cannot simply use Pythagoras' theorem on latitude/longitude as if they were x-y coordinates on a flat sheet. The curvature introduces a systematic error that grows with distance (roughly 0.5 % per 100 km). To handle this, navigators use either the **Great-Circle route** (the shortest arc, like the rim of a giant disc slicing through the globe) or the **Rhumb-Line route** (a path that crosses every meridian at the same angle, like a corkscrew — easier to steer but longer).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory hook:** Great-Circle = "as the crow flies over the curve"; Rhumb-Line = "as the ship's compass steers a constant heading."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spherical Earth Model — Coordinate Setup

Let two points be defined on the WGS-84 sphere:

- **Point A** — latitude $\phi_1$, longitude $\lambda_1$
- **Point B** — latitude $\phi_2$, longitude $\lambda_2$

with all angles in **radians** (a key source of marks-loss if forgotten).

$$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 \quad ; \quad \Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$$

The Earth's mean radius adopted in KTU problems is:

$$R = 6371 \text{ km}$$

## 2.2 Three Foundational Formulas

### 2.2.1 Spherical Law of Cosines (Naïve form — fast but unstable for short distances)

$$\cos\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

$$d = R \cdot \arccos\left[\sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)\right]$$

> [!WARNING]
> Numerical instability arises when the argument of $\arccos$ rounds outside $[-1, 1]$ for very short distances. Use the haversine form to avoid this.

### 2.2.2 Haversine Formula (KTU-Recommended — stable for all distances)

The **haversine** of an angle $\alpha$ is defined as:

$$\text{hav}(\alpha) = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

The great-circle distance is then:

$$a = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$

$$c = 2 \arctan2\!\left(\sqrt{a},\; \sqrt{1-a}\right)$$

$$d = R \cdot c$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The function $\arctan2(y, x)$ (rather than $\arctan(y/x)$) is used so that the quadrant of the result is correctly preserved — vital when $\Delta\lambda$ is small.

### 2.2.3 Vincenty's Formulae (Ellipsoidal Earth — WGS-84)

For sub-metre accuracy (essential for autonomous vehicles), Vincenty's iterative formulae on the WGS-84 ellipsoid are preferred. They replace the single spherical arc with an iterative solution for the reduced latitude $u$ and the equatorial projection.

$$U_1 = \arctan\!\left[(1-f)\tan\phi_1\right] \quad ; \quad U_2 = \arctan\!\left[(1-f)\tan\phi_2\right]$$

where $f = 1/298.257223563$ is the flattening. Iterations continue on $\lambda$ until $\vert\Delta\lambda\vert < 10^{-12}$.

## 2.3 Direction — Forward & Back Azimuth

The **forward azimuth** $\theta_{AB}$ (bearing from A to B) on a sphere is given by:

$$\theta_{AB} = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sin(\Delta\lambda)\cos\phi_2,\; \cos\phi_1\sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)\right)$$

Normalised to $[0°, 360°)$:

$$\theta_{AB}^{\circ} = \left(\theta_{AB} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} + 360\right) \bmod 360$$

The **back azimuth** is then:

$$\theta_{BA} = \theta_{AB} + 180° \quad (\bmod \; 360°)$$

## 2.4 Rhumb Line (Loxodrome) — Constant-Bearing Path

A loxodrome crosses every meridian at the same compass angle. Its length and bearing are:

$$\Delta\psi = \ln\!\left[\frac{\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_2}{2}\right)}{\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_1}{2}\right)}\right]$$

$$q = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta\psi}, & \Delta\psi \neq 0 \\[6pt] \cos\phi_1, & \Delta\psi = 0 \;\;(\text{East–West along same parallel}) \end{cases}$$

$$\boxed{\;D_{\text{rhumb}} = R \cdot \sqrt{(\Delta\phi)^2 + q^2(\Delta\lambda)^2}\;}$$

$$\theta_{\text{rhumb}} = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta\lambda,\; \Delta\psi) \quad \text{mod}\; 360°$$

> [!NOTE]
> On a **Mercator projection**, every rhumb line becomes a perfectly straight line — this is exactly why Mercator charts were the historical choice for marine navigation before GPS.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Earth radius (mean) | $R = 6371$ | km |
| 2 | WGS-84 semi-major axis | $a = 6378.137$ | km |
| 3 | WGS-84 semi-minor axis | $b = 6356.752$ | km |
| 4 | WGS-84 flattening | $f = 1/298.257223563$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Haversine intermediate | $a = \sin^2(\Delta\phi/2) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2(\Delta\lambda/2)$ | $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | Angular distance | $c = 2\arctan2(\sqrt{a},\sqrt{1-a})$ | radians |
| 7 | Great-circle distance | $d = R \cdot c$ | km |
| 8 | Forward azimuth | $\theta = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left[\sin(\Delta\lambda)\cos\phi_2,\; \cos\phi_1\sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)\right]$ | radians |
| 9 | Back azimuth | $\theta_{BA} = (\theta_{AB} + 180°) \bmod 360°$ | degrees |
| 10 | Rhumb-line quantity $q$ | $q = \Delta\phi / \Delta\psi$ when $\Delta\psi \neq 0$, else $\cos\phi_1$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Rhumb-line distance | $D = R\sqrt{(\Delta\phi)^2 + q^2(\Delta\lambda)^2}$ | km |
| 12 | Rhumb-line bearing | $\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta\lambda,\Delta\psi) \bmod 360°$ | degrees |

## 2.6 Real-World Automotive Utility

- **In-vehicle GPS / Sat-Nav:** Computes distance and bearing to the next waypoint 10–50 times per second.
- **ADAS Turn-by-Turn Routing:** Constant recomputation as the vehicle moves (typically at 1 Hz).
- **Fleet Management & Telematics:** Bearing logs used to detect route deviation, fuel-wasting idling, and unauthorised detours.
- **V2X & Autonomous Driving:** Rhumb-line approximations allow stable, smooth steering commands; great-circle is used for inter-city ETA estimation.
- **Insurance Telematics:** Bearing-mismatch between GPS heading and steering-wheel angle is a fraud indicator.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Haversine Formula

### Step 1 — Start from the Spherical Law of Cosines

For a spherical triangle on Earth of radius $R$:

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Haversine Substitution

Define:

$$h = \text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{d}{2R}\right)$$

Apply the cosine double-angle identity $\cos\alpha = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha/2)$:

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = 1 - 2\,\text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Re-express the Right-Hand Side

Use the identity $\sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(\phi_1-\phi_2) - \cos(\phi_1+\phi_2)]$ and $\cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(\phi_1-\phi_2) + \cos(\phi_1+\phi_2)]$ to write:

$$1 - 2\,\text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

After algebraic rearrangement (collecting $1 - \sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2$ terms):

$$2\,\text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = (1 - \sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2) - \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Cosine Half-Angle Identities

Use $\;1 - \cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)\;$ and $\;1 + \cos\theta = 2\cos^2(\theta/2)\;$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) &= \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\phi_1-\phi_2}{2}\right) + \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\phi_1+\phi_2}{2}\right) \\
&\quad - \left[\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\phi_1+\phi_2}{2}\right) - \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\phi_1-\phi_2}{2}\right)\right]\cos(\Delta\lambda)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Identity $\;\cos^2\alpha - \sin^2\alpha = \cos(2\alpha)\;$

After careful expansion and collecting terms:

$$2\,\text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Central Angle

Let $\;a = \sin^2(\Delta\phi/2) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2(\Delta\lambda/2)\;$. Then:

$$\text{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \frac{a}{2}$$

$$\frac{d}{R} = 2\,\arcsin(\sqrt{a}) = 2\,\arctan2\!\left(\sqrt{a},\; \sqrt{1-a}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;d = 2R\,\arctan2\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)},\; \sqrt{1-\text{arg}}\right)\;}$$

The right-side square-root term uses $1 - a$ rather than $(1-a)$ inside the haversine; both forms are mathematically identical because $\text{hav}(\alpha) + \text{hav}(\pi - \alpha) = 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why prefer $\arctan2$ over $\arcsin$?** The arcsin branch is undefined at $\pi$, while atan2 is well-defined across $[0, 2\pi]$, eliminating the antimeridian crossing edge case — critical for vehicles driving across the International Date Line.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — London → Paris

This is the canonical KTU textbook example.

**Given:**
- A (London): $\phi_1 = 51.5074°$ N, $\lambda_1 = -0.1278°$ E
- B (Paris): $\phi_2 = 48.8566°$ N, $\lambda_2 = 2.3522°$ E
- $R = 6371$ km

### Step A — Convert to Radians

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= 51.5074° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.89932 \text{ rad} \\
\phi_2 &= 48.8566° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.85278 \text{ rad} \\
\lambda_1 &= -0.1278° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = -0.00223 \text{ rad} \\
\lambda_2 &= 2.3522° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.04105 \text{ rad} \\
\Delta\phi &= \phi_2 - \phi_1 = -0.04654 \text{ rad} \\
\Delta\lambda &= \lambda_2 - \lambda_1 = 0.04328 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B — Compute the Haversine Intermediate $a$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) &= \sin^2(-0.02327) = (-0.02327)^2 = 5.415 \times 10^{-4} \\
\cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2 &= \cos(0.89932) \cdot \cos(0.85278) = 0.62195 \times 0.65808 = 0.40928 \\
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) &= \sin^2(0.02164) = (0.02164)^2 = 4.683 \times 10^{-4} \\
a &= 5.415\times 10^{-4} + (0.40928)(4.683\times 10^{-4}) \\
&= 5.415\times 10^{-4} + 1.917\times 10^{-4} \\
a &= 7.332 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step C — Compute the Angular Distance $c$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{a} &= \sqrt{7.332 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02708 \\
\sqrt{1-a} &\approx 0.99963 \\
c &= 2\arctan2(0.02708,\; 0.99963) \\
&= 2 \times \arctan(0.02709) \\
&= 2 \times 0.02709 \text{ rad} \\
c &= 0.05417 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D — Compute the Great-Circle Distance

$$d = R \cdot c = 6371 \times 0.05417 = 345.13 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The published road distance London–Paris (via Channel Tunnel) is ~456 km, and the straight-line (as-the-crow-flies) distance is ~344 km. Our 345 km result matches within 0.3 % — a textbook verification. ✓

### Step E — Compute the Forward Azimuth

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= \sin(\Delta\lambda)\cos\phi_2 = \sin(0.04328) \times 0.65808 = 0.04327 \times 0.65808 = 0.02847 \\
x &= \cos\phi_1\sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda) \\
  &= (0.62195)(0.75703) - (0.78314)(0.65808)(0.99906) \\
  &= 0.47082 - 0.51482 \\
x &= -0.04400 \\
\theta_{AB} &= \operatorname{atan2}(0.02847,\; -0.04400)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $y > 0$ and $x < 0$, the bearing lies in the second quadrant:

$$\theta_{AB} = 180° - \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.02847}{0.04400}\right) = 180° - 32.91° = 147.09°$$

### Step F — Compute the Back Azimuth

$$\theta_{BA} = (147.09° + 180°) \bmod 360° = 327.09°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric interpretation:** From London, Paris lies roughly **East-South-East** but tilted strongly to the **South-East** because the great circle bulges northward over the British Isles, then sweeps down through the English Channel.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Rhumb Line London → Paris

Using the same coordinates:

### Step A — Compute $\Delta\psi$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_1}{2}\right) &= \tan(0.78540 + 0.44966) = \tan(1.23506) = 2.8347 \\
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_2}{2}\right) &= \tan(0.78540 + 0.42639) = \tan(1.21179) = 2.6679 \\
\Delta\psi &= \ln\!\left(\frac{2.6679}{2.8347}\right) = \ln(0.94122) = -0.06050 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B — Compute $q$

$$q = \frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta\psi} = \frac{-0.04654}{-0.06050} = 0.7693$$

### Step C — Compute Rhumb-Line Distance

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{rhumb}} &= R\sqrt{(\Delta\phi)^2 + q^2(\Delta\lambda)^2} \\
&= 6371 \times \sqrt{(-0.04654)^2 + (0.7693)^2 \times (0.04328)^2} \\
&= 6371 \times \sqrt{0.002166 + 0.5918 \times 0.001873} \\
&= 6371 \times \sqrt{0.002166 + 0.001109} \\
&= 6371 \times \sqrt{0.003275} \\
&= 6371 \times 0.05723 \\
D_{\text{rhumb}} &= 364.6 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D — Compute Rhumb-Line Bearing

$$\theta_{\text{rhumb}} = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta\lambda,\; \Delta\psi) = \operatorname{atan2}(0.04328,\; -0.06050)$$

Second quadrant → $\theta_{\text{rhumb}} = 180° - \arctan(0.04328/0.06050) = 180° - 35.59° = 144.41°$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Great-Circle (Haversine) | Rhumb-Line (Loxodrome) |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| London → Paris distance | **345.1 km** | **364.6 km** |
| Excess over great-circle | 0 km (reference) | +19.5 km (+5.6 %) |
| Initial bearing | 147.09° | 144.41° |
| Final bearing | 327.09° | 324.41° |
| Bearing along path | Constantly changes | **Constant** |
| Practical use | Air travel, inter-city ETA | Maritime, constant-heading steering |
| Map representation | Curve on Mercator | **Straight line on Mercator** |
| Computation cost | 1 trig evaluation | 1 logarithm + 1 trig |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Automotive Distance & Bearing Pipeline

This flowchart captures how an automotive ECU fuses raw GPS fixes with the distance/direction algorithm to produce actionable turn-by-turn data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GNSS ANTENNA RECEIVER"] --> B["RAW NMEA SENTENCE GGA RMC"]
    B --> C["WGS84 LATITUDE LONGITUDE FIX"]
    C --> D{"DESTINATION SET BY DRIVER"}
    D --> E["WAYPOINT COORDINATE B PHI LAMBDA"]
    C --> F["VEHICLE COORDINATE A PHI LAMBDA"]
    F --> G["DELTA PHI DELTA LAMBDA COMPUTATION"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["HAVERSINE INTERMEDIATE A"]
    H --> I["ATAN2 CENTRAL ANGLE C"]
    I --> J["DISTANCE D EQUALS R TIMES C"]
    G --> K["ATAN2 FORWARD AZIMUTH THETA"]
    K --> L["BACK AZIMUTH THETA PLUS 180"]
    J --> M["NAVIGATION DISPLAY ETA REMAINING"]
    L --> M
    M --> N{"TURN BY TURN DECISION"}
    N --> O["LEFT TURN COMMAND"]
    N --> P["RIGHT TURN COMMAND"]
    N --> Q["STRAIGHT CONTINUE"]
    O --> R["STEERING ACTUATOR INPUT"]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.3 Algorithm-Decision Tree — Great Circle vs Rhumb Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["DISTANCE QUERY INITIATED"] --> Q1{"DISTANCE LESS THAN 20 KM"}
    Q1 -->|YES| RHUMB1["USE RHUMB LINE FAST SUFFICIENT ACCURACY"]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{"DISTANCE LESS THAN 500 KM"}
    Q2 -->|YES| HAV["USE HAVERSINE GOOD BALANCE"]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{"SUB METRE ACCURACY NEEDED"}
    Q3 -->|YES| VINCENTY["USE VINCENTY ELLIPSOIDAL"]
    Q3 -->|NO| HAV2["USE HAVERSINE SUFFICIENT FOR ROUTING"]
    RHUMB1 --> OUT["OUTPUT DISTANCE BEARING"]
    HAV --> OUT
    VINCENTY --> OUT
    HAV2 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Visual Glossary of Geometric Terms

| Symbol | Meaning | Plain-English Explanation |
|--------|---------|---------------------------|
| $\phi$ | Latitude | Angle up from the equator, ranges from $-90°$ (S) to $+90°$ (N). |
| $\lambda$ | Longitude | Angle around the equator from Greenwich, ranges from $-180°$ to $+180°$. |
| $\Delta\phi$ | Latitude difference | North–South offset between two points. |
| $\Delta\lambda$ | Longitude difference | East–West offset between two points. |
| $d$ | Great-circle distance | Shortest arc length joining the two points. |
| $D$ | Rhumb-line distance | Length of the constant-bearing path. |
| $\theta$ | Azimuth / Bearing | Compass direction of travel, clockwise from North. |
| $c$ | Central angle | Angle at Earth's centre subtended by the arc (in radians). |
