topics.title: Laser Radar Principle and Working - Non-Contact Ground Velocity Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laser Radar Principle and Working - Non-Contact Ground Velocity Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Laser Radar (LIDAR — Light Detection And Ranging)** is an opto-electronic remote sensing technique that employs coherent laser radiation to determine the range, velocity, and angular position of a target by analysing the back-scattered energy. In the automotive context, a *non-contact ground velocity sensor* is a vehicle-mounted instrument that measures the **true longitudinal ground speed** of the host vehicle without any mechanical coupling to the wheels or drivetrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Module 2):** A Laser Doppler Velocimeter (LDV) is a non-contact speed sensor that infers ground velocity by detecting the **Doppler frequency shift** imparted to a laser beam upon scattering from the road surface. It is immune to wheel slip, wheel lock, and tyre-inflation variations — making it the ground-truth reference for ABS, ESP, ACC, and autonomous navigation stacks.

The governing physical effect is the **Doppler Effect for Electromagnetic Waves**, first described by Christian Doppler in 1842. When a coherent EM wave of frequency $f_0$ reflects from a surface moving with velocity component $v$ along the line-of-sight, the returned frequency $f_r$ is shifted by an amount proportional to $v$. Measuring $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$ therefore yields $v$ directly.

**Standard Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Automotive Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ |
| He-Ne laser wavelength (lab) | $\lambda$ | 632.8 nm |
| Diode laser wavelength (vehicle) | $\lambda$ | $\mathbf{780 \; nm \; / \; 1550 \; nm}$ (Eye-safe band) |
| Doppler frequency shift (at 100 km/h) | $f_d$ | ≈ 71.4 MHz (for 780 nm) |
| Measurement accuracy | — | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 1 \;\%$ |
| Update rate | — | 10 Hz to 10 kHz |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Train Whistle"**
> Imagine standing on a railway platform. A train blows its whistle as it approaches you — the pitch sounds **higher** than its true value. As it moves away, the pitch drops. Your brain automatically decodes the pitch shift into a speed estimate. A Laser Doppler Velocimeter does the *exact same thing*, but with **light** instead of sound, and at the speed of light the shifts become millions of cycles per second (MHz–GHz), which an electronic mixer can measure with extreme precision.

**Why "non-contact"?** Unlike a wheel-speed sensor (which counts toothed-wheel pulses at the hub), a laser sensor **does not touch the road**. It shines a beam downward through a window in the underbody, lets the asphalt scatter the photons back into a photodetector, and computes speed purely from the colour (frequency) change of the returned light. This is the same principle police LIDAR guns use to catch speeding motorists — but mounted on the vehicle itself to *measure its own* speed.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In modern drive-by-wire systems, the engine control unit (ECU) and brake-by-wire unit must know whether the vehicle is *actually* moving at 80 km/h or whether the driven wheels are merely *spinning* at 80 km/h on black ice. Wheel-speed sensors lie in both cases; only a non-contact ground-velocity sensor reports the truth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Doppler Effect in Electromagnetic Waves

The classical Doppler formula for sound is asymmetric (depends on the medium). For **light (EM waves)**, relativity demands a symmetric form. For the typical automotive case where vehicle speed $v \ll c$, the non-relativistic approximation is more than sufficient.

**The transmitted (local oscillator) beam** has frequency:

$$f_0 = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

The beam strikes the road at an angle $\theta$ measured from the road normal. The road acts as a diffuse Lambertian reflector — every microfacet of the asphalt scatters a small fraction of the incident energy back toward the sensor.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Beam emission and incidence.**
A diode laser (typically a single-mode 780 nm or 1550 nm source) emits a continuous-wave (CW) beam. The beam is collimated by a lens and directed at angle $\theta$ to the road.

**Step 2 — Two-way propagation with moving scatterer.**
As the source (vehicle) moves, the road surface effectively "sees" an incoming wave whose frequency is shifted according to the relative radial velocity. Because light undergoes a *double* Doppler shift — once on the way down (moving source → moving target) and once on the way back (moving target → moving receiver) — the net shift is doubled.

**Step 3 — Heterodyne (coherent) detection.**
The returned, frequency-shifted beam is mixed with a reference sample of the transmitted beam (the *local oscillator*) on a fast photodiode. The photodiode output contains a **beat note** at exactly $f_d$, which is the difference between transmitted and received frequencies.

**Step 4 — Signal processing.**
The beat frequency is extracted via FFT or zero-crossing counters. Substituting into the Doppler equation yields the ground speed. The sign of $f_d$ (positive or negative around $f_0$) tells the processor whether the vehicle is moving forward or reverse.

**Step 5 — Output interface.**
The ECU receives a digital or pulsed signal (e.g., 10,000 pulses per kilometre) equivalent to a wheel-speed sensor's output, but with a **speed** rather than **wheel rotation** basis.

### 2.3 Why "How" and "Why" — The Underlying Logic

- **Why CW rather than pulsed?** A Doppler shift is a *frequency-domain* phenomenon. A CW beam gives the cleanest possible narrow-linewidth beat note, maximising signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and hence measurement accuracy.
- **Why two-way shift doubling?** Both the source and the receiver are on the same moving platform; the moving reflector changes the wave's wavelength in its own rest frame, then re-emits it back into the lab frame, applying the Doppler effect a *second* time. This is the geometric origin of the factor of 2 in the formula.
- **Why coherent (heterodyne) detection?** The optical frequency $f_0$ is on the order of $10^{14}$ Hz — far too fast for any direct counter. Mixing the return beam with a local oscillator down-converts the signal to a much lower *radio frequency* beat note, which is trivially measurable.
- **Why an oblique angle $\theta$?** If the beam fired straight down ($\theta = 0$), the Doppler shift would be zero (no radial motion component). A forward-pointing geometry gives a usable, measurable shift, with $\cos\theta$ acting as a known scaling factor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the table below — it is the single most-tested cluster of equations for this topic in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_0 = c / \lambda$ | Optical carrier frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{\lambda}$ | Doppler frequency shift (CW laser radar) | Hz |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{f_d \, \lambda}{2 \cos\theta}$ | Ground velocity (rearranged) | m/s |
| 4 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta \, f_0}{c}$ | Doppler shift in terms of $f_0$ | Hz |
| 5 | $\text{PRF}_{\text{equiv}} = \dfrac{f_d}{\text{cycles per m}} = \dfrac{2 \cos\theta}{\lambda}$ | Pulses per metre (sensor output scaling) | pulses/m |
| 6 | $R_{\text{max}} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{P_{\min} \, (4\pi)^3}}$ | Monostatic radar/lidar range equation | m |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{coh}} = \dfrac{\eta P_{\text{LO}} P_s}{h f \, B}$ | Heterodyne SNR (shot-noise limited) | dB |
| 8 | $\Delta v_{\min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cos\theta \, T_{\text{int}}}$ | Velocity resolution (FFT bin width) | m/s |
| 9 | $v = f_d \cdot k$ | Output scaling constant (with $k = \lambda/(2\cos\theta)$) | m/s per Hz |
| 10 | $\text{Accuracy} (\%) = \dfrac{v_{\text{true}} - v_{\text{meas}}}{v_{\text{true}}} \times 100$ | Measurement error | % |

**Engineering Real-World Utility:**

- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS):** A wheel-speed sensor cannot detect low-μ (ice) conditions because all wheels decelerate together. An LDV detects the actual ground deceleration mismatch and pre-empts lock-up.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Stationary-ground-reference speed allows headway distance to be expressed in true time-to-collision, not just wheel revolutions.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Range Prediction:** Wheel slip on inclines misleads state-of-charge estimators; LDV eliminates the error.
- **Autonomous Driving (Level 3+):** Required for sensor-fusion odometry and SLAM as a wheel-independent truth source.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Laser Doppler Frequency-Shift Equation

We derive the central equation $f_d = 2v\cos\theta / \lambda$ in full, step by step, using the moving-source / moving-observer Doppler formulation for EM waves.

**Step 1 — Frequency seen by the ground (moving observer in EM frame).**
The vehicle carries the laser source at velocity $v$ along the road. The component of $v$ along the beam direction is $v_{\parallel} = v \cos\theta$. In the lab frame, the ground (observer) sees an incident wave whose frequency is shifted by the relativistic Doppler effect. For $v \ll c$:

$$f_{\text{ground}} = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

*Reasoning:* The negative sign arises because the source moves *towards* the ground patch when the vehicle moves forward, compressing the wavefronts (blue shift). Here $f_0 = c / \lambda$.

**Step 2 — Wavelength of the incident wave as seen by the ground patch.**
The local wavelength in the ground's reference frame is:

$$\lambda_{\text{ground}} = \frac{c}{f_{\text{ground}}}$$

But the asphalt scatters light as if the road were a stationary diffuse reflector; the scattered wave inherits the *frequency* $f_{\text{ground}}$ (a passive diffuse surface re-emits at the frequency of the wave incident upon it).

**Step 3 — Frequency of the return wave as seen by the moving vehicle.**
Now the vehicle is the observer. The ground patch is the source, emitting at $f_{\text{ground}}$ while the vehicle is moving *towards* it. Applying the Doppler effect *again* (now source stationary, observer approaching):

$$f_r = f_{\text{ground}} \left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

*Reasoning:* The positive sign reflects the observer moving towards the source, again blue-shifting the wave. For reflection geometry, the same angle $\theta$ applies.

**Step 4 — Substitute $f_{\text{ground}}$ from Step 1 into Step 3.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)\left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right) \\
&= f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v^2 \cos^2\theta}{c^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the non-relativistic limit $v \ll c$.**
Because $v^2/c^2 \ll 1$ (for a car at 300 km/h, $v/c \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$), the squared term is utterly negligible:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &\approx f_0
\end{aligned}
$$

This appears to suggest no shift at all — but this is the *first-order* expansion. To recover the leading-order Doppler shift, we must NOT use the product expansion. Instead, return to the *addition* form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= f_r - f_0 \\
&= f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)\left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right) - f_0 \\
&= f_0 \left[\left(1 - \frac{v^2 \cos^2\theta}{c^2}\right) - 1\right] \\
&= -\frac{f_0 \, v^2 \cos^2\theta}{c^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *second-order* (relativistic) effect, and it is genuinely negligible. Where, then, is the *first-order* Doppler shift that LIDARs actually use?

**Step 5 (corrected) — The correct first-order derivation via phase tracking.**
The first-order shift arises from the **change in path length** of successive wavefronts, not from the multiplicative product. Consider two consecutive wave crests emitted a period $T_0 = 1/f_0$ apart. Between emissions, the vehicle moves $v T_0$ along the road. The first crest travels to the ground patch and back, an extra round-trip of $2 v T_0 \cos\theta$ compared to a stationary system. This extra path equals $\lambda_f$ of extra phase, so the round-trip time is longer by $\lambda_f / c$, where $\lambda_f = c / f_r$ is the return wavelength. The period seen at the receiver is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_r &= T_0 + \frac{2 v T_0 \cos\theta}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= \frac{1}{T_r} = \frac{1}{T_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right)} \\
&= \frac{f_0}{1 + \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}} \\
&\approx f_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right) \quad \text{(binomial, } v \ll c\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — The Doppler shift is the difference $f_d = f_0 - f_r$ (sign convention).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= f_0 - f_r \\
&= f_0 - f_0\left(1 - \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right) \\
&= \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c} \\
&= \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{\lambda} \quad \text{(since } f_0 = c/\lambda\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master equation** for any CW laser Doppler ground-velocity sensor. The factor of 2 is the hallmark of a *reflection-mode* (radar/lidar) configuration, distinguishing it from a *one-way* (sonic) Doppler system.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Verification)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks students to compute $f_d$ given a vehicle speed, wavelength, and angle — practise this exact calculation.

**Problem.** A laser ground-velocity sensor uses a $\lambda = 780 \; \text{nm}$ diode laser mounted at $\theta = 35^\circ$ from the road normal. A vehicle travels at $v = 108 \; \text{km/h}$. Find the Doppler beat frequency and the equivalent number of output pulses per kilometre.

**Solution.**

Convert the velocity to SI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 108 \; \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = \frac{108 \times 1000}{3600} \; \text{m/s} = 30 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the master Doppler equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 30 \times \cos 35^\circ}{780 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 0.8192}{780 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{49.15}{7.80 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 6.30 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Hz} \\
&= 63.0 \; \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent output pulses per metre:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \frac{f_d}{v} = \frac{2 \cos\theta}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 0.8192}{780 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.10 \times 10^{6} \; \text{pulses/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Pulses per kilometre:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= N' \times 1000 = 2.10 \times 10^{9} \; \text{pulses/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently drop the factor of 2 in $f_d = 2v\cos\theta/\lambda$. Forgetting it gives *half* the correct frequency and hence a velocity error of 50%. Always state explicitly that the system is *reflection-mode* (two-way).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Non-Contact Velocity Sensors

| Sensor Type | Physical Principle | Accuracy | Update Rate | Cost | Slip-Sensitive? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Doppler (LDV)** | Optical Doppler on back-scattered light | $\pm 0.1$ % | 10 kHz | High | No |
| **Microwave Radar Doppler** | RF Doppler on returned microwave | $\pm 1$ % | 1 kHz | Medium | No |
| **Optical Correlation** | Spatial pattern matching of road texture | $\pm 0.5$ % | 100 Hz | Medium | No |
| **Ultrasonic Doppler** | Acoustic Doppler on reflected ultrasound | $\pm 3$ % | 50 Hz | Low | No |
| **Wheel-Speed Sensor (contact)** | Hall effect / variable reluctance on tone wheel | $\pm 5$ % (slip-affected) | 1 kHz | Very low | **Yes** |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define the term "Doppler shift" in the context of a laser ground-velocity sensor. State the governing equation relating Doppler shift to ground speed.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Doppler shift is the change in observed frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source and the observer. In a reflection-mode laser radar (LIDAR), the laser beam is transmitted to the road, and the back-scattered beam returns with a frequency shifted by $f_d$. The governing equation is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$

where $v$ is the ground speed, $\theta$ is the angle between the beam and the road normal, and $\lambda$ is the laser wavelength. The factor of 2 arises because the wave undergoes a Doppler shift on the way to the road *and* on the way back, doubling the effect.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** List any three advantages of a non-contact laser Doppler ground-velocity sensor over a conventional wheel-speed sensor used in ABS.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Slip independence:** The LDV measures the true speed of the road beneath the car, so it is not affected by wheel slip, wheel lock, or aquaplaning — conditions in which a wheel-speed sensor gives wrong readings. (1 mark)
2. **Independent of tyre condition:** Tyre pressure, tread depth, and wear do not affect the optical scattering characteristics sufficiently to change the Doppler beat. (1 mark)
3. **Direction sensing:** A single LDV can resolve the sign of $f_d$ (forward vs. reverse) natively, whereas wheel-speed sensors need additional logic or reverse-gear signals. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a laser radar (LIDAR) based non-contact ground-velocity sensor used in automobiles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A laser radar (LIDAR) ground-velocity sensor uses a coherent laser beam to measure the longitudinal speed of a vehicle relative to the road surface without any mechanical contact with the wheels or the road. The principle is based on the optical Doppler effect.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 above. The main blocks are: diode laser → collimating lens → beam splitter → photodiode mixer → transimpedance amplifier → band-pass filter → comparator → DSP/microcontroller.

**Working principle (3 marks):**
- A single-mode diode laser (typically 780 nm or 1550 nm) emits a continuous-wave beam of frequency $f_0$.
- The beam is collimated and projected at an angle $\theta$ to the road normal through a window in the vehicle underbody.
- The road surface scatters a small fraction of the incident energy back into the sensor.
- Because the vehicle is moving, the back-scattered beam has a Doppler-shifted frequency $f_r = f_0 - f_d$.
- The return beam is mixed with a reference sample of the transmitted beam (local oscillator) on a fast photodiode. The mixing product is a beat note at the Doppler frequency $f_d$.
- The microcontroller measures $f_d$ via FFT or zero-crossing counting and computes $v = f_d \lambda / (2 \cos\theta)$.
- The output is sent to the vehicle ECU via CAN bus as ground-truth speed, replacing or augmenting the wheel-speed signal.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The LDV provides a slip-independent, true ground reference speed that is critical for ABS, ESP, ACC, and autonomous-driving functions.

---

**(b)** A vehicle fitted with a laser Doppler ground-velocity sensor is travelling at $v = 90 \; \text{km/h}$. The laser wavelength is $1550 \; \text{nm}$ (eye-safe band) and the beam is mounted at $\theta = 40^\circ$ to the road normal. Calculate:
- (i) the Doppler beat frequency $f_d$, and
- (ii) the number of equivalent output pulses per metre. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit conversion.**
$v = 90 \; \text{km/h} = 90 \times 1000 / 3600 = 25 \; \text{m/s}$.
$\lambda = 1550 \; \text{nm} = 1550 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}$.
$\cos 40^\circ = 0.7660$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Doppler equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 25 \times 0.7660}{1550 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{38.30}{1.550 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 2.471 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Hz} \\
&\approx 24.71 \; \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pulses per metre.**
The pulse-train equivalent pulses per metre is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \frac{f_d}{v} = \frac{2 \cos\theta}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.7660}{1550 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= 9.884 \times 10^{5} \; \text{pulses/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.** At 25 m/s, in one second the car travels 25 m, generating $f_d \times 1 = 24.71 \times 10^6$ pulses. Hence pulses per metre $= 24.71 \times 10^6 / 25 = 9.884 \times 10^5$. ✓

**Final answer (1 mark for clearly boxed result):**
- (i) $f_d = 24.71 \; \text{MHz}$
- (ii) $N' = 9.884 \times 10^5 \; \text{pulses/m}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE – Dec 2023 Style Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare the working of a laser Doppler ground-velocity sensor with a microwave Doppler radar ground-velocity sensor. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Operating principle — similarity (1 mark):**
Both sensors are non-contact and exploit the reflection-mode Doppler effect. The general equation $f_d = 2v\cos\theta / \lambda$ applies, with $\lambda$ being the carrier wavelength.

**Key differences (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Laser Doppler (LIDAR) | Microwave Doppler Radar |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier | Light ($\lambda \approx 0.78 - 1.55 \; \mu m$) | Microwave ($\lambda \approx 3 - 30 \; \text{mm}$) |
| Beam divergence | Very low (mrad) — pencil beam | Wide (degrees) — floodlight |
| Speed of light in formula | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$ |
| Typical $f_d$ at 100 km/h | $\approx 70 \; \text{MHz}$ (large) | $\approx 2 - 20 \; \text{kHz}$ (small) |
| Optical access | Needs clean underbody window | Tolerates dirt, water, snow on bumper |
| Penetration of fog/rain | Poor | Good |
| Detector | Photodiode (fast, low noise) | Schottky mixer diode |
| Cost | Higher (diode laser + optics) | Lower (GaAs Gunn + waveguide) |
| Susceptibility to vibration | High (optical alignment) | Low |

**Merits of LIDAR (1 mark):** Higher accuracy ($\pm 0.1 \; \%$), better spatial resolution, sharper beam (insensitive to adjacent lane interference), faster update rates.

**Demerits of LIDAR (1 mark):** Sensitive to dirt / ice on the optical window, requires eye-safety certification (Class 1M laser), expensive.

---

**(b)** A vehicle is decelerating from $v_1 = 80 \; \text{km/h}$ to $v_2 = 20 \; \text{km/h}$ uniformly over $t = 5 \; \text{s}$. The laser Doppler sensor has $\lambda = 780 \; \text{nm}$ and $\theta = 30^\circ$. Calculate the average deceleration and the initial and final Doppler frequencies observed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert velocities to SI units.**
$v_1 = 80 \times 1000 / 3600 = 22.22 \; \text{m/s}$.
$v_2 = 20 \times 1000 / 3600 = 5.56 \; \text{m/s}$.

**Step 2 — Average deceleration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{v_2 - v_1}{t} = \frac{5.56 - 22.22}{5} = -3.333 \; \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitude: $|a| = 3.33 \; \text{m/s}^2$ (deceleration). (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Initial Doppler frequency at $v_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{d1} &= \frac{2 v_1 \cos 30^\circ}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 22.22 \times 0.8660}{780 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{38.49}{7.80 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.934 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Hz} \\
&\approx 49.34 \; \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Final Doppler frequency at $v_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{d2} &= \frac{2 v_2 \cos 30^\circ}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 5.56 \times 0.8660}{780 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{9.630}{7.80 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.234 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Hz} \\
&\approx 12.34 \; \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Rate of change of Doppler frequency (sanity check).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{df_d}{dt} &= \frac{2 \cos\theta}{\lambda} \cdot a = \frac{2 \times 0.8660}{780 \times 10^{-9}} \times (-3.333) \\
&\approx -7.40 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Hz/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark for connecting to FFT resolution / chirp analysis)

**Final results:** $|a| = 3.33 \; \text{m/s}^2$, $f_{d1} = 49.34 \; \text{MHz}$, $f_{d2} = 12.34 \; \text{MHz}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $f_d = 2v\cos\theta/\lambda$. This single omission gives 50% error — a 7-mark part (b) can drop to 4 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units**: using km/h directly in the formula (no, you must convert to m/s first). Wavelength must be in **metres**, not nm.
> 3. **Confusing $\theta$ definitions**: $\theta$ in our derivation is the angle from the road normal. Some textbooks define it from the road surface, giving $\sin\theta$ instead of $\cos\theta$. Always state the convention in your answer.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows or signal flow** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a labelled diagram, but only if it is *clearly functional* (arrows showing direction of optical/electrical signal).
> 5. **Confusing the Doppler shift with a range measurement.** LDV measures *velocity*, not distance. Pulsed LIDAR (time-of-flight) measures *range*; CW LDV measures *velocity*. The KTU expects you to know which is which.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Laser Radar Ground-Velocity Sensors**

- ✅ The governing equation is $f_d = 2v\cos\theta / \lambda$. Memorise the factor of **2** (reflection-mode).
- ✅ Rearranged for speed: $v = f_d \lambda / (2\cos\theta)$.
- ✅ **CW (continuous-wave)** laser is used, **not** pulsed, because Doppler shift is a frequency-domain phenomenon.
- ✅ Detection is **heterodyne (coherent)**: the photodiode mixes the return beam with a local-oscillator reference sample, producing an electrical beat note at $f_d$.
- ✅ The Doppler beat frequency is in the **MHz** range for typical vehicle speeds (e.g., 24.7 MHz at 90 km/h with 1550 nm laser).
- ✅ Output format: equivalent pulse train (pulses per metre), CAN bus speed message, and signed direction flag.
- ✅ **Key advantage** over wheel-speed sensors: immune to wheel slip, wheel lock, and tyre-condition variations — provides *true* ground-truth speed.
- ✅ **Limitations**: requires a clean optical window; sensitive to mud, ice, snow, and heavy rain; eye-safety (Class 1M) regulations apply.
- ✅ Wavelength choice: **1550 nm** is preferred for automotive because it is *eye-safe* and penetrates water droplets better than 780 nm.
- ✅ The angle $\theta$ is from the **road normal**; $\theta = 0$ gives zero Doppler shift (useless); typical mounting is 30°–45°.
- ✅ Velocity resolution scales as $\lambda / (2\cos\theta \, T_{\text{int}})$ — longer integration time gives finer resolution.
- ✅ Modern vehicles use LIDAR ground-velocity sensors in **ABS, ESC, ACC, and autonomous-driving** stacks as a slip-independent truth source.
- ✅ Compares to: microwave Doppler radar (cheaper, all-weather, less accurate), optical correlation (texture matching, medium cost), ultrasonic Doppler (low cost, low accuracy).
- ✅ The Doppler effect for EM waves is **relativistic** but reduces to the linear formula above in the limit $v \ll c$ (always true for cars).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
