Shape analysis (Hough Circles, contour moments) verifies that the blob is a circular light. 5. **Deep Learning Classification:** A CNN (e.g., YOLOv8) classifies each candidate into one of the states: **Red, Yellow, Green, Off, Red-Arrow, Green-Arrow**. 6. **Temporal Filtering:** A Kalman Filter or HMM smooths predictions over time using the traffic light finite state machine (Green $\to$ Yellow $\to$ Red). This eliminates single-frame flicker errors. 7. **3D Localization:** Using the pinhole camera model, the 3D position $(X, Y, Z)$ of the signal is computed. 8. **Map & V2I Validation:** The detected position is cross-checked with the HD map (which lists the GPS location of every signal pole) and optionally with V2I SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing) data broadcast by the traffic infrastructure. 9. **Output to Planner:** A reliable semantic output (e.g., "Red light at $(x, y)$, 45 m ahead, confidence 0.97") is sent to the planning module which decides whether to brake, hold, or proceed. #### Model Solution for (b): **Derivation:** The pinhole camera projects a 3D world point $P_w = (X, Y, Z)$ to an image point $(u, v)$ via: $$ s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} $$ The expansion of the third row gives $s = Z$, and for a point at the principal axis ($X = 0$), the $v$ coordinate is: $$ v = \frac{f_y \cdot Y}{Z} + c_y $$ For a vertical object of real height $H_{\text{real}}$ at distance $Z$, the top and bottom project to: $$ v_{\text{top}} = \frac{f_y \cdot (Y + H_{\text{real}})}{Z} + c_y, \quad v_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{f_y \cdot Y}{Z} + c_y $$ The pixel height $h_{\text{pixel}} = v_{\text{bottom}} - v_{\text{top}}$ simplifies to: $$ h_{\text{pixel}} = \frac{f_y \cdot H_{\text{real}}}{Z} $$ Rearranging: $$ \boxed{Z = \frac{f_y \cdot H_{\text{real}}}{h_{\text{pixel}}}} $$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $f_y = 1500$ px, $H_{\text{real}} = 0.30$ m, $h_{\text{pixel}} = 12$ px. $$ Z = \frac{1500 \times 0.30}{12} = \frac{450}{12} = 37.5 \text{ m} $$ **Answer:** The traffic light is approximately **37.5 m** from the camera. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4 — Internal Choice B]** **(a)** Explain the role of deep learning (CNN-based detectors such as YOLO) in modern traffic sign recognition systems. Compare it with traditional template-matching approaches. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A traffic light recognition system reports the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1000 images: | | Predicted Red | Predicted Yellow | Predicted Green | |---|---|---|---| | **Actual Red** | 480 | 15 | 5 | | **Actual Yellow** | 20 | 90 | 10 | | **Actual Green** | 10 | 5 | 365 | Compute the **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score for the "Green" class** and comment on the system's performance. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Role of Deep Learning (YOLO-based detectors):** You Only Look Once (YOLO) is a **single-stage, real-time object detector** that simultaneously predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities in a single forward pass of a CNN. In modern TSR: - **End-to-End Learning:** No hand-crafted features (HOG, SIFT) are needed. The network learns traffic sign features directly from labeled datasets like **GTSRB** (German Traffic Sign Recognition Benchmark) with 50,000+ images and 43 classes. - **Multi-Scale Detection:** YOLO uses a **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)** to detect small signs (distant) and large signs (nearby) at the same time. - **Speed:** YOLOv8 runs at **30–160 FPS** on embedded GPUs (NVIDIA Orin, Qualcomm Ride), making it real-time capable. - **Robustness:** CNNs generalize well to viewpoint changes, partial occlusion, motion blur, and weather variations. **Comparison with Template Matching:** | Aspect | Template Matching | CNN (YOLO) | |---|---|---| | Feature Design | Manual templates | Automatic feature learning | | Scale Invariance | Poor (needs multi-scale templates) | Excellent (FPN) | | Rotation Invariance | Poor (needs rotated templates) | Good (data augmentation) | | Speed | Fast on small sets | Fast on GPU | | Accuracy (GTSRB) | ~85% | >99% | | Robustness to noise | Low | High | | Training data needed | Low | High (10k+ images) | **Conclusion:** YOLO/CNN-based approaches dominate modern automotive TSR because they are **robust, scalable, and accurate**, while template matching is now limited to **specific controlled scenarios** (e.g., lane markers in a structured warehouse). #### Model Solution for (b): **Extracting "Green" class from the confusion matrix:** - **True Positives (TP) for Green:** Actual Green predicted as Green $= 365$. - **False Positives (FP) for Green:** Actual Red or Yellow predicted as Green $= 5 + 10 = 15$. - **False Negatives (FN) for Green:** Actual Green predicted as Red or Yellow $= 10 + 5 = 15$. **Precision (P):** $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{365}{365 + 15} = \frac{365}{380} = 0.9605 \approx 96.05\% $$ **Recall (R):** $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{365}{365 + 15} = \frac{365}{380} = 0.9605 \approx 96.05\% $$ **F1-Score:** $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.9605 \times 0.9605}{0.9605 + 0.9605} = 0.9605 \approx 96.05\% $$ **Comment on Performance:** The system achieves a **balanced, high F1-score of ~96%** for the Green class, indicating strong performance. The symmetric Precision and Recall (both 96.05%) suggest the model does not have a bias toward false positives or false negatives. This performance exceeds typical industry acceptance thresholds (F1 $\geq 0.90$). However, the 4% misclassifications should be further reduced using temporal filtering and sensor fusion before being used for safety-critical braking decisions. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TLSR Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to state the assumption** in the pinhole derivation (e.g., not mentioning that $R = I$, $t = 0$, or that the object is at the principal axis). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for missing assumptions. > 2. **Confusing Precision and Recall.** Precision = *of the items I claimed to be Green, how many are actually Green?* Recall = *of all the Greens that exist, how many did I find?* > 3. **Forgetting the F1-Score formula** or computing it as $(P + R)/2$ instead of the harmonic mean $2PR/(P+R)$. > 4. **Not mentioning HSV's illumination invariance** in color-detection answers. This is a **favorite 2-mark bonus question** in KTU papers. > 5. **Skipping the temporal filtering** (Kalman / HMM) stage in pipeline diagrams. Examiners consider it a **mandatory stage** and award 1 mark separately. > 6. **In confusion-matrix problems**, mixing up rows (Actual) and columns (Predicted). Always re-read the matrix header. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** TLSR = detection + localization + classification + state tracking of traffic lights and signs. - **Two Sub-systems:** TLR (vertical light heads) and TSR (flat road signs). - **Pipeline:** Camera $\to$ Pre-process $\to$ ROI $\to$ Color/Shape detection $\to$ CNN $\to$ Temporal filter $\to$ 3D localize $\to$ Map/V2I validate $\to$ Output to planner. - **HSV preferred over RGB** for color detection due to **illumination invariance**. - **Hue ranges (memorize):** Red $\in [0, 10] \cup [170, 180]$, Yellow $\in [20, 35]$, Green $\in [40, 85]$. - **Distance formula:** $Z = \frac{f_y \cdot H_{\text{real}}}{h_{\text{pixel}}}$. - **IoU formula:** $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$, threshold $\geq 0.5$ for TP. - **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP); **Recall** = TP / (TP + FN); **F1** = harmonic mean of P and R. - **State machine rule:** Red $\to$ Red+Yellow $\to$ Green $\to$ Yellow $\to$ Red (cyclic, with constraints). - **Deep learning models:** YOLO, SSD, Faster R-CNN, DETR; trained on GTSRB (signs) and custom TLR datasets. - **Sensor fusion:** Camera + LiDAR + Radar + HD Map + V2I (SPaT) is the modern standard. - **Industry metrics:** $\geq 99\%$ accuracy, $\leq 100$ ms latency, 0–250 m range, 30–60 FPS. - **Standards:** SOTIF (ISO 21448), Euro NCAP, GTSRB benchmark, SAE J3016 (driving automation levels). - **Failure modes:** Sun glare, occlusion, faded signs, brake-light confusion, country-specific designs. - **V2I SPaT data** is the future of signal recognition — it eliminates vision errors entirely. ---

topics.title: Pedestrian Recognition - Basic Principle, Architecture, and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pedestrian Recognition - Basic Principle, Architecture, and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pedestrian Recognition (PR)** is a sub-system of the broader **Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)** family, specifically falling under the **Pedestrian Detection and Protection System (PDPS)** framework defined in ISO 19206 and adopted by Euro NCAP / NHTSA assessment protocols. It is a real-time, on-vehicle, sensor-fusion based **computer-vision system** whose primary engineering objective is the perception, classification, and trajectory prediction of vulnerable road users (VRUs) — primarily pedestrians — within the forward collision zone of an ego-vehicle, with the secondary objective of triggering appropriate actuation responses (visual / haptic warning, automatic emergency braking, or steering intervention).

$$ \text{PR-System} : f(\mathbf{I}_{t}, \mathbf{S}_{t}, \mathbf{Ego}_{t}) \rightarrow \{ \text{Warn, Brake, Steer, \textbf{None}} \} $$

where $\mathbf{I}_{t}$ is the image tensor at time $t$, $\mathbf{S}_{t}$ is the supplementary sensor vector (radar / LiDAR), and $\mathbf{Ego}_{t}$ is the ego-vehicle state vector $\{v, a, \dot{\psi}, \delta\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pedestrian Recognition is assessed as a **perception layer topic** of ADAS. Students must clearly distinguish between *Detection* (where is the pedestrian?), *Classification* (is it really a pedestrian?), *Tracking* (where is it moving?), and *Intent Prediction* (what will it do next?).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car at **50 km/h** down a busy street. Your eyes continuously scan the road ahead. Within fractions of a second, your brain performs three tasks simultaneously: (1) it **spots** a human figure near the kerb, (2) it **recognises** that the figure is a pedestrian (and not a lamp-post or a traffic sign), and (3) it **judges** whether the pedestrian might step onto your path. If risk is detected, your foot automatically hovers over the brake pedal.

**Pedestrian Recognition is the engineering replication of this biological reflex chain using cameras, processors, and machine learning models instead of eyes, neurons, and a human brain.** The camera acts as the "eye," the convolutional neural network (CNN) acts as the "visual cortex," and the ECU acts as the "motor cortex" that commands the brakes or steering.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Pedestrian Recognition

- **Detection Rate (True Positive Rate / Sensitivity):** $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$. Industry benchmark for Euro NCAP AEB-Pedestrian protocols: **$\geq 90\%$** at $40\ \text{km/h}$ daytime.
- **False Positives per Hour (FPH):** Number of spurious detections per hour of driving. Acceptable: **$\leq 1$ FPH** for highway operation.
- **Time-to-Collision (TTC):** $TTC = \dfrac{d_{\text{rel}}}{v_{\text{rel}}}$ where $d_{\text{rel}}$ is the relative distance and $v_{\text{rel}}$ is the relative velocity. Critical intervention threshold: **TTC $\leq 1.2\ \text{s}$** for AEB activation.
- **Detection Range:** Typically **$30$ – $60\ \text{m}$** for monocular camera systems; extended to **$120$ – $200\ \text{m}$** with thermal imaging.
- **Frame Rate Requirement:** **$\geq 30\ \text{fps}$** for real-time tracking, with a latency budget of **$\leq 100\ \text{ms}$** end-to-end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Worth Remembering:**
> - Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ (used in LiDAR ranging)
> - Standard gravity $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ (used in braking distance calculations)
> - Euro NCAP pedestrian test target height: **$\mathbf{1.20\ \text{m}}$ child dummy / $\mathbf{1.80\ \text{m}}$ adult dummy**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Functional Decomposition of the PR Pipeline

A modern Pedestrian Recognition system is structured as a **six-stage sequential pipeline**. Each stage transforms its input data representation into a higher-level semantic abstraction.

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition (Sensing Layer)**
- **Sensor types:** Monocular RGB camera (most common), stereo camera pair, far-infrared (FIR) thermal camera, short-range LiDAR, and millimetre-wave radar.
- The **region of interest (ROI)** is pre-defined as a trapezoidal mask in the image plane corresponding to the road surface in front of the vehicle (extending from the bumper to the vanishing horizon line).
- Image resolution is typically **$\mathbf{1280 \times 960}$ pixels** at **$30$ – $60\ \text{fps}$**, with HDR (High Dynamic Range) support to handle tunnel-exit and shadow transitions.

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing**
- **Why?** Camera raw Bayer-pattern data contains sensor noise, lens distortion, and dynamic range issues that would corrupt downstream feature extraction.
- Operations include: dark-current subtraction, **Bayer demosaicing**, **lens distortion correction** (using the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ and distortion vector), **histogram equalisation** for contrast enhancement, and **temporal smoothing** using exponential moving average filters.

**Stage 3 — Candidate Generation (Region Proposal)**
- Sliding-window search over the ROI using a multi-scale image pyramid. Each window is a candidate pedestrian region.
- Classical methods used **Haar-like wavelets + AdaBoost classifiers** (Viola–Jones framework, 2001). Modern systems use **Region Proposal Networks (RPN)** as part of Faster R-CNN architecture, or anchor-free methods like **CenterNet** and **FCOS**.

**Stage 4 — Feature Extraction & Classification**
- The cropped candidate region is normalised (e.g., resized to $64 \times 128$ pixels) and passed through a feature extractor.
- Classical feature: **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)** descriptor. Modern feature: deep CNN feature maps from backbones like **ResNet-50**, **MobileNet-V3**, or **EfficientNet-B0**.
- Classifier: classical — **SVM (Support Vector Machine)**; modern — fully-connected classification head outputting $P(\text{person}\vert x)$.

**Stage 5 — Multi-Object Tracking (MOT)**
- Pedestrian candidates across consecutive frames are linked using a **Kalman Filter** state estimator combined with the **Hungarian Algorithm** for optimal assignment.
- State vector: $\mathbf{x}_{k} = [u, v, s, r, \dot{u}, \dot{v}, \dot{s}]^{T}$ where $(u,v)$ is the bounding-box centre, $s$ is scale, $r$ is aspect ratio, and dots denote temporal derivatives.
- This stage converts per-frame detections into a **trajectory** with a unique track-ID.

**Stage 6 — Trajectory Prediction & Decision**
- A motion model (Constant Velocity, or learned LSTM / Social-LSTM) predicts the future position of each tracked pedestrian.
- A risk function combines **TTC**, **lateral overlap**, and **pedestrian pose** (whether the body is oriented toward the road) to produce a final actuation decision.

### 2.2 Why Sensor Fusion is Mandatory

No single sensor is sufficient for robust pedestrian recognition across all conditions:

| Sensor | Strength | Weakness | Why Fusion is Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RGB Camera** | Rich texture, colour | Fails in low light / glare | Cannot provide direct distance |
| **Thermal FIR** | Works in darkness | Cannot work through glass; warm objects only | Low resolution |
| **Radar** | Direct velocity, all-weather | Low spatial resolution, many false positives | Cannot classify objects |
| **LiDAR** | Accurate 3-D point cloud | Expensive, struggles in heavy rain | Sparse at long range |

The **early-fusion** and **late-fusion** architectures combine these complementary strengths.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** a KTU student is expected to know for this topic. These appear regularly in **Part A (3-mark)** derivations and **Part B (14-mark)** numericals.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-to-Collision | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{\text{rel}}}{v_{\text{rel}}}$ | seconds; $d$ in m, $v$ in m/s |
| 2 | Braking Distance (kinematic) | $d_{b} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | metres; $\mu$ = tyre-road friction coeff. |
| 3 | Reaction Distance | $d_{r} = v \cdot t_{r}$ | metres; $t_{r}$ in s |
| 4 | Stopping Distance | $d_{s} = d_{r} + d_{b}$ | total stopping distance |
| 5 | Pinhole Camera Projection | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length, $Z$ = depth |
| 6 | Disparity (Stereo) | $d_{\text{disp}} = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z}$ | $B$ = baseline, $Z$ = depth |
| 7 | Depth from Disparity | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d_{\text{disp}}}$ | metres |
| 8 | IoU (Intersection over Union) | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le IoU \le 1$ |
| 9 | HOG Cell Gradient Magnitude | $G = \sqrt{G_{x}^{2} + G_{y}^{2}}$ | per-pixel intensity gradient |
| 10 | HOG Gradient Orientation | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{G_{y}}{G_{x}}\right)$ | in degrees, binned $0^{\circ}$ – $180^{\circ}$ |
| 11 | Kalman Filter Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1}$ | linear state propagation |
| 12 | Kalman Filter Update | $\mathbf{K}_{k} = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}^{T} + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 13 | LiDAR Range Equation | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\Delta t$ = time-of-flight |
| 14 | Radar Doppler Velocity | $v_{r} = \dfrac{f_{D} \lambda}{2}$ | $f_{D}$ = Doppler shift, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 15 | Detection Confidence (Sigmoid) | $P(c) = \sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | output of classification head |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations 1–4 underpin the **decision layer** of every AEB system — they determine *when* to brake. Equations 5–7 underpin the **perception layer** — they determine *where* the pedestrian is in 3-D space. Equations 11–12 underpin the **temporal layer** — they determine *how the pedestrian is moving*. KTU questions typically test one equation from each group in a single problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time-to-Collision (TTC) and Braking Decision Logic

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):** An ego-vehicle is travelling at $v_{e} = 60\ \text{km/h}$ on a dry asphalt road ($\mu = 0.7$). A pedestrian is detected by the vision system at a longitudinal distance $d = 25\ \text{m}$ directly ahead. The pedestrian is stationary ($v_{p} = 0$). The system reaction latency is $t_{r} = 0.3\ \text{s}$. Determine: (a) the current Time-to-Collision, (b) the required deceleration if the system chooses to brake, and (c) whether the AEB must intervene, given a target TTC threshold of $1.2\ \text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Unit Conversion of Ego Velocity**

We first convert $v_{e}$ from $\text{km/h}$ to $\text{m/s}$ to maintain SI consistency in the kinematic equations.

$$ v_{e} = 60\ \dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = 60 \times \dfrac{1000\ \text{m}}{3600\ \text{s}} $$

$$ v_{e} = \dfrac{60000}{3600} = 16.67\ \text{m/s} $$

> This is the standard conversion factor: $1\ \text{km/h} = \dfrac{5}{18}\ \text{m/s}$.

**Step 2 — Time-to-Collision Calculation**

The relative velocity between the ego-vehicle and the stationary pedestrian is simply the ego-vehicle's velocity, since the pedestrian is not moving.

$$ v_{\text{rel}} = v_{e} - v_{p} = 16.67 - 0 = 16.67\ \text{m/s} $$

Applying the TTC formula from the cheat sheet:

$$ TTC = \dfrac{d_{\text{rel}}}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \dfrac{25}{16.67} $$

$$ TTC = 1.50\ \text{s} $$

> **Interpretation:** At the current speed and distance, the collision will occur in 1.5 seconds if neither the vehicle brakes nor the pedestrian moves.

**Step 3 — Required Deceleration to Avoid Collision**

The vehicle must come to rest within the available distance $d = 25\ \text{m}$, accounting for the system reaction latency distance.

First, compute the distance travelled during the reaction time (constant velocity assumption):

$$ d_{r} = v_{e} \cdot t_{r} = 16.67 \times 0.3 = 5.00\ \text{m} $$

The remaining distance available for actual braking is:

$$ d_{b,\text{avail}} = d - d_{r} = 25 - 5 = 20\ \text{m} $$

Now, from the kinematic equation $v_{f}^{2} = v_{i}^{2} - 2 a d_{b,\text{avail}}$ with $v_{f} = 0$ (vehicle must stop), we solve for the deceleration $a$:

$$ 0 = v_{e}^{2} - 2 a \cdot d_{b,\text{avail}} $$

$$ a = \dfrac{v_{e}^{2}}{2 \cdot d_{b,\text{avail}}} $$

Substituting values:

$$ a = \dfrac{(16.67)^{2}}{2 \times 20} = \dfrac{277.89}{40} $$

$$ a = 6.95\ \text{m/s}^{2} $$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Friction Limit**

The maximum achievable deceleration is limited by the tyre-road friction coefficient:

$$ a_{\max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.87\ \text{m/s}^{2} $$

> **Comparison:** Since the required $a = 6.95\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ is **slightly greater** than the friction-limited $a_{\max} = 6.87\ \text{m/s}^{2}$, a pure braking manoeuvre is **insufficient** to avoid the collision. The system must therefore either: (a) combine braking with **steering evasive manoeuvre**, or (b) trigger the warning earlier, or (c) accept a reduced impact velocity.

**Step 5 — AEB Intervention Decision**

Compare the computed TTC with the intervention threshold:

$$ TTC_{\text{current}} = 1.50\ \text{s} \quad \text{vs} \quad TTC_{\text{threshold}} = 1.20\ \text{s} $$

Since $TTC_{\text{current}} > TTC_{\text{threshold}}$ **and** the available friction is insufficient for pure braking, the system logic is:

- If $TTC > 1.20\ \text{s}$: Issue a **forward collision warning (FCW)** only — no actuation.
- If $1.0 < TTC \le 1.20\ \text{s}$: Issue FCW + **partial autonomous braking** at $4\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.
- If $TTC \le 1.0\ \text{s}$: Full **AEB** at maximum deceleration.

> **Conclusion:** The system issues a **forward collision warning** to the driver. Braking intervention is **not yet commanded** but is pre-armed.

### 3.2 Derivation: Depth Estimation from a Stereo Camera Pair

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle is equipped with a stereo camera having focal length $f = 6\ \text{mm}$, baseline $B = 200\ \text{mm}$, and a pedestrian is detected with a disparity of $d_{\text{disp}} = 12\ \text{pixels}$ on a sensor with pixel pitch $p = 4\ \mu\text{m}$. Find the depth (longitudinal distance) of the pedestrian.

**Step 1 — Convert Disparity from Pixels to Metres**

The disparity in pixel units must be converted to physical units using the pixel pitch.

$$ d_{\text{disp,metric}} = d_{\text{disp}} \cdot p = 12 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} $$

$$ d_{\text{disp,metric}} = 4.8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stereo Depth Formula**

From the cheat sheet:

$$ Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d_{\text{disp,metric}}} $$

Substituting (with consistent SI units — focal length and baseline in metres):

$$ Z = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \times 10^{-3}}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ Z = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ Z = 25\ \text{m} $$

> **Interpretation:** The pedestrian is located **25 metres** in front of the vehicle. This matches the input distance in the previous problem, demonstrating cross-validation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Detection Paradigms

| Paradigm | Era | Feature Extractor | Classifier | Speed (FPS) | Accuracy (mAP) | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Viola–Jones + Haar** | 2001 | Haar wavelets | AdaBoost cascade | 15 | Low | Conceptual question only |
| **HOG + SVM (Dalal–Triggs 2005)** | 2005 | HOG descriptors | Linear SVM | 8–10 | Moderate | Derivations on HOG gradient |
| **DPM (Deformable Parts Model)** | 2008 | HOG + part filters | Latent SVM | 5 | Moderate-High | Rarely asked |
| **Faster R-CNN** | 2015 | VGG-16 / ResNet-50 | Two-stage RPN + detector | 7 | Very High | Architecture diagram question |
| **YOLOv3 / YOLOv8** | 2018 / 2023 | Darknet-53 / CSP-Darknet | One-stage regression head | 45 – 160 | High | Common in architecture Q |
| **Transformer-based (DETR)** | 2020 | CNN backbone + Transformer encoder-decoder | Set-prediction head | 25 | High | Mentioned in advanced topics |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of a Pedestrian Recognition System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Scenario Input: Urban Road Environment] --> A1[Sensor Array Suite]
    A1 --> A2[Monocular RGB Camera 1280x960]
    A1 --> A3[Stereo Camera Pair]
    A1 --> A4[Millimetre Wave Radar 77 GHz]
    A1 --> A5[Short Range LiDAR]
    A2 --> B1[Image Pre-processing Pipeline]
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Region of Interest ROI Extraction]
    B2 --> B3[Multi-scale Image Pyramid Generation]
    B3 --> C1[Candidate Region Proposal Module]
    C1 --> C2[Feature Extraction Backbone CNN]
    C2 --> C3[Classification Head: Person vs Non Person]
    C3 --> C4[Confidence Score Threshold 0.5]
    C4 --> C5[Bounding Box Regression and NMS]
    A4 --> D1[Sensor Fusion Layer]
    A5 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Multi Object Tracker Kalman Filter]
    E1 --> E2[Trajectory History Buffer 30 Frames]
    E2 --> E3[Trajectory Prediction LSTM Model]
    E3 --> F1[Risk Assessment Module TTC Calculation]
    F1 --> F2[Decision Logic Matrix]
    F2 --> G1[Driver Warning Haptic and Visual]
    F2 --> G2[Automatic Emergency Braking AEB]
    F2 --> G3[Emergency Steering Assist ESA]
    F2 --> G4[No Action Safe State]
    G1 --> H1[Vehicle Actuator Bus CAN FlexRay]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Detailed Data-Flow of the CNN-Based Pedestrian Detection Core

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Input Image 1280x960x3] --> P1[Convolutional Layer Conv1 7x7 stride 2]
    P1 --> P2[Max Pooling 3x3 stride 2]
    P2 --> P3[Residual Block Stage 2]
    P3 --> P4[Residual Block Stage 3]
    P4 --> P5[Residual Block Stage 4]
    P5 --> P6[Residual Block Stage 5]
    P6 --> P7[Feature Map 20x15x2048]
    P7 --> P8[Region Proposal Network RPN]
    P8 --> P9[Anchor Box Generation 3 scales 3 ratios]
    P9 --> P10[Objectness Score and Box Regression]
    P10 --> P11[RoI Pooling Layer]
    P11 --> P12[Fully Connected Layer 1024 units]
    P12 --> P13[Softmax Classifier Person Background]
    P12 --> P14[Bounding Box Coordinate Regressor]
    P13 --> P15[Final Detection Pedestrian ID Confidence]
    P14 --> P15
    P15 --> P16[Non Maximum Suppression NMS IoU 0.5]
    P16 --> P17[Output: Pedestrian Bounding Boxes]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Pedestrian Tracking with Kalman Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Frame k-1: Detected Pedestrian Box] --> Q2[Predict State Estimate x_k|k-1]
    Q2 --> Q3[Predict Error Covariance P_k|k-1]
    Q3 --> Q4[Frame k: New Measurement z_k]
    Q4 --> Q5[Compute Kalman Gain K_k]
    Q5 --> Q6[Update State x_k|k = x_k|k-1 + K_k * Innovation]
    Q6 --> Q7[Update Covariance P_k|k]
    Q7 --> Q8[Output: Corrected Pedestrian Position and Velocity]
    Q8 --> Q9[Trajectory Buffer: Append Position]
    Q9 --> Q10[LSTM Predicts Position at k+5]
    Q10 --> Q11[Risk Function Evaluates TTC and Lateral Offset]
    Q11 --> Q12{TTC less than 1.2 s?}
    Q12 -- Yes --> Q13[Trigger AEB or ESA]
    Q12 -- No --> Q14[Continue Monitoring Loop]
    Q13 --> Q15[Driver Haptic and Visual Warning]
    Q14 --> Q1
    Q15 --> Q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the Mermaid flowcharts above, every node is identified by an alphanumeric token (e.g., `P1`, `Q3`, `G2`) and every label uses clean uppercase text without embedded markdown formatting. This ensures the diagrams render correctly in any Mermaid-compatible viewer including GitHub, VS Code, and the KTU digital learning portal.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define *Pedestrian Recognition* in the context of ADAS. List the **two most commonly used sensor modalities** for pedestrian detection and state one **specific limitation** of each.

**Model Answer:**
Pedestrian Recognition is a real-time computer-vision-based perception function of an Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) that identifies, classifies, and tracks vulnerable road users — primarily pedestrians — within the forward path of an ego-vehicle, to enable collision avoidance or mitigation responses.

**Two commonly used sensor modalities:**
1. **Monocular RGB Camera:** Provides high-resolution colour imagery rich in texture features for classification. **Limitation:** Performance degrades severely in low-light, backlight, and glare conditions; cannot directly measure depth.
2. **Millimetre-Wave Radar (77 GHz):** Provides direct range and radial velocity of objects and operates in all weather. **Limitation:** Very low spatial resolution; cannot distinguish a pedestrian from a small animal, sign post, or roadside debris, leading to high false-positive rates.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is the **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)** descriptor? Why is it particularly suitable for pedestrian detection in classical vision pipelines?

**Model Answer:**
The HOG descriptor is a feature extraction technique that characterises local object appearance and shape within an image by the **distribution of intensity gradient orientations**. The image is divided into small connected cells, and for each cell, a 1-D histogram of gradient directions (typically binned over $0^{\circ}$ – $180^{\circ}$ in 9 bins) is computed. These cell histograms are then contrast-normalised over larger overlapping blocks to produce the final feature vector.

**Why suitable for pedestrians:** Human bodies have a distinctive **silhouette and edge orientation pattern** — vertical edges dominate the torso and limbs, while horizontal edges define the shoulders and head. HOG captures this structural signature compactly and is **invariant to small geometric and photometric transformations**, which are common in real driving scenes due to viewpoint, lighting, and clothing variation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **confuse the roles of detection and classification** in the pipeline. Detection = *localising* the pedestrian (where?). Classification = *confirming* the pedestrian (what?).
> 2. Many students forget to **convert units** (km/h to m/s) in TTC and braking problems — this single error causes a **3-mark deduction** in numericals.
> 3. Drawing the architecture without **labelling data flow directions** (e.g., arrows) is considered an incomplete answer and incurs a **2-mark penalty**.
> 4. Stating only the algorithm name (e.g., "YOLO") without explaining its **working principle or why it suits pedestrian detection** is treated as a surface-level answer and capped at **half marks** for that sub-part.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 4 Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the complete architecture of a vision-based Pedestrian Recognition system. Clearly label the following stages in your diagram: (i) Image acquisition, (ii) ROI extraction, (iii) Pre-processing, (iv) Feature extraction and classification, (v) Tracking, (vi) Decision and actuation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ego-vehicle travelling at $v_{e} = 45\ \text{km/h}$ on a wet road ($\mu = 0.4$) detects a pedestrian at a longitudinal distance of $d = 18\ \text{m}$. The system reaction time is $t_{r} = 0.4\ \text{s}$. Calculate: (i) the Time-to-Collision (TTC), (ii) the friction-limited maximum deceleration, and (iii) state with justification whether the AEB should intervene assuming a TTC intervention threshold of $1.2\ \text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

The architecture consists of six sequential stages connected by a **CAN/FlexRay** communication bus to the vehicle's actuation layer.

| Stage | Function | Key Components | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Image Acquisition | Capture raw image data | CMOS sensor, lens, AFE | Bayer pattern / YUV frame |
| (ii) ROI Extraction | Mask out sky, buildings | Image segmentation, vanishing point | Trapezoidal road-region mask |
| (iii) Pre-processing | Remove noise, normalise | ISP, demosaic, gamma correction | Enhanced RGB image |
| (iv) Feature + Classification | Detect and identify pedestrian | CNN backbone (ResNet-50) + head | Bounding boxes + class scores |
| (v) Tracking | Maintain identity over time | Kalman filter + Hungarian matcher | Pedestrian track with ID |
| (vi) Decision + Actuation | Evaluate risk and act | Risk function, ECU, brake actuator | Brake / warn / steer commands |

*A full block diagram is to be drawn in the examination hall. Reference: see Section 4.1 of these notes.*

**Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Unit conversion (1 mark):**
$$ v_{e} = 45\ \dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = 45 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 12.5\ \text{m/s} $$

**Step 2 — Time-to-Collision (2 marks):**

The pedestrian is stationary, so $v_{\text{rel}} = 12.5\ \text{m/s}$.

$$ TTC = \dfrac{d}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \dfrac{18}{12.5} = 1.44\ \text{s} $$

**Step 3 — Friction-limited deceleration (2 marks):**

$$ a_{\max} = \mu g = 0.4 \times 9.81 = 3.92\ \text{m/s}^{2} $$

**Step 4 — Decision (2 marks):**

Since $TTC_{\text{current}} = 1.44\ \text{s} > TTC_{\text{threshold}} = 1.20\ \text{s}$, the system is in the **warning zone, not the intervention zone**. The system should:
- Issue a **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** to alert the driver.
- **Pre-arm** the brake system (move brake pads closer to discs) to reduce latency.
- **Not yet apply** full AEB braking.

**Conclusion:** AEB intervention is **not yet triggered**; only a warning is issued.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 4 Set B] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a CNN-based pedestrian detector** such as Faster R-CNN or YOLO. In your answer, describe: (i) the role of the convolutional backbone, (ii) the concept of **anchor boxes**, and (iii) the function of **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stereo camera system has a focal length $f = 4.5\ \text{mm}$, baseline $B = 150\ \text{mm}$, and pixel pitch $p = 5\ \mu\text{m}$. A pedestrian is detected with a pixel disparity of $8\ \text{pixels}$. Calculate: (i) the metric disparity in metres, and (ii) the depth (longitudinal distance) of the pedestrian from the camera. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution (a) — CNN Detector Working (7 marks):**

(i) **Convolutional Backbone (3 marks):** The backbone is a deep CNN (e.g., ResNet-50) pre-trained on large image datasets such as ImageNet and fine-tuned on pedestrian-specific datasets like CityPersons or EuroCity Persons. It performs **hierarchical feature extraction** — early layers capture low-level features (edges, gradients), middle layers capture mid-level features (textures, parts), and deep layers capture high-level semantic features (whole-object concepts like "head," "torso," "limbs"). The output is a rich feature map that serves as input to the detection head.

(ii) **Anchor Boxes (2 marks):** Anchor boxes are pre-defined reference bounding boxes of various scales and aspect ratios tiled across the spatial positions of the feature map. In Faster R-CNN, typically **3 scales** $(\{128^{2}, 256^{2}, 512^{2}\})$ and **3 aspect ratios** $(\{1{:}1, 1{:}2, 2{:}1\})$ are used, giving 9 anchors per location. The network learns to **refine** these anchors into accurate pedestrian-sized boxes by predicting offsets and class scores for each anchor.

(iii) **Non-Maximum Suppression (2 marks):** A single pedestrian typically generates hundreds of overlapping candidate boxes with high confidence. NMS iteratively selects the box with the **highest confidence score**, then suppresses (removes) all other boxes that have an **IoU (Intersection over Union) greater than a threshold** (typically 0.5) with the selected box. This process repeats until no boxes remain, yielding a single, clean bounding box per pedestrian. NMS is critical because downstream tracking and TTC calculation require **one unique detection per pedestrian**.

**Solution (b) — Stereo Depth Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert pixel disparity to metric disparity (3 marks):**

$$ d_{\text{disp,metric}} = d_{\text{pixels}} \times p = 8 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} $$

$$ d_{\text{disp,metric}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the stereo depth formula (3 marks):**

With focal length and baseline converted to metres:

$$ f = 4.5\ \text{mm} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} $$

$$ B = 150\ \text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{m} $$

$$ Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d_{\text{disp,metric}}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-1}}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ Z = \dfrac{6.75 \times 10^{-4}}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 16.875\ \text{m} $$

**Step 3 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$ \boxed{Z \approx 16.9\ \text{m}} $$

The pedestrian is located approximately **16.9 metres** in front of the stereo camera rig.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Know Points for KTU Module 4:**

- **Definition:** Pedestrian Recognition is a perception function of ADAS that **detects, classifies, and tracks pedestrians** for collision avoidance.
- **Core Pipeline (in order):** Sensing → Pre-processing → ROI Extraction → Detection → Classification → Tracking → Trajectory Prediction → Decision.
- **Primary Sensor:** Vision camera (monocular or stereo); supported by radar and LiDAR via **sensor fusion**.
- **Classical Feature:** **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)** + SVM classifier — Dalal & Triggs, 2005.
- **Modern Feature:** Deep **CNN** features (ResNet, EfficientNet) + region proposal networks (Faster R-CNN) or single-shot detectors (YOLO).
- **Key TTC Formula:** $TTC = d_{\text{rel}} / v_{\text{rel}}$; intervention threshold typically **1.2 s**.
- **Braking Equation:** $d_{b} = v^{2} / (2 \mu g)$; reaction distance $d_{r} = v \cdot t_{r}$.
- **Stereo Depth:** $Z = f \cdot B / d_{\text{disp}}$; remember to convert all units to **metres** and pixels to metric units using pixel pitch.
- **NMS Purpose:** Eliminates duplicate overlapping boxes using **IoU threshold** (typically 0.5) so that each pedestrian has exactly one bounding box.
- **Kalman Filter Role:** Maintains a smooth estimate of pedestrian **position and velocity** across frames even when individual frame detections are noisy.
- **Euro NCAP Standards:** Test protocol uses child dummy (**1.20 m**) and adult dummy (**1.80 m**) targets; AEB-Pedestrian is a **mandatory rating criterion** since 2016.
- **Common Pitfall:** Always convert **km/h to m/s** by multiplying with $5/18$ before any kinematic calculation.
- **Sensor Limitation:** Cameras fail in darkness → use **thermal FIR**; radar cannot classify → use **vision**; hence **fusion is mandatory** for 5-star safety rating.
- **Architecture Drawing Rule:** Always include **arrowheads** showing data flow direction; clearly label all inter-stage buses (CAN / FlexRay / Ethernet).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Intelligent Systems in New Generation Cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Building Intelligent Systems in New Generation Cars # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intelligent Vehicle System (IVS):** A cyber-physical vehicular system that integrates **on-board sensors, actuators, embedded computing platforms, communication networks, and intelligent algorithms** (signal processing, control theory, machine learning, and artificial intelligence) to perceive its environment, reason about dynamic traffic conditions, plan optimal trajectories, and execute driving decisions with minimal or zero human intervention. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework for *Automotive Navigation and Control*, an "Intelligent System in a New Generation Car" is understood as a **multi-layered, sensor-fused, real-time decision-making architecture** that transforms a conventional vehicle into a context-aware, connected, and (potentially) autonomous machine. ### SAE International Standard J3016 — Levels of Driving Automation The industry-standard taxonomy is mandatory for any KTU answer on this topic: | SAE Level | Name | Steering/Acceleration | Monitoring of Environment | Fallback Performance | System Capability | |-----------|------|------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|-------------------| | **0** | No Automation | Human | Human | Human | None | | **1** | Driver Assistance | Human + System | Human | Human | Lateral **OR** Longitudinal | | **2** | Partial Automation | System | Human | Human | Lateral **AND** Longitudinal | | **3** | Conditional Automation | System | System | Human | Limited ODD | | **4** | High Automation | System | System | System | Specific ODD | | **5** | Full Automation | System | System | System | All Conditions | **ODD = Operational Design Domain** (geographic, weather, speed, and time-of-day envelope in which the system is designed to operate). > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition — Intelligent Transportation System (ITS):** A broad, integrated framework that applies information, communication, sensor, and control technologies across vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), pedestrians (V2P), and networks (V2N) — collectively **V2X** — to improve safety, mobility, and environmental performance of the entire transport ecosystem. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart-Phone on Wheels" Model Imagine your **smartphone**. It is intelligent because it has: - **Cameras and microphones** (perception/sensors), - **A powerful processor** (the brain / decision unit), - **Apps running algorithms** (intelligence / software), - **Internet/Wi-Fi/Bluetooth** (communication), - **Touchscreen and speakers** (HMI — Human-Machine Interface), - **A battery and motors for vibration** (actuators). A **new-generation intelligent car is conceptually a "smartphone on four wheels."** It uses the same architectural layers: - **Perception** = eyes (cameras, LiDAR, radar — they "see" the world). - **Cognition/Decision** = brain (ECU, GPU, AI accelerator chips — they "think"). - **Actuation** = muscles (steering, brake, throttle by-wire — they "act"). - **Communication** = mouth/ears (V2X antennas, 5G, DSRC — they "talk" and "listen"). - **HMI** = face (digital cluster, HUD, voice — they "communicate with the driver"). **Real-world analogy for sensor fusion:** A driver uses **two eyes and two ears** to estimate the distance and direction of an ambulance. If only one sense is used, estimation is poor. **Sensor fusion** mathematically combines multiple "senses" of the car to produce a single, highly confident estimate of the world state. ## 1.3 Core Metrics and Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory numerical awareness for KTU answers:** > - **End-to-end system latency budget:** typically **< 100 ms** for safety-critical decisions (braking). > - **Sensor sampling rate:** Cameras **30–60 fps**, Radar **~20 Hz**, LiDAR **10–20 Hz**. > - **Operational temperature for automotive ECUs:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** (AEC-Q100 Grade 2). > - **Functional Safety Standard:** **ISO 26262**, with **ASIL-D** being the highest integrity level. > - **SOTIF (Safety of the Intended Functionality):** **ISO 21448** for AI/ML-based perception. > - **V2X Communication Standards:** **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** and **C-V2X (3GPP Release 14+)**. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Curriculum - Aligns with **Course Outcome CO4** (apply intelligent control concepts to automotive systems). - Supports **CO5** (design of perception and decision modules). - Maps to **Bloom's L2 (Understand)** and **L4 (Analyze)** cognitive levels in the official question paper pattern. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four-Layer Architectural Model of an Intelligent Vehicle Modern intelligent systems are universally decomposed into the following **functional layers** (this is a *guaranteed 7-mark question* structure in KTU ESE): ### Layer 1 — **Perception Layer (Sense)** - **Exteroceptive sensors** (sense the external world): - **Camera** (mono/stereo): Class, lane, traffic sign, pedestrian detection. - **Radar** (24/77 GHz): Range, radial velocity of obstacles; robust to fog/rain. - **LiDAR** (905 nm or 1550 nm laser): High-resolution 3D point cloud. - **Ultrasonic** (40–70 kHz): Close-range parking (0.1–5 m). - **Proprioceptive sensors** (sense the car itself): - Wheel-speed encoders, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit: gyroscope + accelerometer), GNSS receiver, steering-angle sensor. ### Layer 2 — **Localization & Mapping Layer (Locate)** - **GNSS** (Global Navigation Satellite Systems: GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS/NavIC) → absolute position. - **IMU + Odometry** → high-frequency relative motion. - **HD Maps** (High-Definition Maps): Lane-level, cm-accuracy prior maps. - **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping): Builds map while localizing. ### Layer 3 — **Decision & Planning Layer (Think)** - **Behavioural planning:** lane-change, intersection handling, yielding. - **Motion planning:** trajectory generation (polynomial, clothoid, A*, RRT*). - **Predictive modelling:** trajectory prediction of other road users using ML (LSTMs, GRUs, Transformers). ### Layer 4 — **Actuation Layer (Act)** - **Drive-by-wire:** Electronic throttle, electronic brake, steer-by-wire. - **Vehicle Dynamics Control:** ESC (Electronic Stability Control), AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking), ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control). - **Powertrain coordination:** Torque vectoring, regenerative braking blending. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.** | # | Concept | Equation | Variable Definition | Units | Typical Value / Boundary | |---|---------|----------|---------------------|-------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | $d_{rel}$ = relative distance to lead vehicle; $v_{rel}$ = relative velocity (closing) | s | Braking initiated if $TTC \le 2.7$ s (Euro-NCAP AEB protocol) | | 2 | **Longitudinal Safety Distance** | $d_{safe} = v \cdot t_{reaction} + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | $v$ = ego velocity, $t_{reaction} \approx 1.5$ s, $\mu$ = tyre-road friction, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | m | At $v = 100$ km/h, $\mu = 0.7$: $d_{safe} \approx 41.7 + 54.9 = 96.6$ m | | 3 | **Radar Range Equation** | $R_{max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{min}} \right]^{1/4}$ | $P_t$ = transmit power, $G$ = antenna gain, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\sigma$ = RCS, $S_{min}$ = minimum detectable signal | m | 77 GHz radar: $R_{max} \approx 200$ m for car RCS | | 4 | **LiDAR Photogrammetry** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time of light pulse | m | Range resolution $\Delta d = 3$ cm typical | | 5 | **Camera Pinhole Projection** | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length, $(X,Y,Z)$ = world point, $(x,y)$ = image point | pixels | Used for intrinsic camera calibration | | 6 | **Bayesian Sensor Fusion (posterior)** | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | $H$ = hypothesis (e.g., obstacle present), $E$ = evidence from sensor | unitless | Normalized 0–1 | | 7 | **Kalman Filter — Prediction** | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B u_{k-1}$
$P_{k \mid k-1} = A P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A^T + Q$ | $A$ = state transition, $B$ = input matrix, $Q$ = process noise covariance | — | Linear-Gaussian system assumption | | 8 | **Kalman Filter — Update** | $K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$
$\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1})$ | $K$ = Kalman gain, $H$ = measurement matrix, $R$ = measurement noise | — | $K \in [0,1]$; trust ratio | | 9 | **Bicycle Model (Kinematic)** | $\dot{x} = v \cos(\psi)$
$\dot{y} = v \sin(\psi)$
$\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v}{L} \tan(\delta_f)$ | $(x,y)$ = CG position, $\psi$ = heading, $v$ = speed, $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta_f$ = front steer | m, m/s, rad/s | Foundation of vehicle dynamics simulation | | 10 | **Ackermann Steering Geometry** | $\delta_i = \arctan\left( \dfrac{L}{R - \tfrac{t_r}{2}} \right) \quad ; \quad \delta_o = \arctan\left( \dfrac{L}{R + \tfrac{t_r}{2}} \right)$ | $R$ = turning radius at CG, $t_r$ = track width, $L$ = wheelbase | rad | Inner wheel steers more than outer | | 11 | **Free-Space Flow (5G URLLC)** | $L_{flow} \le \dfrac{1}{\mu} \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{P h^2}{N_0 B \mu}\right)$ | $\mu$ = SINR threshold, $B$ = bandwidth, $N_0$ = noise PSD | bits/s/Hz | URLLC target latency = 1 ms | | 12 | **Path Tracking Error (Stanley)** | $\delta(t) = \psi(t) + \arctan\left( \dfrac{k \cdot e(t)}{v(t)} \right)$ | $e$ = cross-track error, $k$ = gain, $\psi$ = heading error | rad | Used in autonomous racing (Stanford DARPA) | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Systems - **Safety:** AEB alone has been shown to reduce rear-end crashes by **~38 %** (Euro-NCAP, 2023 report). - **Efficiency:** Predictive ACC using V2I traffic-light data reduces urban fuel consumption by **up to 15 %**. - **Comfort:** Adaptive dampers using road-preview camera reduce body acceleration by **~30 %**. - **Regulatory driver:** UNECE Regulation 157 (Automated Lane Keeping Systems, ALKS) is the first international Level-3 homologation law, effective **2021**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner note:** Three derivations are given in full. These are the most likely analytical parts of a 14-mark question. Do not skip any line. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayesian Sensor Fusion for Object Existence (Pedestrian Detection) **Problem Setup:** Two independent sensors report evidence about whether a pedestrian is in front of the car. - **Camera** reports "pedestrian" with detection probability (likelihood): $P(E_C \mid H) = 0.95$, and false-positive rate: $P(E_C \mid \overline{H}) = 0.05$. - **Radar** reports "pedestrian" with likelihood: $P(E_R \mid H) = 0.80$, false-positive rate: $P(E_R \mid \overline{H}) = 0.10$. - Prior probability of pedestrian at intersection: $P(H) = 0.10$ (base rate). **Goal:** Find posterior $P(H \mid E_C \cap E_R)$. ### Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem (Single Sensor, Camera) $$ P(H \mid E_C) = \frac{P(E_C \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E_C)} $$ ### Step 2 — Expand the Denominator Using the Law of Total Probability $$ P(E_C) = P(E_C \mid H) P(H) + P(E_C \mid \overline{H}) P(\overline{H}) $$ $$ P(E_C) = (0.95)(0.10) + (0.05)(0.90) $$ $$ P(E_C) = 0.095 + 0.045 = 0.140 $$ ### Step 3 — Camera-Only Posterior $$ P(H \mid E_C) = \frac{(0.95)(0.10)}{0.140} = \frac{0.095}{0.140} \approx 0.6786 $$ ### Step 4 — Combine With Radar (Joint Likelihood Assumption of Independence) $$ P(H \mid E_C \cap E_R) = \frac{P(E_C \cap E_R \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E_C \cap E_R)} $$ Because the sensors are **conditionally independent** given the hypothesis: $$ P(E_C \cap E_R \mid H) = P(E_C \mid H) \cdot P(E_R \mid H) = (0.95)(0.80) = 0.760 $$ $$ P(E_C \cap E_R \mid \overline{H}) = (0.05)(0.10) = 0.005 $$ ### Step 5 — Denominator of the Joint Posterior $$ P(E_C \cap E_R) = (0.760)(0.10) + (0.005)(0.90) = 0.0760 + 0.0045 = 0.0805 $$ ### Step 6 — Fused Posterior (Decision) $$ P(H \mid E_C \cap E_R) = \frac{(0.760)(0.10)}{0.0805} = \frac{0.0760}{0.0805} \approx 0.9441 $$ ### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation > The fused confidence jumped from **67.9 % (camera only)** to **94.4 % (camera + radar)**. This is the **fundamental quantitative justification for multi-sensor fusion in intelligent vehicles.** A threshold of $P \ge 0.90$ is typically required to trigger AEB; therefore, *the camera alone would have failed to trigger braking*, while fusion succeeds. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kalman Filter One-Step Cycle for Vehicle Longitudinal State Estimation **State vector to estimate:** $x_k = \begin{bmatrix} p_k \\ v_k \end{bmatrix}$, where $p$ = position, $v$ = velocity. **Process model (constant velocity):** $$ x_{k} = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_{k-1} $$ $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \Delta t^2 \\ \Delta t \end{bmatrix}, \quad u = a_{cmd} $$ Assume $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, $a_{cmd} = 2$ m/s² (commanded acceleration). ### Step 1 — Predicted State $$ \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{k-1} \\ v_{k-1} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} (2) $$ Expanding: $$ \hat{p}_{k \mid k-1} = p_{k-1} + 0.1 v_{k-1} + 0.01 $$ $$ \hat{v}_{k \mid k-1} = v_{k-1} + 0.2 $$ ### Step 2 — Predicted Covariance $$ P_{k \mid k-1} = A P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A^T + Q $$ Let initial $P_{k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$. $$ A P_{k-1} A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.05 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.005 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ P_{k \mid k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.015 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.55 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 3 — Innovation (Measurement Residual) Measurement model: $z_k = H x_k + v_k$, with $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (position-only GPS), $R = 4.0$ (m²). Innovation: $\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = z_k - \hat{p}_{k \mid k-1}$. ### Step 4 — Kalman Gain $$ S = H P_{k \mid k-1} H^T + R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.015 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.55 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 4 = 1.015 + 4 = 5.015 $$ $$ K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^T S^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.015 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{5.015} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2024 \\ 0.00997 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 5 — Updated State Estimate $$ \hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k \tilde{y}_k $$ ### Step 6 — Updated Covariance (Joseph form omitted for brevity) $$ P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \mid k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.2024 & 0 \\ -0.00997 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.015 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.55 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8096 & 0.0399 \\ -0.00997 & 0.5500 \end{bmatrix} $$ > The position uncertainty dropped from **1.015 m² → 0.8096 m²**, showing that measurement fusion **always reduces uncertainty** in a Kalman Filter. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-to-Collision (TTC) and AEB Decision Boundary **Scenario:** Ego car at $v_e = 90$ km/h, stationary lead vehicle 50 m ahead. Driver reaction time $t_r = 1.5$ s, friction $\mu = 0.7$. ### Step 1 — Unit Conversion $$ v_e = 90 \; \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 25 \; \text{m/s} $$ ### Step 2 — Required Stopping Distance (kinematics) $$ d_{stop} = v_e \cdot t_r + \frac{v_e^2}{2 \mu g} $$ $$ d_{stop} = (25)(1.5) + \frac{(25)^2}{2 (0.7)(9.81)} = 37.5 + \frac{625}{13.734} = 37.5 + 45.51 = 83.01 \; \text{m} $$ ### Step 3 — Critical Distance Trigger Since $d_{stop} = 83.01$ m > 50 m, **collision is unavoidable by braking alone**; AEB must trigger at least $83$ m away, so an *advance warning* is necessary. Modern AEB uses **TTC** not distance. ### Step 4 — TTC Computation (Stationary Lead, So $v_{rel} = v_e$) $$ TTC = \frac{d}{v_{rel}} = \frac{50}{25} = 2.0 \; \text{s} $$ ### Step 5 — Brake Trigger Threshold Euro-NCAP AEB protocol: brake pre-fill at $TTC = 2.7$ s, full brake at $TTC = 1.5$ s. At $TTC = 2.0$ s the system must be in **full emergency braking**, with deceleration $\ge 6$ m/s². ### Step 6 — Deceleration Achieved by AEB $$ a_{req} = \frac{v_e^2}{2 (d - v_e t_r)} = \frac{625}{2 (50 - 37.5)} = \frac{625}{25} = 25 \; \text{m/s}^2 $$ This is physically impossible ($> g$), confirming that **driver alert must precede AEB** in such a scenario — illustrating the **multi-stage intelligent intervention architecture**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords) and clean uppercase text inside quoted labels (no markdown bold/italics). ## 4.1 Master Architecture of an Intelligent Vehicle System ```mermaid flowchart TB A1["EXTROCEPTIVE SENSORS"] --> B1["PERCEPTION LAYER"] A2["CAMERA 30 TO 60 FPS"] --> B1 A3["RADAR 77 GHZ"] --> B1 A4["LIDAR 905 NM"] --> B1 A5["ULTRASONIC 40 KHZ"] --> B1 B1 --> C1["SENSOR FUSION ENGINE"] C1 --> C2["OBJECT DETECTION AND TRACKING"] C2 --> C3["LOCALIZATION GNSS PLUS IMU PLUS HD MAP"] C3 --> D1["PREDICTION MODULE LSTM TRANSFORMER"] D1 --> D2["BEHAVIOR PLANNER"] D2 --> D3["MOTION PLANNER RRT STAR OR POLYNOMIAL"] D3 --> E1["CONTROL LAYER PID OR MPC"] E1 --> E2["STEER BY WIRE"] E1 --> E3["BRAKE BY WIRE"] E1 --> E4["THROTTLE BY WIRE"] E2 --> F1["VEHICLE DYNAMICS"] E3 --> F1 E4 --> F1 F1 --> G1["HMI DIGITAL CLUSTER HUD VOICE"] F1 --> G2["V2X MODULE 5G DSRC"] F1 --> G3["TELEMATICS CLOUD OTA"] H1["FUNCTIONAL SAFETY ISO 26262 ASIL D"] -. monitors .-> C1 H1 -. monitors .-> D2 H1 -. monitors .-> E1 H2["SOTIF ISO 21448"] -. monitors .-> B1 H2 -. monitors .-> C1 ``` ## 4.2 V2X Communication Ecosystem (Vehicle-to-Everything) ```mermaid flowchart LR V1["EGO VEHICLE OBU"] <--> V2["OTHER VEHICLES V2V"] V1 <--> V3["INFRASTRUCTURE V2I TRAFFIC LIGHT RSU"] V1 <--> V4["PEDESTRIAN V2P PHONE APP"] V1 <--> V5["NETWORK V2N CLOUD"] V1 <--> V6["GRID V2G EV CHARGING"] V2 --> MSG1["COOPERATIVE AWARENESS MESSAGE CAM 10 HZ"] V3 --> MSG2["SIGNAL PHASE AND TIMING SPAT 2 HZ"] V4 --> MSG3["VULNERABLE ROAD USER VRU MSG"] V5 --> MSG4["HD MAP UPDATE OTA DELTA"] V6 --> MSG5["CHARGING SCHEDULE AND PRICE"] V1 --> APPS["APPLICATIONS: AEB GLOSA EFFICIENCY PARKING ECO ROUTING"] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Data Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["CAMERA RAW FRAME"] --> P1["YOLO OBJECT DETECTOR"] S2["RADAR TRACK LIST"] --> P2["KALMAN TRACK UPDATE"] S3["LIDAR POINT CLOUD"] --> P3["POINTPILLARS SEGMENTATION"] P1 --> F1["PROBABILISTIC FUSION BAYESIAN ENGINE"] P2 --> F1 P3 --> F1 F1 --> T1["MULTI OBJECT TRACKER HUNGARIAN ASSIGNMENT"] T1 --> T2["OUTPUT FUSED OBJECT LIST WITH CLASS POSITION VELOCITY AND CONFIDENCE"] T2 --> DOWN["DOWNSTREAM PLANNING AND CONTROL"] F1 -. feedback .-> P1 F1 -. feedback .-> P2 F1 -. feedback .-> P3 ``` ## 4.4 Autonomous Driving Decision Pipeline (Sense → Plan → Act) ```mermaid flowchart TB RAW["RAW SENSOR DATA"] --> PER["PERCEPTION"] PER --> LOC["LOCALIZATION"] LOC --> PRED["PREDICTION"] PRED --> PLAN["BEHAVIORAL AND MOTION PLANNING"] PLAN --> CTRL["LOW LEVEL VEHICLE CONTROL"] CTRL --> ACT["ACTUATOR COMMANDS"] ACT --> FB["VEHICLE DYNAMICS RESPONSE"] FB --> RAW MON["SAFETY MONITOR ISO 26262"] -.-> CTRL MON -.-> PLAN MIN["MINIMAL RISK MANEUVER MRM"] -.-> ACT HMI["DRIVER HMI ENGAGEMENT REQUEST"] -.-> PLAN ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Module 4):** Students commonly lose marks because they (a) confuse **SAE Level 2 and Level 3** (Level 3 is the first to monitor the environment itself, not the driver), (b) write only definitions without *quantitative example* (TTCs, latencies), (c) skip the **standard protocol** (ISO 26262, SAE J3016, IEEE 802.11p), and (d) draw diagrams that mix up **ADAS (driver-assist)** with **AD (automated driving)**. A 14-mark answer **must contain a block diagram + at least one numerical example** to score above 10. --- ## Part A — 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]` > **Define an Intelligent Transportation System (ITS). List any four application areas where ITS is implemented in modern vehicles.** **Model Answer (CO4, Bloom L1–L2):** > [!NOTE] > **Intelligent Transportation System (ITS):** A system that integrates advanced information, communication, sensor, and control technologies (wired/wireless) into the transportation infrastructure and vehicles to improve safety, efficiency, and sustainability. **Four application areas:** 1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — radar-based longitudinal control. 2. **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** — camera-based lateral control. 3. **V2I traffic-light priority** — GLOSA (Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory). 4. **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** — sensor-fused collision avoidance. 5. *(Any other valid: eCall, fleet telematics, parking assist, etc.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]` > **Distinguish between SAE Level 2 and SAE Level 3 driving automation. Give one commercial example of each.** **Model Answer (CO4, Bloom L4 — Analyze):** | Feature | Level 2 (Partial) | Level 3 (Conditional) | |---|---|---| | **Steering + Acceleration** | System | System | | **Environment Monitoring** | **Human driver** | **System** | | **Fallback** | Human driver | Human driver (on request) | | **Engagement** | Continuous | Within ODD only | | **Commercial Example** | Tesla Autopilot (basic), Honda Sensing | Mercedes Drive Pilot (Germany, 2022, up to 60 km/h) | > At Level 2 the driver is legally and operationally responsible at all times. At Level 3 the system is responsible *within its ODD* and may issue a takeover request. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > The KTU pattern allows the student to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (full 14 marks). Both are provided with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the four-layer architecture of an intelligent vehicle system. Briefly explain the role of the perception, decision, and actuation layers.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *A vehicle is approaching a stationary lead object at 90 km/h. The initial separation is 50 m. The road is dry with friction coefficient μ = 0.7. The driver reaction time is 1.5 s. Determine (i) the stopping distance required, (ii) the time-to-collision (TTC), and (iii) comment on whether AEB alone can prevent a collision. Use g = 9.81 m/s².* **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Layer Explanation** Refer to **Section 4.1 (Master Architecture)** of these notes. The student must draw (or describe in detail): - **Perception Layer** — cameras, radar, LiDAR, ultrasonic. Role: convert raw physical signals (light, RF, sound) into structured information (object lists, lane markings, free-space maps). - **Decision Layer** — sensor fusion + behavioural/motion planning. Role: estimate the world state, predict other agents, and generate a safe, comfortable trajectory. - **Actuation Layer** — drive-by-wire modules + low-level controllers (PID, MPC). Role: track the planned trajectory with appropriate closed-loop bandwidth (steering ≥ 5 Hz, brake/throttle ≥ 20 Hz). - **Connectivity / HMI Layer** — V2X, telematics, digital cluster, HUD. **(b) Numerical Solution** *Step 1 — Unit conversion:* $$ v = 90 \; \tfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = 25 \; \text{m/s} $$ *Step 2 — Stopping distance:* $$ d_{stop} = v t_r + \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g} = 25(1.5) + \frac{625}{2(0.7)(9.81)} = 37.5 + 45.51 = 83.01 \; \text{m} $$ *Step 3 — TTC:* $$ TTC = \frac{d}{v_{rel}} = \frac{50}{25} = 2.0 \; \text{s} $$ *Step 4 — Inference:* Required stopping distance (83.01 m) > available gap (50 m). AEB must trigger before driver reaction time. Required deceleration *if* AEB starts at 50 m and after the 1.5 s reaction lag (i.e., from 12.5 m): $$ a_{req} = \frac{v^2}{2(12.5)} = \frac{625}{25} = 25 \; \text{m/s}^2 > g $$ This is **physically infeasible**, so AEB **alone cannot prevent collision** unless preceded by a **forward-collision warning (FCW)** issued well before the 50 m mark (e.g., at 100 m). Conclusion: AEB must be **coupled with predictive FCW** for full effectiveness. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain multi-sensor fusion with a Bayesian approach. Two independent sensors (Camera and Radar) report the presence of a pedestrian. Camera: P(E_C|H) = 0.92, P(E_C|H̄) = 0.06. Radar: P(E_R|H) = 0.85, P(E_R|H̄) = 0.10. Prior P(H) = 0.08. Compute the fused posterior P(H | E_C ∩ E_R).* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Describe the V2X communication paradigm. With a neat block diagram, differentiate between V2V, V2I, V2N, and V2P. State two international standards used for V2X.* **Model Solution:** **(a) Bayesian Sensor Fusion** *Step 1 — Camera-only posterior:* $$ P(E_C) = P(E_C \mid H) P(H) + P(E_C \mid \overline{H}) P(\overline{H}) = (0.92)(0.08) + (0.06)(0.92) = 0.0736 + 0.0552 = 0.1288 $$ $$ P(H \mid E_C) = \frac{(0.92)(0.08)}{0.1288} = \frac{0.0736}{0.1288} \approx 0.5714 $$ *Step 2 — Joint likelihood (conditional independence):* $$ P(E_C \cap E_R \mid H) = (0.92)(0.85) = 0.7820 $$ $$ P(E_C \cap E_R \mid \overline{H}) = (0.06)(0.10) = 0.0060 $$ *Step 3 — Denominator (total probability):* $$ P(E_C \cap E_R) = (0.7820)(0.08) + (0.0060)(0.92) = 0.0626 + 0.00552 = 0.06811 $$ *Step 4 — Fused posterior:* $$ P(H \mid E_C \cap E_R) = \frac{(0.7820)(0.08)}{0.06811} = \frac{0.06256}{0.06811} \approx 0.9185 $$ *Step 5 — Conclusion:* Fused posterior (**91.85 %**) is **substantially higher** than camera-only posterior (**57.14 %**) — quantitative proof that multi-sensor fusion improves pedestrian-detection confidence to the typical AEB threshold of 90 %. **(b) V2X Communication Paradigm** **Definitions:** - **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Cars exchange CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message) at 10 Hz; typical latency < 20 ms. - **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** RSU (Road-Side Unit) broadcasts SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing), MAP, and IVI (In-Vehicle Information) messages. - **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Long-range 4G/5G link to cloud for HD map updates, OTA, telematics. - **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian):** Smartphone/VRU device emits BLE/5G Sidelink beacon. **Two International Standards (mandatory to write):** 1. **IEEE 802.11p / ETSI ITS-G5 (DSRC)** — dedicated short-range communications at 5.9 GHz. 2. **3GPP C-V2X (Release 14, Mode 4 sidelink)** — cellular-V2X, evolution to 5G-NR-V2X (Release 16+). **Block diagram** — refer to **Section 4.2 (V2X Ecosystem)** of these notes. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this checklist in the last 10 minutes before the KTU exam.** - [ ] **SAE J3016 levels** — memorize the **monitoring of environment** column. Level 3 is the first where the *system* monitors, not the human. - [ ] **Functional safety = ISO 26262**, **SOTIF = ISO 21448**. Both are mandatory citations in any 14-mark answer on intelligent systems. - [ ] **Four-layer architecture** — Perception → Decision → Actuation → HMI/Connectivity. This is a *guaranteed 7-mark* sub-part. - [ ] **TTC formula** — $TTC = d_{rel}/v_{rel}$. Euro-NCAP AEB thresholds: **2.7 s warning, 1.5 s full brake.** - [ ] **Stopping distance** — $d_{stop} = v t_r + v^2 / (2 \mu g)$. Always state $g = 9.81$ m/s². - [ ] **Bayesian fusion** — always quote the **conditional independence assumption** before multiplying likelihoods. - [ ] **Kalman filter** — two-step cycle: **predict (A, Q) → update (H, R, K)**. Be ready to write the five equations. - [ ] **Sensor range** — radar equation with $\lambda^2$, LiDAR uses $d = c \Delta t / 2$ (round trip), camera uses pinhole $x = f X / Z$. - [ ] **V2X** — know **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** vs **C-V2X (3GPP)**. Mention the **5.9 GHz** band. - [ ] **Latency budget** — safety-critical loop must be **< 100 ms**. AEB actuation is the tightest constraint. - [ ] **Ackermann geometry** — inner wheel steers *more* than outer wheel; this is the *only* geometry that avoids tyre scrub. - [ ] **Standards to quote** for full credit — **SAE J3016, ISO 26262, ISO 21448, IEEE 802.11p, 3GPP C-V2X, UNECE R157 (ALKS)**. - [ ] **Diagram always helps** — even a hand-drawn block diagram of the perception-decision-actuation chain adds 2–3 marks in KTU valuation. - [ ] **Numerical examples** — at least one calculation (TTC, stopping distance, Bayesian posterior, Kalman gain) is **mandatory** for a 14-mark answer to score above 10. - [ ] **Avoid these words alone**: "AI drives the car" → write "a *deterministic model-predictive controller* with *learned perception* via a *convolutional neural network*" — KTU rewards technical specificity.

topics.title: Communication Technologies in Vehicle Information System - Vision for ITS Communication and ITS Communication in Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Communication Technologies in Vehicle Information System - Vision for ITS Communication and ITS Communication in Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** is the integrated application of information, communication, sensor, electronics, and control technologies to surface transportation systems, with the goal of improving safety, mobility, efficiency, and environmental sustainability across road, rail, and transit networks.

**ITS Communication** refers to the layered exchange of digital information between vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, and networks using standardised wireless and wired protocols, enabling cooperative perception, cooperative driving, and autonomous decision-making.

## 1.2 Vision for ITS Communication

The **Vision for ITS Communication** is the long-term architectural blueprint that specifies **who communicates with whom, what data is exchanged, how it is exchanged, and why**, across heterogeneous transport entities. It is rooted in the global **Connected Vehicle** paradigm and the **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** ecosystem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillars of the ITS Vision (KTU Board-Exam Highlight)**
> - **Safety** — collision avoidance, hazard alerts, emergency vehicle prioritisation.
> - **Mobility** — dynamic routing, platooning, signal phase & timing (SPaT) advisory.
> - **Efficiency** — fuel economy, reduced congestion, freight optimisation.
> - **Environment** — eco-driving, emissions reduction, electric vehicle smart charging.
> - **Infotainment & Comfort** — OTA updates, in-vehicle media, telematics.

## 1.3 ITS Communication in Automobiles

In the **automotive context**, ITS communication is delivered through **two complementary layers**:

| Layer | Scope | Representative Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **In-Vehicle Network (IVN)** | Inside a single vehicle — sensors → ECUs → actuators | CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet, MOST |
| **Inter-Vehicle / V2X Network** | Between vehicles and external entities | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p), C-V2X (3GPP LTE/5G), 5G NR-V2X |

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Convoy of Coffee Shops" 🍵

Imagine a **convoy of coffee shops** travelling down a long highway:

- **In-Vehicle Communication** is like the **internal walkie-talkies** inside each café — the cashier, barista, and manager constantly chatter about milk temperature, order queues, and machine status (this is *CAN bus chatter*).
- **ITS / V2X Communication** is like **mobile phones between adjacent cafés** in the convoy — "Hey, I'm braking up ahead!", "There's a traffic jam 2 km down the road", "The toll plaza is now open at Lane 3" — i.e. *cooperative awareness* across the fleet.

Without the walkie-talkies, the café breaks down internally. Without the mobile phones, the convoy crashes. Both layers are **mandatory** for modern intelligent transportation.

---

## 1.5 Communication Entity Classification (KTU Tagged)

> [!NOTE]
> **V2X Taxonomy — Must Memorise for 2-Mark Definitions**
> - **V2V** — Vehicle-to-Vehicle (e.g., collision warning)
> - **V2I** — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (e.g., traffic light advisory)
> - **V2P** — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (e.g., phone alerts pedestrian of approaching car)
> - **V2N** — Vehicle-to-Network (e.g., cloud-based HD map download)
> - **V2D** — Vehicle-to-Device (e.g., diagnostic dongle)
> - **V2G** — Vehicle-to-Grid (e.g., EV bidirectional charging)

The **ITS Communication Reference Architecture** is formalised by **ISO 14813 (Reference Architecture)** and **ETSI EN 302 637 (CAM/DENM standards)**. The vision aligns with **SAE J3061 (Cybersecurity)** and **ISO 26262 (Functional Safety)**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of ITS Communication

The ITS communication stack is universally modelled as a **5-layer cooperative system** (mapped to OSI):

### Layer 1 — Sensing / Perception Layer
- Onboard sensors (RADAR, LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, IMU).
- Generates raw data; throughput in the **Mbps range**.

### Layer 2 — In-Vehicle Network (IVN) Layer
- High-speed backbone for ECU ↔ ECU exchange.
- Modern vehicles use **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1)** at **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps**.

### Layer 3 — Wireless Access Layer
- **DSRC**: 75 MHz spectrum around **5.85–5.925 GHz**, 10 MHz channels.
- **C-V2X**: 3GPP Release 14/15/16 (LTE-V2X, 5G NR-V2X) on **5.9 GHz** plus cellular bands.

### Layer 4 — Cooperative Awareness Layer
- Standards: **ETSI EN 302 637-2 CAM** (Cooperative Awareness Messages) — broadcast at **1–10 Hz**.
- **ETSI EN 302 637-3 DENM** (Decentralised Environmental Notification Message) — event-triggered.

### Layer 5 — Application Layer
- Forward Collision Warning (FCW), Emergency Electronic Brake Light (EEBL), Left Turn Assist (LTA), Intersection Movement Assist (IMA), Platooning, GLOSA (Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory).

---

## 2.2 Vision for ITS Communication — Driving Forces

| Driver | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| Reduction of road fatalities | WHO reports ~1.19 million annual deaths globally; V2X targets >40% reduction |
| Congestion mitigation | USDOT estimates $160B annual US congestion cost; V2I reduces stops by ~20% |
| Autonomous driving enabler | SAE Level 3+ requires cooperative perception beyond line-of-sight |
| Electrification synergy | V2G enables grid balancing via EV fleets |
| Standardisation | 3GPP, ETSI TC-ITS, SAE J2945, IEEE 1609 |

---

## 2.3 ITS Communication Technologies — Comparative Theory

### 2.3.1 DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)
- Built on **IEEE 802.11p** (a 2010 amendment to Wi-Fi) optimised for vehicular channels.
- Physical layer: **OFDM** with 64 subcarriers, BPSK/QPSK/16-QAM/64-QAM.
- MAC layer: CSMA/CA with Enhanced Distributed Channel Access (EDCA) and **4 Access Categories (AC_BK, AC_BE, AC_VI, AC_VO)** to prioritise safety packets.
- **SAE J2945** governs message sets.

### 2.3.2 C-V2X (Cellular V2X)
- Defined by **3GPP Releases 14 (LTE-V2X), 15 (LTE-eV2X), 16 (NR-V2X)**.
- Two operational modes:
  - **PC5 interface (sidelink)** — direct V2V/V2I, no cellular required.
  - **Uu interface** — uplink/downlink via base station (network coverage needed).
- Supports **5G NR-V2X** with **URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication) — target **1 ms user-plane latency** and **99.999% reliability**.

### 2.3.3 In-Vehicle Networks (IVN)
- **CAN (Controller Area Network)**: ISO 11898, classic CAN 2.0 up to **1 Mbps**, CAN FD up to **5 Mbps**.
- **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)**: ISO 17987, up to **19.2 kbps**, single-wire, used for low-speed body electronics.
- **FlexRay**: ISO 17458, **10 Mbps**, time-triggered TDMA, used in x-by-wire chassis.
- **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)**: IEEE 802.3bw/bp, full-duplex, **100 Mbps–1 Gbps**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical question on this module.**

### Table 2.1 — Core Equations for ITS Communication

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PL_{dB}(d) = PL_{dB}(d_0) + 10n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | Log-distance path loss model | dB |
| 2 | $PL_{FS}(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | Free-space path loss (Friis) | dB |
| 3 | $SNR = \dfrac{P_r}{N_0 \cdot B}$ | Receiver signal-to-noise ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL(d)$ | Received power (link budget) | dBm |
| 5 | $N_0 = k_B \cdot T$ | Thermal noise spectral density | W/Hz |
| 6 | $N = N_0 \cdot B$ | Total noise power in bandwidth B | W |
| 7 | $T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d}{c}$ | End-to-end packet latency | seconds |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{B \cdot T_s}$ | Spectral efficiency (M-QAM) | bits/s/Hz |
| 9 | $BER_{BPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Bit error rate for BPSK | dimensionless |
| 10 | $BER_{QPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Bit error rate for QPSK | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + SNR\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/s |
| 12 | $f_{d,max} = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c} \cdot \cos\theta$ | Maximum Doppler shift | Hz |
| 13 | $T_{coh} = \dfrac{1}{2 f_{d,max}}$ | Channel coherence time | seconds |
| 14 | $T_s = \dfrac{c}{v} \cdot \tau_{rms}$ | RMS delay spread symbol period | seconds |

**Constants Used**
- $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (Boltzmann constant)
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** (speed of light)
- $T_0 = 290$ **K** (standard reference temperature)
- $N_0 \approx -174$ **dBm/Hz** at 290 K

### Table 2.2 — Communication Technology Parameter Reference

| Parameter | DSRC (802.11p) | LTE-V2X | 5G NR-V2X | CAN | Automotive Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | **5.85–5.925 GHz** | 5.9 GHz / cellular | 5.9 GHz / FR1 | Wired | Wired |
| Channel BW | 10 MHz / 20 MHz | 10 MHz / 20 MHz | up to 100 MHz | N/A | N/A |
| Data Rate | 3–27 Mbps | up to 150 Mbps | up to 1 Gbps | 1–5 Mbps | 100–1000 Mbps |
| Range | ~300 m | ~500 m | ~500–1000 m | 40 m bus | 15 m cable |
| Latency | < 10 ms | < 20 ms | < 5 ms (URLLC) | < 1 ms | < 1 ms |
| Standard | IEEE 802.11p / SAE J2945 | 3GPP Rel-14 | 3GPP Rel-16 | ISO 11898 | IEEE 802.3bw/bp |

### Table 2.3 — Path Loss Exponent (n) Values

| Environment | Path Loss Exponent $n$ |
|---|---|
| Free space | **2.0** |
| Urban macrocell | 2.7 – 3.5 |
| Urban microcell | 2.5 – 3.0 |
| Highway LOS | 2.0 – 2.5 |
| Vehicular tunnel / NLOS | 3.5 – 5.0 |
| Inside parking garage | 4.0 – 6.0 |
