## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Framework

- **Traffic Engineers** use path-loss exponents to plan **RSU (Roadside Unit)** deployment density (typically every 300–500 m on urban corridors).
- **OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) Designers** select IVN protocol based on **safety integrity level (ASIL)** — e.g., FlexRay/Ethernet for ASIL-D braking; LIN for ASIL-A mirror adjust.
- **Smart-City Planners** leverage CAM throughput to compute cooperative awareness radius and congestion cost.
- **Autonomy Stack Engineers** consume V2X data via the **Autoware / Apollo** modular pipeline alongside LIDAR/camera perception.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Log-Distance Path Loss for V2V Link

A V2V transmitter (Tx) on **vehicle A** communicates with a receiver (Rx) on **vehicle B** at a horizontal separation of $d$ metres in a suburban environment.

**Given:**
- Frequency $f_c = 5.9$ **GHz**
- Reference distance $d_0 = 1$ **m**
- Path loss exponent $n = 2.7$ (suburban NLOS)
- Shadow fading standard deviation $\sigma = 6$ **dB**
- Transmit power $P_t = 20$ **dBm**
- Tx/Rx antenna gain $G_t = G_r = 3$ **dBi**

**Step 1 — Compute free-space path loss at reference distance.**

$$PL_{FS}(d_0) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d_0 f_c}{c}\right)$$

$$PL_{FS}(1\,\text{m}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 1 \cdot 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right)$$

$$= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{7.412 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(247.07)$$

$$= 20 \times 2.3929 = 47.86 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply log-distance extension at $d = 100$ m.**

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

$$PL(100\,\text{m}) = 47.86 + 10(2.7)\log_{10}(100) + X_\sigma$$

$$= 47.86 + 27 \cdot \log_{10}(10^2) + X_\sigma$$

$$= 47.86 + 27 \cdot 2 + X_\sigma = 47.86 + 54 + X_\sigma = 101.86 + X_\sigma \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Average received power computation.**

$$\overline{P_r} = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL(d)$$

$$= 20 + 3 + 3 - 101.86 = -75.86 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 4 — Fade margin calculation (for 99.9% reliability, $X_\sigma = 3.09\sigma$).**

$$\text{Fade margin} = 3.09 \times 6 = 18.54 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum received power for $P_{outage} = 0.1\%$.**

$$P_{r,min} = \overline{P_r} - 18.54 = -75.86 - 18.54 = -94.40 \text{ dBm}$$

This is the **link-budget floor** below which the V2V message cannot be decoded with **QPSK modulation at BER $\leq 10^{-5}$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity of 5G NR-V2X Channel

**Given:**
- Channel bandwidth $B = 20$ **MHz**
- Receiver SNR $= 18$ **dB** $= 63.10$ (linear)

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear.**

$$SNR_{\text{lin}} = 10^{(SNR_{dB}/10)} = 10^{1.8} = 63.10$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)$$

$$C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 63.10)$$

$$= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(64.10)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate logarithm.**

$$\log_2(64.10) = \frac{\ln(64.10)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.159}{0.693} = 6.001 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Final capacity.**

$$C = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 6.001 = 1.2002 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits/s} \approx 120.0 \text{ Mbps}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Latency for V2X Safety Message

**Given:**
- Processing delay $T_{proc} = 1.5$ **ms**
- Queuing delay $T_{queue} = 0.5$ **ms**
- Packet length $L = 400$ **bytes** $= 3200$ **bits**
- Transmission rate $R = 6$ **Mbps** (802.11p, 64-QAM 3/4)
- Distance $d = 150$ **m**

**Step 1 — Compute transmission delay.**

$$T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{3200}{6 \times 10^{6}} = 5.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.533 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute propagation delay.**

$$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{150}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.0005 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all latency components.**

$$T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} + T_{prop}$$

$$= 1.5 + 0.5 + 0.533 + 0.0005 = 2.534 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with safety threshold.**

The EU ETSI standard for **CAM** requires $T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} \leq 100$ **ms**. Our computed latency of **2.534 ms** is well within budget, leaving **97.466 ms** slack for retransmission.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Doppler Spread on Highway at 120 km/h

**Given:**
- Vehicle speed $v = 120$ **km/h** $= 33.33$ **m/s**
- Carrier frequency $f_c = 5.9$ **GHz**
- Angle of arrival $\theta = 0°$ (head-on approaching)

**Step 1 — Maximum Doppler shift.**

$$f_{d,max} = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c} \cdot \cos\theta = \frac{33.33 \times 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \cdot 1$$

$$= \frac{1.966 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 655.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Coherence time.**

$$T_{coh} = \frac{1}{2 f_{d,max}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 655.4} = 7.63 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.763 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — OFDM symbol duration requirement.**

For a channel to appear **slow-fading within one OFDM symbol**, the symbol duration $T_s \leq T_{coh}$. IEEE 802.11p uses $T_s = 8\,\mu s$ (with cyclic prefix), which satisfies the slow-fading criterion easily.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ITS Communication — Layered Reference Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Application Layer: FCW, EEBL, GLOSA, Platooning] --> A2[Message Layer: CAM, DENM, SPaT, MAP]
    A2 --> A3[Facilities Layer: SAE J3061, ETSI EN 302 637]
    A3 --> A4[Network and Transport Layer: IPv6, GeoNetworking, BTP]
    A4 --> A5[Access Layer: 802.11p OR C-V2X PC5 OR 5G NR-Uu]
    A5 --> A6[Physical Layer: 5.9 GHz OFDM]
    A6 --> A7[Sensor Perception: LIDAR, RADAR, Camera, IMU]
```

> **Block-Level Interpretation**: The architecture is a top-down stack. The *Application Layer* consumes pre-processed messages; the *Access Layer* toggles between DSRC and C-V2X; the *Physical Layer* operates on the 5.9 GHz band. The bottommost block represents on-board sensors that **feed cooperative awareness** into the stack.

---

## 4.2 V2X Entity Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1[Vehicle A] -- V2V --> V2[Vehicle B]
    V1 -- V2I --> R1[RSU Traffic Signal]
    V1 -- V2N --> C1[Cloud Server HD Map]
    V1 -- V2P --> P1[Smartphone Pedestrian]
    V1 -- V2G --> G1[Smart Grid Charging Station]
    R1 -- I2I --> R2[Central Traffic Management]
    C1 -- OTA --> V2
```

> **Block-Level Interpretation**: Vehicle A acts as a **multi-domain hub**, simultaneously exchanging data with peers (V2V), roadside units (V2I), the cloud (V2N), vulnerable road users (V2P), and the power grid (V2G). RSUs in turn communicate with central traffic management (I2I) for closed-loop adaptive signal control.

---

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PowertrainDomain
        ECU1[Engine Control Unit]
        ECU2[Transmission ECU]
        ECU3[Battery Management ECU]
    end

    end

    end

```

> **Block-Level Interpretation**: Each domain uses the IVN protocol that matches its **safety integrity and bandwidth** requirement. ADAS/Infotainment share **Automotive Ethernet at 1 Gbps** for high-throughput sensor data; chassis uses **FlexRay** for time-deterministic x-by-wire control; powertrain uses **CAN FD**; body electronics fall back to **LIN** for cost-sensitive devices.

---

## 4.4 ITS Communication Flow — Forward Collision Warning Use-Case

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sensor Acquires Range and Relative Velocity] --> S2[ADAS ECU Computes TTC Time to Collision]
    S2 --> S3{Is TTC less than 4 seconds?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[No Action Continue Cruise]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Trigger CAM Cooperative Awareness Message]
    S5 --> S6[802.11p or C-V2X PC5 Transmission]
    S6 --> S7[Neighbour Vehicle RSU Receives]
    S7 --> S8[Decode Validate CAM]
    S8 --> S9[Driver HMI Warning Visual plus Haptic]
    S8 --> S10[Automatic Emergency Braking AEB Activated]
```

> **Block-Level Interpretation**: The decision diamond is the **threshold gate** (TTC ≤ 4 s). If triggered, a CAM is generated and broadcast over either 802.11p or C-V2X sidelink (PC5). Both the human driver (via HMI) and the autonomous AEB subsystem react in parallel.

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Option A

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message)** and **DENM (Decentralised Environmental Notification Message)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **log-distance path loss** for a V2V link at 5.9 GHz in an urban environment, given reference distance $d_0 = 1$ m, path loss exponent $n = 3.0$, transmit power $P_t = 23$ dBm, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and required receiver sensitivity $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm. Determine the **maximum reliable communication range** assuming a 6 dB shadow fade margin. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution B-A(a) — V2X Architecture and CAM/DENM (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of V2X (1 Mark)**

V2X is a vehicular communication paradigm enabling a vehicle to exchange information with **other vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), pedestrians (V2P), and networks (V2N)**, using either **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** or **C-V2X (3GPP LTE/5G)** as the underlying radio access technology.

**Step 2 — Architecture Diagram (2 Marks)**

```
       ┌──────────────┐
       │ Cloud / HD   │ ◄── V2N
       │   Maps       │
       └──────────────┘
              ▲
              │
┌───────┐  V2V  ┌───────┐  V2I  ┌──────┐
│Veh A  │ ◄────►│Veh B  │ ◄────►│ RSU  │
└───────┘       └───────┘       └──────┘
    ▲                              ▲
    │ V2P                          │
    ▼                              │
┌──────────┐                       │
│Ped Phone │ ◄──── I2I ────────────┘
└──────────┘
```

**Step 3 — Role of CAM (2 Marks)**

- **CAM** = Cooperative Awareness Message, standardised under **ETSI EN 302 637-2**.
- Periodically broadcast (1–10 Hz) carrying: position (GPS), speed, heading, acceleration, vehicle dimensions, and sensor status.
- Enables **neighbouring vehicles** to build a **local dynamic map (LDM)** and predict trajectories.
- Latency target: ≤ 100 ms.

**Step 4 — Role of DENM (2 Marks)**

- **DENM** = Decentralised Environmental Notification Message, standardised under **ETSI EN 302 637-3**.
- **Event-triggered** (e.g., hard braking, slippery road, stationary vehicle).
- Sent **multi-hop** via GeoNetworking to alert vehicles within a geographic relevance area.
- Latency target: ≤ 50 ms for safety-critical events.

---

### Solution B-A(b) — Maximum V2V Range (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute free-space path loss at $d_0 = 1$ m (1 Mark).**

$$PL_{FS}(1) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 1 \cdot 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(247.07) = 47.86 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply log-distance model (1 Mark).**

$$PL(d) = 47.86 + 10(3.0)\log_{10}(d) + X_\sigma$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum allowable path loss (1 Mark).**

$$PL_{max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{r,min} - \text{Fade Margin}$$

$$= 23 + 2 + 2 - (-90) - 6 = 111 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for maximum range $d_{max}$ (2 Marks).**

$$111 = 47.86 + 30 \log_{10}(d_{max})$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{max}) = \frac{111 - 47.86}{30} = \frac{63.14}{30} = 2.105$$

$$d_{max} = 10^{2.105} = 127.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (2 Marks).**

The maximum reliable V2V range is **127 m**, which is below the 300 m target for highway cooperative awareness. **Remedies:**
- Use higher-gain directional antennas (e.g., 8 dBi patch).
- Reduce shadow fade margin by deploying relays or RSUs.
- Use C-V2X PC5 with retransmissions to recover faded packets.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **in-vehicle network architecture** of a modern automotive electronics system. Compare **CAN, LIN, and Automotive Ethernet** in terms of data rate, typical application, and bus arbitration mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A V2X system uses **BPSK modulation** over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ **dB**. Compute:
  (i) The **bit error rate (BER)**.

### Solution B-B(a) — In-Vehicle Network Architecture (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 Marks)**

```
[Powertrain] ─┐
[Chassis]    ─┼─► CAN FD Bus ─┐
[Body]       ─┘                │
                              ▼
                       Gateway ECU
                              ▼
                    Automotive Ethernet 1 Gbps
                              ▼
       [ADAS] [Infotainment] [V2X Modem] [Telematics]
                              ▼
                          Cloud / OTA
```

**Step 2 — Comparison Table (3 Marks)**

| Parameter | CAN | LIN | Automotive Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard | ISO 11898 | ISO 17987 | IEEE 802.3bw/bp |
| Data Rate | 1 Mbps (Classic); 5 Mbps (FD) | up to 19.2 kbps | 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps |
| Arbitration | CSMA/CR — non-destructive bitwise | Master-Slave polling | Full-duplex switch-based, no arbitration |
| Typical App | Engine, ABS, airbag | Mirror, seat, window | ADAS, infotainment, V2X backbone |

**Step 3 — Engineering Justification (1 Mark)**

The choice of IVN depends on **bandwidth-latency-safety trade-off**: CAN for cost-sensitive control; LIN for low-speed body; Ethernet for sensor-rich ADAS domains.

---

### Solution B-B(b) — BER and Doppler Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert $E_b/N_0$ to linear (1 Mark).**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0}\bigg|_{lin} = 10^{9.6/10} = 10^{0.96} = 9.12$$

**Step 2 — BPSK BER formula (1 Mark).**

$$BER_{BPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute argument of Q-function (1 Mark).**

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.12} = \sqrt{18.24} = 4.27$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Q-function (2 Marks).**

$$BER = Q(4.27) \approx 9.8 \times 10^{-6} \approx 10^{-5}$$

This satisfies the **ETSI CAM reliability requirement of $BER \leq 10^{-5}$.**

**Step 5 — Coherence time (1 Mark).**

$$T_{coh} = \frac{1}{2 f_d} = \frac{1}{2 \times 547} = 9.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 914 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 6 — Slow-fading check (1 Mark).**

Since OFDM symbol $T_s = 8\,\mu s \ll T_{coh} = 914\,\mu s$, the channel is **approximately constant within one OFDM symbol**, validating the slow-fading assumption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB ↔ Linear** — most numerical errors in path loss / SNR happen here. Always state both.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_d$ formulas** — remember $\cos\theta$ factor when angle of arrival is non-zero.
> 3. **Omitting units** — KTU strict marking awards 0.5 marks for missing units in final numerical answer.
> 4. **Confusing CAN Classic with CAN FD** — examiners explicitly test the **5 Mbps vs 1 Mbps** distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** — even when the math is correct, concluding with a 1-line "so this satisfies the requirement" earns the final 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the standard** — always pair DSRC with IEEE 802.11p, CAN with ISO 11898, Ethernet with IEEE 802.3bw/bp, etc.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorise Before Exam**

### A. Definitions (2-Mark Ready)
- **ITS** — Integrated application of ICT to transportation.
- **V2X** — Vehicle-to-Everything (umbrella term).
- **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G** — sub-categories of V2X.
- **CAM** — Periodic cooperative awareness (ETSI EN 302 637-2).
- **DENM** — Event-driven environmental notification (ETSI EN 302 637-3).
- **DSRC** — IEEE 802.11p + SAE J2945.
- **C-V2X** — 3GPP LTE-V2X (Rel-14) and 5G NR-V2X (Rel-16).
- **IVN** — In-Vehicle Network (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet).
- **ASIL** — Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ISO 26262).
- **RSU** — Roadside Unit (fixed infrastructure node).

### B. Numerical Must-Knows
- Free-space path loss formula: $PL_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$.
- Log-distance model: $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$.
- Shannon capacity: $C = B\log_2(1 + SNR)$.
- Doppler shift: $f_d = v f_c \cos\theta / c$.
- Coherence time: $T_{coh} = 1 / (2 f_d)$.

### C. Standard Memorisation Table

| Standard | Scope |
|---|---|
| IEEE 802.11p | DSRC PHY/MAC |
| SAE J2945 | DSRC message sets |
| 3GPP Rel-14 | LTE-V2X (C-V2X PC5) |
| 3GPP Rel-16 | 5G NR-V2X |
| ETSI EN 302 637-2 | CAM |
| ETSI EN 302 637-3 | DENM |
| ISO 11898 | CAN / CAN FD |
| ISO 17987 | LIN |
| ISO 17458 | FlexRay |
| IEEE 802.3bw | 100BASE-T1 Ethernet |
| IEEE 802.3bp | 1000BASE-T1 Ethernet |
| ISO 26262 | Functional Safety |
| SAE J3061 | Cybersecurity |

### D. Frequency Bands & Data Rates Snapshot
- **5.85–5.925 GHz** — global ITS spectrum allocation.
- **DSRC**: 3–27 Mbps.
- **5G NR-V2X URLLC**: up to 1 Gbps, < 5 ms latency.
- **CAN FD**: 5 Mbps.
- **Automotive Ethernet**: 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps.

### E. Quick Comparison Anchors
- **DSRC vs C-V2X**: DSRC is Wi-Fi-derived, decentralized; C-V2X is cellular-derived, integrates with mobile networks.
- **CAN vs Ethernet**: CAN uses CSMA/CR arbitration; Ethernet is full-duplex switched.
- **LIN vs CAN**: LIN is single-master, low-cost, low-speed; CAN is multi-master, higher speed.
- **CAM vs DENM**: CAM is periodic beacon; DENM is event-triggered alert.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle-to-Vehicle and Road-Vehicle Communication Systems - Basic Principle and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Vehicle and Road-Vehicle Communication Systems - Basic Principle and Working # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication** is a suite of wireless communication technologies that enable the exchange of real-time kinematic, environmental, and infrastructural data between a vehicle and its surrounding entities. Within this domain, two principal sub-systems are defined by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus: 1. **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication** — A short-range, low-latency, peer-to-peer wireless ad-hoc network in which On-Board Units (OBUs) installed in individual road vehicles directly broadcast and receive Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAMs) and Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages (DENMs) without the need for fixed infrastructure. 2. **Road-Vehicle Communication (R2V / V2I)** — A structured communication link between a vehicle's OBU and stationary Roadside Units (RSUs) deployed along the transportation infrastructure, enabling bidirectional exchange of traffic, signal phase, tolling, and hazard data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The operating band allocated globally for Intelligent Transport System (ITS) safety communications is the **5.9 GHz Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) band (5.85 – 5.925 GHz)**, with channel bandwidth of **10 MHz** or **20 MHz**. The alternate cellular-based protocol is **C-V2X (3GPP Release 14 onwards)** operating in the **5.9 GHz** and **PC5 sidelink** spectrum. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Cockpit Chatter" Imagine a group of cyclists riding blindfolded in a dense forest. If each cyclist had a small radio and continuously announced three things — *"My position, my speed, and my intended next turn"* — every other cyclist could draw a mental map of everyone else's trajectory, even if they couldn't see them. The forest becomes predictable and survivable. This is exactly what V2V does. Each car **broadcasts its kinematic state (position, speed, heading, acceleration, brake status) 10 times per second** at a data rate of **6–27 Mbps**. Every receiving car within a radius of roughly **300 m** builds a cooperative awareness map. When you add a roadside traffic signal or a smart lamppost to this network (R2V), it behaves like a "wise traffic warden" who whispers queue lengths, signal countdown, and accident warnings into every passing car's dashboard. ## 1.3 Key Standardized Terminology (KTU Expected Vocabulary) | Term | Expansion | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | **OBU** | On-Board Unit | Transceiver + processor mounted in vehicle | | **RSU** | Roadside Unit | Fixed infrastructure transceiver along road | | **CAM** | Cooperative Awareness Message | Periodic heartbeat of vehicle state | | **DENM** | Decentralized Environmental Notification Message | Event-triggered hazard alert | | **WAVE** | Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments | IEEE 802.11p protocol stack | | **C-V2X** | Cellular V2X | 3GPP LTE/5G sidelink based | | **BSM** | Basic Safety Message | SAE J2735 US-equivalent of CAM | | **HMI** | Human-Machine Interface | Driver warning display | > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Highlight:** V2V is fundamentally a *broadcast* paradigm, not a request-response one. There is no "ACK" from receivers — this is a deliberate design choice because the message becomes obsolete within 100 ms. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 System Architecture — Layered OSI Decomposition The V2V / R2V stack is modeled on a slimmed-down 4-layer architecture (lighter than full TCP/IP because of latency constraints): **Layer 1 — Physical (PHY) Layer** - IEEE 802.11p (DSRC) or 3GPP C-V2X sidelink (PC5 interface) - Operating frequency: **5.9 GHz** (5.85–5.925 GHz) - OFDM with 52 sub-carriers, channel bandwidth **10 MHz** - Modulation: BPSK (3 Mbps) up to 64-QAM (27 Mbps) - Transmission power: **0 dBm to 23 dBm (typical 20 dBm ≈ 100 mW)** - Symbol duration: **8 µs** (4× longer than Wi-Fi to combat Doppler at high speed) **Layer 2 — MAC (Medium Access Control)** - CSMA/CA with Enhanced Distributed Channel Access (EDCA) - Four Access Categories (ACs) prioritized for safety messages - Arbitration Inter-Frame Spacing (AIFS) — safety AC has the *shortest* wait - Channel access without requiring association with a base station (BSS-less) **Layer 3 — Network & Transport** - WSMP (WAVE Short Message Protocol) — single-hop, IPv6-optional - UDP/TCP used only for non-safety infotainment traffic - Geo-networking (ETSI EN 302 636) for multi-hop dissemination **Layer 4 — Facilities (Application Support)** - CA Service (CAM/DENM generation) - Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC) — limits channel load to < 4 % CBR (Channel Busy Ratio) - Local Dynamic Map (LDM) — geographic database fusing received messages **Layer 5 — Applications (Safety / Non-Safety)** - Forward Collision Warning, Emergency Electronic Brake Light, Intersection Collision Warning, Blind Spot Warning, Platooning, Tolling, Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT). ## 2.2 Why "Why and How" — Operational Logic Per Step - **Why OFDM with extended symbol duration?** At 120 km/h, the Doppler shift is $f_d \approx 833\text{ Hz}$. A longer symbol (8 µs vs. 4 µs in Wi-Fi) keeps inter-carrier interference tolerable. - **Why broadcast not unicast?** Latency budget for collision warning is **≤ 100 ms**; TCP handshake (RTT ~ 50–200 ms) would consume the entire budget. - **Why 5.9 GHz?** It sits in the unlicensed safety band, offers line-of-sight propagation suited to highways, and supports 10 MHz channels that fit vehicular mobility. - **Why EDCA prioritization?** Safety messages (e.g., hard-braking) must preempt traffic updates from the same node. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All quantities use SI units unless otherwise noted. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value. Use $\vert\cdot\vert$ or `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Boundary / Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | Valid for d > far-field distance | dB | | 2 | Two-ray ground reflection path loss | $PL_{dB} = 40\log_{10}(d) - 10\log_{10}(G_t G_r h_t^2 h_r^2)$ | d > crossover distance | dB | | 3 | Doppler shift (vehicular) | $f_d = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $v \le 70$ m/s, $f_c = 5.9$ GHz | Hz | | 4 | Maximum Doppler | $f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{v_{\max} \cdot f_c}{c}$ | At $\theta = 0$ | Hz | | 5 | Coherence time | $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_{d,\max}}$ | Jakes' model | s | | 6 | SNR (received) | $SNR_{dB} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - PL_{dB} - N_{0,dBm}$ | dB | dB | | 7 | Thermal noise floor (10 MHz) | $N_0 = kTB = -174 + 10\log_{10}(T) + 10\log_{10}(B)$ | $T=290$ K, $B=10^{7}$ Hz | dBm | | 8 | End-to-end latency budget | $t_{total} = t_{sens} + t_{proc} + t_{queue} + t_{prop} + t_{tx}$ | Safety budget $\le 100$ ms | ms | | 9 | Propagation delay | $t_{prop} = d/c$ | $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s | s | | 10 | Data rate (Shannon capacity) | $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ | Upper bound | bps | | 11 | CAM broadcast frequency | $f_{CAM} = 1\text{–}10$ Hz | ETSI EN 302 637-2 | Hz | | 12 | IEEE 802.11p max data rate | 27 Mbps (64-QAM, ½ rate) | With 20 MHz channel | Mbps | | 13 | Range target (DSRC) | 300 – 1000 m | Highway line-of-sight | m | | 14 | Packet size (CAM) | 100 – 800 bytes | Variable payload | bytes | | 15 | Channel load (CBR limit) | $CBR \le 0.4$ | DCC regulated | dimensionless | | 16 | Achievable throughput at v | $\eta \approx B \log_2(1 + SINR) \cdot (1 - PER)$ | Depends on PER | bps | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Active Safety (ADAS augmentation):** Forward Collision Warning, Do-Not-Pass Warning, Intersection Movement Assist. - **Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (CACC):** String stability in platoons via low-latency V2V. - **Smart Cities:** R2V enables adaptive signal timing, eco-routing, and emergency vehicle pre-emption. - **Insurance & Telematics:** Risk profiling based on inter-vehicle proximity logs. - **Autonomous Driving Stack (L3–L5):** V2X complements on-board sensors (cameras, LiDAR, radar) by overcoming line-of-sight, weather, and occlusion limitations. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss for V2V Link **Problem statement:** Two vehicles separated by $d = 250$ m operate in the DSRC band at $f_c = 5.9$ GHz. Using the Friis free-space path loss equation, find the path loss in dB. Assume isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$). **Step 1 — Identify the Friis free-space path loss equation (carrier-frequency form):** $$ PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the operating frequency in MHz:** $$ f_{MHz} = 5.9 \times 10^3 = 5900 \text{ MHz} $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.7709 = 75.418 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the distance in km:** $$ d_{km} = 250 \times 10^{-3} = 0.25 \text{ km} $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(0.25) = 20 \times (-0.6021) = -12.041 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4 — Sum all three terms:** $$ PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 75.418 - 12.041 $$ $$ PL_{dB} = 95.82 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The path loss at 250 m in 5.9 GHz free space is roughly **95.8 dB**. To maintain a link, the received power $P_r$ at the antenna terminals must therefore satisfy: $$ P_{r,dBm} \ge P_{t,dBm} + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB} = 20 + 0 + 0 - 95.82 = -75.82 \text{ dBm} $$ Since the typical DSRC receiver sensitivity is around **-82 dBm @ 6 Mbps (BPSK)**, the 250 m link is feasible with $\approx 6$ dB fade margin. This margin accommodates shadowing, multipath, and Doppler. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Doppler Shift at Highway Speed **Problem statement:** A car moving at $v = 144$ km/h receives a 5.9 GHz signal from a stationary RSU directly in front. Find the maximum Doppler shift and the resulting channel coherence time. **Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units:** $$ v = 144 \, \dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = \dfrac{144 \times 1000}{3600} = 40 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum Doppler formula (cos θ = 1 for head-on approach):** $$ f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$ f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{40 \times 5.9 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} $$ $$ f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{2.36 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 786.7 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 4 — Coherence time (Jakes' 0.423 constant):** $$ T_c = \dfrac{0.423}{f_{d,\max}} = \dfrac{0.423}{786.7} $$ $$ T_c \approx 5.38 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 538 \, \mu\text{s} $$ **Step 5 — Design implication:** A CAM packet containing 100 bytes at 6 Mbps occupies a transmission time of: $$ t_{tx} = \dfrac{100 \times 8}{6 \times 10^6} \approx 133 \, \mu\text{s} $$ Since $t_{tx} \ll T_c$ (533 µs), the channel remains approximately constant *within* a single packet — but inter-packet channel variation is significant. This is the physical justification for the IEEE 802.11p extended symbol time of 8 µs. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Noise Floor and Link Budget **Problem statement:** For a 10 MHz DSRC channel at 290 K ambient, compute the thermal noise floor and the SNR given a received power of $P_r = -75$ dBm. **Step 1 — Johnson–Nyquist thermal noise:** $$ N_0 \, (\text{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}(T) + 10\log_{10}(B) $$ **Step 2 — Insert values T = 290 K, B = 10 MHz:** $$ 10\log_{10}(290) = 24.62 \text{ dBK} $$ $$ 10\log_{10}(10^{7}) = 70 \text{ dB-Hz} $$ $$ N_0 = -174 + 24.62 + 70 = -79.38 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 3 — SNR calculation:** $$ SNR_{dB} = P_{r,dBm} - N_{0,dBm} = -75 - (-79.38) $$ $$ SNR_{dB} = 4.38 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4 — Shannon capacity (upper bound):** $$ C = B \log_2(1 + SNR) = 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(1 + 10^{4.38/10}) $$ $$ C = 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(1 + 2.74) = 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(3.74) $$ $$ C \approx 10^{7} \cdot 1.903 = 19.03 \text{ Mbps} $$ The achievable practical throughput is ≈ 60–70 % of this bound once coding, pilot overhead, and PER are subtracted. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of V2V vs. R2V | Parameter | V2V | R2V (V2I) | |---|---|---| | Topology | Ad-hoc, decentralized | Infrastructure-based, star or mesh | | Communication | Direct OBU-to-OBU | OBU ↔ RSU ↔ backhaul | | Latency target | ≤ 20 ms (pre-crash sensing) | ≤ 100 ms (signal timing, tolling) | | Coverage | Limited to OBU density | RSU footprint dependent | | Use cases | Collision warning, platooning | SPaT, tolling, HD-map updates | | Standard | ETSI EN 302 637 / IEEE 1609 | ETSI EN 302 893 / IEEE 1609.4 | | Dependency | Other vehicles' OBU presence | Requires deployed RSUs | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: End-to-End V2V / R2V Architecture ```mermaid graph TD sensorA[Vehicle Sensors GPS IMU CAN Bus] --> obuA[OBU A On Board Unit] cameraA[Camera Radar Lidar] --> obuA obuA --> camA[CAM Generator 1 to 10 Hz] obuA --> denmA[DENM Generator Event Triggered] camA --> macA[MAC 802 11p EDCA Priority] denmA --> macA macA --> phyA[PHY OFDM 5.9 GHz 10 MHz] phyA --> antennaA[Antenna 5.9 GHz Omni 3 dBi] antennaA <--> channel((Wireless Channel 5.9 GHz DSRC 300 to 1000 m)) antennaB[Antenna 5.9 GHz Omni 3 dBi] <--> channel antennaB --> phyB[PHY OFDM 5.9 GHz 10 MHz] phyB --> macB[MAC 802 11p EDCA Priority] macB --> demuxB[Message Demultiplexer] demuxB --> ldmB[Local Dynamic Map LDM] ldmB --> appB[Safety Apps FCW EEBL BSW] appB --> hmiB[HMI Driver Warning Display] rsuA[RSU 1 Roadside Unit Traffic Light Pole] --> rsuApp[SPaT MAP Signal Phase and Timing] rsuApp --> rsuMac[RSU MAC Scheduler] rsuMac --> rsuPhy[RSU PHY 5.9 GHz] rsuPhy --> rsuAnt[RSU Antenna Sector] rsuAnt <--> channel rsuApp --> backhaul[Backhaul Fiber LTE 5G] backhaul --> tmc[Traffic Management Center Cloud] tmc --> centerApp[Analytics HD Map Incident Mgmt] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: V2V Emergency Electronic Brake Light (EEBL) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CarX as Vehicle X Front Car participant CarY as Vehicle Y Following Car participant CarZ as Vehicle Z Adjacent Lane CarX->>CarX: Sensor detects hard brake deceleration > 0.4 g CarX->>CarX: Generate DENM Trigger event driven CarX-->>CarY: Broadcast DENM CAM with brake status CarX-->>CarZ: Broadcast DENM CAM with brake status CarY->>CarY: LDM updates hazard geofence CarY->>CarY: HMI alerts driver auditory and haptic CarZ->>CarZ: LDM marks zone, no driver alert rear Note over CarY,CarZ: End to end latency budget 100 ms
Propagation 1 us, MAC contention 2 to 5 ms
Tx 0.13 ms, Processing 5 ms ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Frequency-Channel Allocation Chart (DSRC Band, US FCC Plan) ```mermaid graph LR ch172[CH 172 SCH 5.855 to 5.865 GHz 10 MHz Critical Safety of Life] ch174[CH 174 SCH 5.865 to 5.875 GHz 10 MHz Intersection Safety] ch176[CH 176 SCH 5.875 to 5.885 GHz 10 MHz Platooning] ch178[CH 178 SCH 5.885 to 5.895 GHz 10 MHz Advanced Safety] ch180[CH 180 SCH 5.895 to 5.905 GHz 10 MHz Control and Probe] ch182[CH 182 SCH 5.905 to 5.915 GHz 10 MHz Service Announcement] ch184[CH 184 SCH 5.915 to 5.925 GHz 10 MHz Public Safety] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architecture Insight:** The "BSS-less" property of 802.11p is what makes V2V possible. Traditional Wi-Fi needs an AP to associate with first; in vehicular scenarios, no AP exists between moving cars, so the standard allows immediate broadcast on the SCH (Service Channel) without a BSSID handshake. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT861]** **Define Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication. List any two message types standardized by ETSI for V2V safety applications and state the typical frequency band of operation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication is a short-range wireless ad-hoc network in which on-board units (OBUs) of moving vehicles directly exchange kinematic and safety data (position, speed, heading, brake status) peer-to-peer without a fixed infrastructure or base station. 2. **Standardized Message Types (1 mark each, any two):** - **CAM — Cooperative Awareness Message** (ETSI EN 302 637-2): periodic heartbeat (1–10 Hz). - **DENM — Decentralized Environmental Notification Message** (ETSI EN 302 637-3): event-triggered hazard alert. 3. **Operating band (1 mark):** Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) band — **5.85 – 5.925 GHz (centre 5.9 GHz)**, channel bandwidth 10 MHz. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series I]** **Differentiate between V2V and R2V (V2I) communication in terms of topology, latency, and typical use cases.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | V2V | R2V (V2I) | |---|---|---| | **Topology (1 mark)** | Decentralized ad-hoc, OBU-to-OBU | Infrastructure-based, OBU ↔ RSU | | **Latency (1 mark)** | ≤ 20 ms (pre-crash) | ≤ 100 ms (SPaT, tolling) | | **Use Cases (1 mark)** | Forward Collision Warning, Platooning, EEBL | Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT), Tolling, HD-map updates | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set A]` **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the basic principle and working of Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication. With the aid of a block diagram, explain the layered architecture (PHY / MAC / Facilities / Application) of the IEEE 802.11p (WAVE) protocol stack used for V2V. **(b) [7 marks)** For a V2V link operating at 5.9 GHz with vehicles separated by 200 m and isotropic antennas, compute the free-space path loss and the required received power to maintain a 6 Mbps BPSK link. Assume transmit power = 20 dBm and receiver sensitivity = −82 dBm. Comment on the link margin. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — Principle & Working (7 marks)** 1. **Basic Principle (2 marks):** V2V works on the *broadcast-cooperative-awareness* paradigm. Each vehicle, equipped with an OBU, broadcasts its kinematic state (position via GPS, speed, heading, acceleration, brake status) as a CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message) at 1–10 Hz on the 5.9 GHz DSRC channel. Surrounding vehicles within range (≈ 300 m line of sight) listen to these messages, decode them, and fuse them into a Local Dynamic Map (LDM). When the LDM detects a hazardous scenario (e.g., hard brake ahead, intersection conflict), the HMI warns the driver or triggers ADAS actuation within 100 ms. Event-triggered hazards use DENMs. 2. **Layered Architecture (5 marks — one mark per layer + one for overall):** - **PHY (IEEE 802.11p, 1 mark):** OFDM with 52 sub-carriers, 10 MHz channel, 8 µs symbol, BPSK → 64-QAM (3–27 Mbps), 5.85–5.925 GHz. - **MAC (IEEE 802.11p EDCA, 1 mark):** CSMA/CA with 4 prioritized Access Categories; safety AC has shortest AIFS to preempt. - **Network (WAVE / WSMP, 1 mark):** Single-hop IPv6-optional WSMP — eliminates handshake latency. - **Facilities (1 mark):** CA service, DENM generator, DCC (Decentralized Congestion Control) capping CBR ≤ 0.4, LDM. - **Applications (1 mark):** FCW, EEBL, BSW, IMA, Platooning. 3. **Block Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above (transcribe into hand-drawn equivalent in the answer book). --- **Part (b) — Numerical Link Budget (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Convert distance and frequency (1 mark):** $d = 200 \text{ m} = 0.2 \text{ km}$, $f = 5900 \text{ MHz}$. **Step 2 — Apply Friis equation (2 marks):** $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 20\log_{10}(0.2)$ $20\log_{10}(5900) = 75.42 \text{ dB}$ $20\log_{10}(0.2) = -13.98 \text{ dB}$ $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 75.42 - 13.98 = 93.88 \text{ dB}$ **Step 3 — Compute received power (1 mark):** $P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB} = 20 + 0 + 0 - 93.88 = -73.88 \text{ dBm}$ **Step 4 — Compare to sensitivity (1 mark):** Required sensitivity for 6 Mbps BPSK = −82 dBm. Received power = −73.88 dBm, which is 8.12 dB above the floor. **Step 5 — Link margin (1 mark):** Link margin = −73.88 − (−82) = **8.12 dB**. This margin accommodates shadow fading, multipath, and Doppler — sufficient for a robust 200 m highway V2V link. **Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):** The 8.12 dB margin means the link can tolerate up to ≈ 8 dB of additional loss. Doubling the distance would cost 6 dB, leaving only 2 dB margin. The practical range ceiling is therefore ≈ 300 m in this configuration. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set B]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a block diagram the architecture of Road-Vehicle Communication (R2V / V2I). Describe the role of Roadside Units (RSU), the SPaT/MAP message types, and how adaptive signal control benefits from R2V. **(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle traveling at 120 km/h approaches a stationary RSU. Calculate: (i) the maximum Doppler shift at carrier 5.9 GHz, (ii) the channel coherence time, and (iii) the total end-to-end latency budget assuming sensing = 10 ms, processing = 5 ms, queueing = 3 ms, propagation = 1 µs, and transmission of a 400-byte packet at 6 Mbps. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — R2V Architecture (7 marks)** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** R2V (Road-to-Vehicle), also called V2I, is a structured communication between a vehicle's OBU and a fixed Roadside Unit (RSU) deployed on traffic signals, lampposts, or gantries. It enables bi-directional data exchange for signal timing, traffic flow, and incident management. 2. **RSU role (1 mark):** Acts as a relay between OBUs and the Traffic Management Center (TMC) over fibre/LTE/5G backhaul. Performs message scheduling, authentication, and local caching. 3. **SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing, 1 mark):** RSU broadcasts the current signal state (red/yellow/green) and time-to-next-phase to approaching OBUs. 4. **MAP (1 mark):** Geographic intersection description (lane geometry, stop lines, approach angles) so OBUs can correlate SPaT to their lane. 5. **Adaptive signal control benefit (2 marks):** Vehicles report queue lengths, turning intentions, and approaching speeds. Signal controllers dynamically re-time phases (e.g., extend green for emergency vehicles detected via V2V/V2I) — improving throughput by 10–20 % and reducing stops. 6. **Block diagram:** Recreate the Mermaid graph from Section 4.1 (RSU portion) by hand. **Part (b) — Doppler, Coherence & Latency (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Speed conversion (0.5 mark):** $v = 120 \text{ km/h} = 120/3.6 = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$ **Step 2 — Maximum Doppler shift (1.5 marks):** $f_{d,\max} = (v \cdot f_c)/c = (33.33 \times 5.9 \times 10^9)/(3 \times 10^8) = 655.6 \text{ Hz}$ **Step 3 — Coherence time (1 mark):** $T_c = 0.423/f_{d,\max} = 0.423/655.6 = 6.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 645 \, \mu\text{s}$ **Step 4 — Transmission time of 400 bytes @ 6 Mbps (1 mark):** $t_{tx} = (400 \times 8)/(6 \times 10^6) = 3200/6 \times 10^6 = 533 \, \mu\text{s}$ **Step 5 — Total latency (2 marks):** $t_{total} = t_{sens} + t_{proc} + t_{queue} + t_{prop} + t_{tx}$ $t_{total} = 10 + 5 + 3 + 0.001 + 0.533 = 18.534 \text{ ms}$ **Step 6 — Validation (1 mark):** Total latency = 18.53 ms, comfortably below the 100 ms safety budget. Doppler coherence time of 645 µs exceeds packet time of 533 µs, ensuring the channel is approximately constant over a packet. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Patterns to Avoid:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Always convert km/h → m/s and MHz → Hz before plugging into Doppler / path-loss formulas. Mixing units is a 1–2 mark loss. > 2. **Writing "5G" instead of "5.9 GHz":** Students often confuse the cellular 5G term with the operating band — KTU examiners specifically expect **5.9 GHz**. > 3. **Confusing CAM with DENM:** CAM is *periodic* (heartbeat); DENM is *event-triggered* (brake, crash). This distinction is a frequent 1-mark question. > 4. **Confusing dBm with dBW:** dBm uses 1 mW reference; dBW uses 1 W. The standard in link budget is dBm. Off-by-30-dB errors are common. > 5. **Skipping the block diagram:** For 7-mark questions on architecture, a missing block diagram costs 1–2 marks. Always draw one. > 6. **Ignoring the 100 ms safety latency budget:** Any latency question must compare the computed value to this benchmark. > 7. **Using Shannon capacity as achievable throughput:** Shannon is an upper bound; real throughput is 50–70 % of Shannon. > 8. **Writing "TCP" for safety messages:** Use **WSMP** (WAVE Short Message Protocol) — TCP adds unacceptable handshake latency. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **V2V = peer-to-peer broadcast; R2V = OBU ↔ RSU infrastructure link.** - **Operating band:** 5.9 GHz DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) or C-V2X (3GPP PC5 sidelink). - **Channel bandwidth:** 10 MHz; **Range:** 300 m (highway LOS); **Latency budget:** ≤ 100 ms. - **Message types:** CAM (periodic, 1–10 Hz) and DENM (event-triggered). - **Stack name:** WAVE = IEEE 1609.x (upper layers) + IEEE 802.11p (PHY/MAC). - **Key formulas to memorize:** - Friis path loss: $PL = 32.44 + 20\log f + 20\log d$ - Doppler: $f_d = v f_c / c$ - Coherence time: $T_c = 0.423 / f_{d,\max}$ - Latency sum: $t_{total} = t_{sens} + t_{proc} + t_{queue} + t_{prop} + t_{tx}$ - **Design rationale:** 8 µs OFDM symbol for Doppler robustness, EDCA priority for safety, BSS-less broadcast for zero-handshake latency. - **Standards to know:** ETSI EN 302 637-2/3, IEEE 802.11p, IEEE 1609, SAE J2735, 3GPP Release 14/15. - **Key applications:** FCW, EEBL, BSW, IMA, CACC Platooning, SPaT, Tolling, Eco-Routing. - **DCC rule:** Channel Busy Ratio (CBR) capped at 0.4 to prevent congestion. - **Receiver sensitivity @ 6 Mbps BPSK:** ≈ −82 dBm. - **Typical transmit power:** 20 dBm (100 mW). - **Symbol period:** 8 µs (4× Wi-Fi's 4 µs). - **Doppler at 144 km/h, 5.9 GHz:** ≈ 787 Hz. - **Coherence time at 787 Hz:** ≈ 538 µs.

topics.title: Relative Merits and Demerits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relative Merits and Demerits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Navigation and Control**, the topic **"Relative Merits and Demerits"** refers to a systematic comparative analytical framework used to evaluate the performance, accuracy, reliability, cost-effectiveness, and operational suitability of various navigation sensors, positioning systems, control algorithms, and path-planning techniques employed in modern intelligent vehicles.

The comparison is generally conducted across the following engineering dimensions:
- **Accuracy** (positional and control)
- **Update Rate / Bandwidth**
- **Robustness** (against environmental disturbances)
- **Cost** (Capital Expenditure - CapEx)
- **Computational Complexity**
- **Scalability and Integration Feasibility**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, students are expected to critically compare:
> 1. **Positioning Systems** — GNSS (GPS/GLONASS/Galileo/BeiDou), INS, Dead Reckoning (DR), and Map Matching.
> 2. **Perception Sensors** — LiDAR, RADAR, Camera (Mono/Stereo), Ultrasonic, and Inertial Measurement Units (IMU).
> 3. **Control Strategies** — Classical PID, State-Space (LQR), Model Predictive Control (MPC), Fuzzy Logic, and Neural Network–based controllers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **blindfolded person trying to navigate a busy city** to reach a specific café. You have several tools available:

- A **smartphone with GPS** → tells you *where you are* on the map (but may fail indoors or in tunnels).
- A **pedometer + compass** → counts your steps and direction (works indoors but accumulates error).
- **Asking a local** → gives you real-time corrections but is unreliable.
- **Your own memory of turns** → works in familiar areas only.

Now imagine you have *one* of these tools — each has clear **merits (good points)** and **demerits (bad points)**. The "Relative Merits and Demerits" study is essentially this same comparison applied to the electronic "senses" and "brains" of an autonomous vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single navigation or control system is universally superior. Automotive engineers always use **Sensor Fusion** — combining the strengths of multiple systems to mask individual weaknesses.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (used for comparison)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Positional Accuracy | $\sigma_p$ | meters (m) |
| Velocity Accuracy | $\sigma_v$ | m/s |
| Update Rate | $f_s$ | Hz |
| Latency | $\tau$ | ms |
| Power Consumption | $P$ | Watts (W) |
| Cost | $C$ | USD |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | hours |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Comparative Analysis of Positioning Systems

### A. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS / GPS)

**Operating Principle:** Triangulation using Time-of-Flight (ToF) signals from at least 4 satellites.

**Merits:**
- **Global Coverage** (worldwide positioning availability).
- High long-term accuracy (2–5 m with SBAS correction).
- Passive receiver (no transmission required).

**Demerits:**
- **Line-of-Sight (LoS) dependency** — fails in tunnels, urban canyons, indoors.
- Susceptible to **multipath errors** in dense cities.
- Low update rate (~1–10 Hz).
- Vulnerable to **jamming and spoofing**.

---

### B. Inertial Navigation System (INS)

**Operating Principle:** Dead reckoning using Newton's equations of motion, integrating accelerometer and gyroscope outputs.

**Merits:**
- **Fully Autonomous** — no external signal required.
- Very high short-term accuracy.
- High bandwidth (typically **100–1000 Hz**).
- Immune to jamming/spoofing.

**Demerits:**
- **Error grows unbounded with time** (drift).
- Requires periodic correction (often from GNSS).
- Higher cost for tactical/strategic grade IMUs.

---

### C. Dead Reckoning (DR) with Wheel Odometry + Compass

**Merits:**
- Low cost, simple implementation.
- Works in GNSS-denied environments.

**Demerits:**
- Cumulative errors due to wheel slip, odometer scaling errors, and magnetic disturbances.
- Compass errors near ferrous structures.

---

### D. Map Matching (MM)

**Merits:**
- Corrects GNSS/INS errors by snapping to road geometry.
- Enhances perceived accuracy for route guidance.

**Demerits:**
- Requires accurate digital maps.
- Computational overhead in dense road networks.

---

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis of Perception Sensors

| Sensor | Range | Accuracy | Cost | Weather Robustness | Key De-Merit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LiDAR** | 100–250 m | ±2 cm | Very High | Poor in fog/rain | Expensive, mechanical wear |
| **RADAR** | 250 m | ±0.1 m | Medium | Excellent | Low angular resolution |
| **Camera (Mono/Stereo)** | 50–250 m | Varies | Low | Poor in low light | Sensitive to illumination |
| **Ultrasonic** | 0.1–5 m | ±1 cm | Very Low | Good | Very short range only |
| **IMU** | N/A | Drift-based | Varies | Excellent | Drift accumulation |

---

## 2.3 Comparative Analysis of Vehicle Control Strategies

### A. PID Controller (Classical)

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Merits:** Simple, robust for linear SISO systems, low computational load.
**Demerits:** Poor handling of multivariable coupling, constraints, and nonlinear vehicle dynamics (tire slip, saturation).

---

### B. Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) – State-Space

$$J = \int_0^{\infty} \left( x^T Q x + u^T R u \right) dt$$

**Merits:** Optimal for linear systems, multivariable capable, systematic tuning via Q and R weights.
**Demerits:** Requires accurate linear model; cannot handle constraints natively.

---

### C. Model Predictive Control (MPC)

$$\min_{u} \sum_{k=0}^{N_p - 1} \left( \Vert x_k - x_{ref}\Vert_Q^2 + \Vert u_k \Vert_R^2 \right)$$

**Merits:** Explicitly handles constraints, predicts future behavior, handles MIMO systems.
**Demerits:** Very high computational cost; requires good prediction model.

---

### D. Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)

**Merits:** Handles linguistic/heuristic rules, no model required, robust to uncertainty.
**Demerits:** Tuning is empirical; no formal stability guarantee.

---

### E. Neural Network–Based Controller

**Merits:** Models highly nonlinear dynamics, learns from data.
**Demerits:** "Black box" behavior, requires large training datasets, safety certification challenges.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_{INS}(t) = \sigma_0 + k \cdot t$ | INS position drift over time | m |
| $\sigma_{GNSS} = \sqrt{\sigma_{iono}^2 + \sigma_{tropo}^2 + \sigma_{mp}^2 + \sigma_{clk}^2}$ | GNSS total error budget | m |
| $DOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$ | Dilution of Precision | dimensionless |
| $J_{LQR} = \int_0^{\infty} (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt$ | LQR cost | varies |
| $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Steady-state error (PID) | varies |
| $\Delta f_{doppler} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | RADAR Doppler shift | Hz |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quantitative Comparison: INS Drift Derivation

The positional error of an Inertial Navigation System grows as a function of time. The standard error propagation model is:

$$\sigma_{INS}(t) = \sigma_0 + v_{bias} \cdot t + \frac{1}{2} a_{bias} \cdot t^2$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Step 1: A position in 1D is given by the double integral of acceleration:

$$p(t) = p_0 + v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} \int_0^t \int_0^{\tau} a(s)\, ds\, d\tau$$

Step 2: If the accelerometer has a constant bias $a_{bias}$, the measured acceleration becomes:

$$a_{measured} = a_{true} + a_{bias}$$

Step 3: Substituting the bias into the position equation:

$$p_{measured}(t) = p_0 + v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} (a_{true} + a_{bias}) t^2$$

Step 4: The positional error $\Delta p(t) = p_{measured} - p_{true}$ is therefore:

$$\Delta p(t) = \frac{1}{2} a_{bias} \cdot t^2$$

Step 5: This is the **fundamental demerit of INS** — the error grows quadratically with time, which is why INS must be periodically corrected by an external reference (typically GNSS).

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **high-weightage KTU 2024 expected question** for both 7-mark and 14-mark derivations.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Composite Score)

The table below summarizes a typical *weighted scoring* method used by automotive engineers to compare navigation suites:

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | GNSS Score (0–10) | INS Score (0–10) | DR Score (0–10) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| Robustness | 0.25 | 4 | 9 | 6 |
| Update Rate | 0.15 | 3 | 10 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 8 | 4 | 9 |
| Scalability | 0.10 | 9 | 6 | 7 |
| **Weighted Total** | **1.00** | **6.30** | **6.70** | **6.55** |

**Score formula:**

$$S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of criterion $i$ and $s_i$ is the score. The system with the highest $S_{total}$ is the most suitable — but in practice, **Sensor Fusion** (e.g., GNSS + INS + DR) achieves the best of all worlds.

---

## 3.3 Why Sensor Fusion is the Engineering Solution

Let the measured state from GNSS be $\hat{x}_{GNSS}$ and from INS be $\hat{x}_{INS}$. A simple **Kalman Filter** fusion gives the optimal estimate:

$$\hat{x}_{fused} = \hat{x}_{INS} + K (\hat{x}_{GNSS} - \hat{x}_{INS})$$

where $K$ is the **Kalman Gain**, computed as:

$$K = P_{INS} (P_{INS} + P_{GNSS})^{-1}$$

Step 1: $P_{INS}$ is the covariance of INS error (large, growing with time).
Step 2: $P_{GNSS}$ is the covariance of GNSS error (bounded).
Step 3: When $P_{INS} \gg P_{GNSS}$, then $K \to 1$ (trust GNSS more).
Step 4: When $P_{INS} \ll P_{GNSS}$, then $K \to 0$ (trust INS more — e.g., during GNSS outage).

**This elegantly solves the relative merits/demerits trade-off.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Navigation System Selection Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vehicle Navigation Need] --> B{Environment Type}
    B -- Open Highway --> C[Primary: GNSS]
    B -- Urban Canyon --> D[Primary: INS plus Map Matching]
    B -- Tunnel or Indoor --> E[Primary: Dead Reckoning plus IMU]
    C --> F{Sensor Fusion Layer}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Kalman Filter Estimator]
    G --> H[Output: Fused State Estimate]
    H --> I[Path Planning Module]
    I --> J[Controller PID or LQR or MPC]
    J --> K[Actuator Commands to Vehicle]
```

## 4.2 Sensor Comparison Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PER[Perception Layer]
        L1[LiDAR]
        R1[RADAR]
        C1[CAMERA]
        U1[Ultrasonic]
    end
    subgraph POS[Positioning Layer]
        G1[GNSS Receiver]
        I1[IMU]
        W1[Wheel Odometry]
    end
    subgraph FUS[Fusion Layer]
        KF[Kalman / Extended Kalman Filter]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Control Layer]
        PID[PID Controller]
        LQR[LQR Controller]
        MPC[MPC Controller]
        FLC[Fuzzy Logic Controller]
    end
    PER --> KF
    POS --> KF
    KF --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT[Vehicle Actuators]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Merits–Demerits Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Merits of Navigation/Control Systems] --> M1[High Accuracy]
    M --> M2[Robustness]
    M --> M3[Low Cost]
    M --> M4[High Bandwidth]
    D[Demerits of Navigation/Control Systems] --> D1[Drift / Cumulative Error]
    D --> D2[LoS Dependency]
    D --> D3[Computational Cost]
    D --> D4[Environmental Sensitivity]
    M1 --> SOL[Solution: Sensor Fusion]
    M2 --> SOL
    M3 --> SOL
    M4 --> SOL
    D1 --> SOL
    D2 --> SOL
    D3 --> SOL
    D4 --> SOL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three merits and three demerits of using GNSS as a primary navigation system in autonomous vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

*Merits:*
1. Provides **global, all-weather positioning** with reasonable accuracy (2–5 m with SBAS).
2. Passive reception — vehicle need not transmit any signal.
3. Long-term stability; no cumulative error like INS.

*Demerits:*
1. Requires **Line-of-Sight (LoS)** to at least 4 satellites — fails in tunnels and urban canyons.
2. Susceptible to **multipath errors** and intentional jamming/spoofing.
3. Low update rate (~1–10 Hz) — insufficient for high-speed control loops.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Compare PID and MPC controllers for autonomous vehicle lateral control, listing two merits and two demerits of each.**

**Model Answer:**

*PID Controller:*
- **Merits:** Simple structure, easy real-time implementation, low computational cost.
- **Demerits:** Cannot handle multivariable coupling; no explicit constraint handling.

*MPC Controller:*
- **Merits:** Handles actuator and state constraints natively; predicts future trajectory.
- **Demerits:** High computational burden; requires accurate prediction model; needs higher-end ECUs.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With the help of suitable equations, derive the expression for **INS positional error growth** as a function of time. Explain why this is considered the primary demerit of a standalone INS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **relative merits and demerits of LiDAR, RADAR, and Camera-based perception systems** for use in an autonomous vehicle. Use a comparison table. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) INS Drift Derivation:**

Step 1: True position from ideal accelerometer (no bias):

$$p_{true}(t) = p_0 + v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} a_{true} t^2$$

Step 2: With accelerometer bias $b_a$:

$$a_{meas} = a_{true} + b_a$$

Step 3: Position from biased measurement:

$$p_{meas}(t) = p_0 + v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} (a_{true} + b_a) t^2$$

Step 4: Positional error:

$$\Delta p(t) = p_{meas} - p_{true} = \frac{1}{2} b_a t^2$$

Step 5: This is **quadratic error growth** — the fundamental demerit. After just 60 seconds with a bias of $0.01 \, m/s^2$, the error reaches:

$$\Delta p = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.01 \times 60^2 = 18 \text{ m}$$

This is why INS must be **aided by GNSS** periodically.

---

**(b) LiDAR vs RADAR vs Camera Comparison Table:**

| Feature | LiDAR | RADAR | Camera |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 100–250 m | up to 250 m | 50–250 m |
| Accuracy | ±2 cm | ±0.1 m (range) | Pixel-level |
| Angular Res. | High (0.1°) | Low (1–2°) | Very High |
| Weather Robust | Poor in fog | Excellent | Poor in low light |
| Cost | Very High | Medium | Low |
| Output | 3D Point Cloud | Range, Velocity | 2D Image |
| Night Operation | Yes | Yes | No (needs IR) |

**Conclusion:** No single sensor is sufficient; hence **Sensor Fusion** is mandatory in modern ADAS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **relative merits and demerits of LQR, MPC, and Fuzzy Logic controllers** for autonomous vehicle longitudinal speed control. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Kalman Filter–based sensor fusion** as a method to overcome the demerits of individual navigation sensors. Include the fusion equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Controller Comparison:**

*LQR:*
- **Merit:** Optimal for linear MIMO systems; systematic tuning via Q, R matrices.
- **Demerit:** Linearization error at high speeds; no constraint handling.

*MPC:*
- **Merit:** Constraint-aware; predictive over a horizon $N_p$.
- **Demerit:** Computationally expensive; needs real-time solver.

*Fuzzy Logic:*
- **Merit:** Uses linguistic rules; no model required; robust.
- **Demerit:** No formal stability proof; tuning is empirical.

---

**(b) Kalman Filter Fusion:**

$$\hat{x}_{fused,k} = \hat{x}_{INS,k}^- + K_k (z_{GNSS,k} - H \hat{x}_{INS,k}^-)$$

Kalman Gain:

$$K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R)^{-1}$$

Where:
- $\hat{x}_{INS,k}^-$ = INS predicted state
- $z_{GNSS,k}$ = GNSS measurement
- $H$ = observation matrix
- $P_k^-$ = predicted error covariance
- $R$ = measurement noise covariance

This fusion **combines the short-term accuracy of INS** with the **long-term stability of GNSS**, effectively overcoming both their demerits.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Writing only "advantages" or only "disadvantages"** — examiners expect *both* merits and demerits with **balanced depth**.
> 2. **Skipping equations** — KTU values derivations heavily; always include the governing equation.
> 3. **Using vague terms** like "GPS is accurate" — quantify! Say "±2–5 m with SBAS correction."
> 4. **Ignoring the "Relative" in the question** — always *compare* systems, not describe them in isolation.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — every numerical comparison must carry SI units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **GNSS** = global, passive, accurate — but LoS-dependent.
- ✅ **INS** = autonomous, high bandwidth — but drifts quadratically with time.
- ✅ **Sensor Fusion (Kalman Filter)** is the standard solution to combine strengths.
- ✅ **LiDAR** offers best 3D accuracy but is expensive; **RADAR** is weather-robust; **Camera** is cheapest but light-sensitive.
- ✅ **PID** is simple but cannot handle constraints; **LQR** is optimal-linear; **MPC** is constraint-aware; **Fuzzy** is model-free.
- ✅ Always **quantify** your comparisons (accuracy in m, cost in USD, update rate in Hz).
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, **a comparison table is mandatory** for full marks.
- ✅ Derivation of **INS drift** ($\Delta p = \frac{1}{2} b_a t^2$) is a **favourite KTU question**.
- ✅ Sensor Fusion formula: $\hat{x}_{fused} = \hat{x}_{INS} + K(z_{GNSS} - \hat{x}_{INS})$
- ✅ Conclusion of every comparison: *No single system is sufficient; fusion is the engineering reality.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
